1	# Contributing to metaswarm
2	
3	Thank you for your interest in contributing to metaswarm.
4	
5	## How to Contribute
6	
7	### Reporting Issues
8	
9	Open an issue on GitHub with:
10	- What you expected to happen
11	- What actually happened
12	- Steps to reproduce
13	- Your environment (Claude Code version, BEADS version, OS)
14	
15	### Adding or Improving Agents
16	
17	Agent definitions live in `agents/`. Each agent is a Markdown file with:
18	
19	1. **Role** — What the agent specializes in
20	2. **Responsibilities** — What it does and produces
21	3. **Process** — Step-by-step workflow
22	4. **Output Format** — Expected deliverables
23	5. **Integration Points** — How it connects to other agents
24	
25	When contributing a new agent:
26	- Place it in `agents/<name>-agent.md`
27	- Add it to the agent roster in `ORCHESTRATION.md`
28	- Document it in `USAGE.md`
29	- Keep it generic (no project-specific references)
30	
31	### Adding Skills
32	
33	Skills are orchestration behaviors in `skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. A skill coordinates multiple agents or provides a reusable workflow pattern.
34	
35	### Improving Rubrics
36	
37	Rubrics in `rubrics/` define quality standards for reviews. Contributions should:
38	- Be actionable (agents can follow them)
39	- Be measurable (clear pass/fail criteria)
40	- Not be project-specific
41	
42	### Knowledge Base Templates
43	
44	The `knowledge/` directory contains schema templates. Improvements to the schema, documentation, or example entries are welcome.
45	
46	## Testing the Plugin
47	
48	After making changes, test the plugin locally:
49	
50	```bash
51	# Test in a fresh directory
52	mkdir /tmp/test-project && cd /tmp/test-project && git init
53	
54	# Install your local copy as a plugin
55	claude plugin add /path/to/metaswarm
56	
57	# In Claude Code, verify skills and commands load correctly
58	# Type / and check that start-task, setup, prime, etc. appear
59	# Run /status to verify all 9 diagnostic checks pass
60	```
61	
62	If you're also contributing external tool adapters (Codex, Gemini), run the verification script:
63	
64	```bash
65	bin/external-tools-verify.sh
66	```
67	
68	## Pull Request Process
69	
70	1. Fork the repository
71	2. Create a branch (`feat/`, `fix/`, `docs/`)
72	3. Make your changes
73	4. Test the plugin locally if you changed skills, commands, or hooks
74	5. Ensure all Markdown is well-formed
75	6. Submit a PR with a clear description
76	
77	## Code of Conduct
78	
79	Be respectful. Focus on the work. Assume good intent.
80	
81	## License
82	
83	By contributing, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the MIT License.
84

1	# metaswarm
2	
3	Multi-agent orchestration framework for software development. 18 specialized agents, 13 skills, quality gates, TDD enforcement.
4	
5	## Getting Started
6	
7	Run `/metaswarm:start-task` to begin tracked work, or `/metaswarm:setup` to configure metaswarm for this project.
8	
9	## Available Commands
10	
11	| Command | Purpose |
12	|---|---|
13	| `/metaswarm:start-task` | Begin tracked work on a task |
14	| `/metaswarm:prime` | Load relevant knowledge before starting |
15	| `/metaswarm:review-design` | Trigger design review gate (5 reviewers) |
16	| `/metaswarm:pr-shepherd` | Monitor a PR through to merge |
17	| `/metaswarm:self-reflect` | Extract learnings after a PR merge |
18	| `/metaswarm:handle-pr-comments` | Handle PR review comments |
19	| `/metaswarm:brainstorm` | Refine an idea before implementation |
20	| `/metaswarm:create-issue` | Create a well-structured GitHub Issue |
21	| `/metaswarm:external-tools-health` | Check status of external AI tools |
22	| `/metaswarm:setup` | Interactive guided setup |
23	| `/metaswarm:status` | Run diagnostic checks |
24	
25	## Workflow
26	
27	For complex features, describe what you want with a Definition of Done and say:
28	`Use the full metaswarm orchestration workflow.`
29	
30	This runs: Research -> Plan -> Design Review Gate -> Work Unit Decomposition -> Orchestrated Execution (4-phase loop per unit) -> Final Review -> PR.
31	
32	## Quality Gates (MANDATORY)
33	
34	- **After brainstorming** -> MUST run `/metaswarm:review-design` before planning
35	- **After any plan** -> MUST run Plan Review Gate before presenting to user
36	- **Before finishing branch** -> MUST run `/metaswarm:self-reflect` before PR
37	- **Coverage** -> `.coverage-thresholds.json` is the single source of truth. BLOCKING gate.
38	- **TDD is mandatory** -> Write tests first, watch them fail, then implement
39	
40	## Rules
41	
42	- NEVER use `--no-verify` on git commits
43	- NEVER use `git push --force` without explicit user approval
44	- ALWAYS follow TDD
45	- STAY within declared file scope
46	
47	## Platform Notes
48	
49	Gemini CLI has limited sub-agent support. When sub-agents are unavailable:
50	- Design review runs sequentially (each reviewer in-session one at a time)
51	- Adversarial review uses rubrics as structured checklists
52	- Quality of review is maintained through rubric structure
53	
54	See `skills/start/references/platform-adaptation.md` for the full cross-platform guide.
55

1	# Getting Started
2	
3	This guide walks you through your first metaswarm-orchestrated workflow.
4	
5	## Step 1: Install metaswarm
6	
7	Choose your platform:
8	
9	**Claude Code:**
10	```bash
11	claude plugin marketplace add dsifry/metaswarm-marketplace
12	claude plugin install metaswarm
13	```
14	
15	**Gemini CLI:**
16	```bash
17	gemini extensions install https://github.com/dsifry/metaswarm.git
18	```
19	
20	**Codex CLI:**
21	```bash
22	curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dsifry/metaswarm/main/.codex/install.sh | bash
23	```
24	
25	**All platforms at once:**
26	```bash
27	npx metaswarm init
28	```
29	
30	Then run setup in your project:
31	- Claude Code: `/setup`
32	- Gemini CLI: `/metaswarm:setup`
33	- Codex CLI: `$setup`
34	
35	The setup skill detects your project's language, framework, test runner, and tools, then configures everything interactively.
36	
37	> **Upgrading from an older version?** If you previously installed via `npx metaswarm init` (pre-v0.10.0), run `/migrate` in Claude Code to clean up old npm-installed files. See [INSTALL.md](INSTALL.md#upgrading-to-v090) for details.
38	
39	Start your first task:
40	- Claude Code: `/start-task`
41	- Gemini CLI: `/metaswarm:start-task`
42	- Codex CLI: `$start`
43	
44	Verify it worked:
45	
46	```bash
47	ls .beads/knowledge/
48	# patterns.jsonl  gotchas.jsonl  decisions.jsonl  ...
49	```
50	
51	Add your first knowledge entry:
52	
53	```bash
54	echo '{"id": "pattern-001", "type": "pattern", "fact": "All API routes require authentication middleware", "recommendation": "Always add authMiddleware() to new route handlers", "confidence": "high", "tags": ["api", "auth"]}' >> .beads/knowledge/patterns.jsonl
55	```
56	
57	## One-Shot Build: From Empty Repo to Working App
58	
59	The fastest way to see metaswarm in action is to give it a project to build from scratch. Here's the full recipe.
60	
61	### 1. Set up the project
62	
63	```bash
64	mkdir my-new-app && cd my-new-app
65	git init
66	npm init -y        # or whatever your stack needs
67	```
68	
69	Then open Claude Code and tell it to install metaswarm:
70	
71	```text
72	> Read through https://github.com/dsifry/metaswarm and install it for my project.
73	```
74	
75	Or install directly: `claude plugin marketplace add dsifry/metaswarm-marketplace` then `claude plugin install metaswarm`, then run `/setup`.
76	
77	### 2. Tell Claude what to build
78	
79	Run `/start-task` with your spec. Include a tech stack, Definition of Done items, and where you want human checkpoints. The more specific the spec, the better the agents perform.
80	
81	```text
82	/start-task I want you to build a real-time collaborative todo list with AI chat.
83	
84	Tech stack: Node.js + Hono, React + Vite, SQLite via better-sqlite3,
85	Server-Sent Events for real-time sync, Anthropic Claude SDK for AI chat,
86	Vitest for testing.
87	
88	Definition of Done:
89	1. Users can create, complete, and delete todo items via the UI
90	2. Todo items persist in SQLite and survive server restarts
91	3. Changes sync in real-time across multiple browser tabs via SSE
92	4. Users can chat with an AI assistant that can read and modify their todos
93	5. AI responses stream in real-time (not buffered)
94	6. All API endpoints have input validation
95	7. 100% test coverage on backend services
96	8. CI pipeline runs tests and lint on every push
97	9. Clean responsive UI that works on mobile
98	
99	File scope:
100	- src/server/ — Backend API and SSE
101	- src/client/ — React frontend
102	- src/shared/ — Shared types
103	- src/server/**/*.test.ts — Backend tests
104	
105	Use the full metaswarm orchestration workflow:
106	research the codebase, create an implementation plan, run the design
107	review gate, decompose into work units, and execute each through the
108	4-phase loop (implement, validate, adversarial review, commit).
109	Set human checkpoints after the database schema and after the AI
110	integration. When all work units pass, create a PR.
111	```
112	
113	That's it. The orchestrator takes over:
114	
115	1. **Research** — Scans your project, notes the tech stack from your prompt
116	2. **Plan** — Architect agent creates an implementation plan with work units
117	3. **Plan Review Gate** — 3 adversarial reviewers (Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment) validate the plan before it reaches the Design Review Gate
118	4. **Plan Validation** — Pre-flight checklist catches structural issues (missing service layer, wrong dependencies, oversized WUs) before spending agent cycles
119	5. **Design Review** — 6 agents review the plan in parallel (PM, Architect, Designer, Security, UX Reviewer, CTO)
120	6. **Decompose** — Breaks the plan into work units with DoD items and dependencies
121	7. **External Dependency Check** — Identifies required API keys/credentials and prompts you to configure them
122	8. **Execute** — For each work unit: implement with TDD, validate independently, adversarial review against DoD. Quality gates are blocking state transitions, not advisory.
123	9. **Checkpoints** — Pauses after schema setup and AI integration for your review
124	10. **Final Review** — Cross-unit integration check after all units pass
125	11. **PR** — Creates the PR and starts shepherding
126	
127	### 4. Review at checkpoints
128	
129	The orchestrator will pause at the checkpoints you specified. You'll see a report like:
130	
131	```text
132	## Checkpoint: Database Schema Complete
133	
134	### Completed Work Units
135	| WU   | Title              | Review          |
136	| ---- | ------------------ | --------------- |
137	| WU-1 | Project scaffolding| Adversarial PASS|
138	| WU-2 | SQLite schema      | Adversarial PASS|
139	
140	### What Comes Next
141	- WU-3: REST API endpoints
142	- WU-4: SSE real-time sync
143	- WU-5: AI chat integration
144	
145	Action required: Reply to continue, or provide feedback.
146	```
147	
148	Review what was built, give feedback if needed, then reply to continue.
149	
150	### 5. Merge and ship
151	
152	After all work units pass and the final comprehensive review is clean, the PR Shepherd monitors CI and handles any review comments. When everything is green, you merge.
153	
154	### What just happened
155	
156	In one prompt, metaswarm:
157	- Decomposed your spec into discrete work units with dependency ordering
158	- Implemented each unit with TDD (tests first, then code)
159	- Independently validated every unit (ran tsc, eslint, vitest itself — never trusted the coding agent)
160	- Had a fresh adversarial reviewer verify each unit against the spec with file:line evidence
161	- Paused for your review at the critical points you specified
162	- Ran a final cross-unit integration check
163	- Created and shepherded a PR
164	
165	You described what you wanted. The system figured out how to build it.
166	
167	### Tips for writing good one-shot specs
168	
169	- **Be specific about DoD items.** "Users can create todos" is better than "todo functionality works." Agents verify exactly what you write.
170	- **Name your tech stack.** Don't make agents guess. Say "Hono + React + SQLite", not "pick a framework."
171	- **Set file scope.** Tell agents where code should live. This prevents sprawl and makes adversarial review effective.
172	- **Include user flows.** Describe what the user sees and does, not just what the code does. Include text wireframes if the app has a UI. This prevents building components that are never wired into the app.
173	- **List external dependencies.** If your app needs API keys (e.g., Anthropic, Stripe), say so in the spec. The orchestrator will prompt you to configure them before building features that depend on them.
174	- **Use human checkpoints.** Put them after risky or foundational work (database schema, auth, AI integration). You can always continue quickly, but you can't easily undo.
175	- **Start with a working spec, not a vague idea.** If you're not sure what you want yet, use `/brainstorm` first to refine the idea, then use the brainstorming output as your spec.
176	
177	---
178	
179	## The Pieces (Step by Step)
180	
181	The rest of this guide walks through metaswarm's components individually. If you just ran the one-shot build above, you've already seen all of these in action.
182	
183	## Step 2: Start Your First Task
184	
185	Just run `/start-task` and describe what you want in plain English. No issue required.
186	
187	```text
188	/start-task Add a webhook system that lets users register callback URLs
189	for events, with retry logic, signature verification, and a delivery log UI.
190	```
191	
192	Pick something with real complexity — a new subsystem, a permissions layer, a reporting pipeline. Something that touches multiple files and needs real architecture. Then watch what happens.
193	
194	This triggers the full pipeline:
195	1. **Prime** — Loads relevant knowledge for the task
196	2. **Research** — Explores your codebase for related patterns
197	3. **Plan** — Creates an implementation plan
198	4. **Plan Review Gate** — 3 adversarial reviewers validate the plan (Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment)
199	5. **Design Review** (if complex) — Runs the 6-agent Design Review Gate
200	6. **Implement** — TDD implementation
201	7. **Review** — Code review + security audit
202	8. **PR** — Creates PR with auto-shepherd
203	
204	### Optional: BEADS Issue Tracking
205	
206	For larger projects, you can create BEADS issues to track work formally. This is optional — `/start-task` works fine with just a plain English description.
207	
208	```bash
209	# Create an epic
210	bd create "Add user profile page" --type epic --priority 2
211	
212	# Create tasks under it
213	bd create "Research existing user data model" --type task --parent <epic-id>
214	bd create "Design profile API endpoints" --type task --parent <epic-id>
215	bd create "Implement profile service" --type task --parent <epic-id>
216	bd create "Build profile UI components" --type task --parent <epic-id>
217	
218	# Add dependencies
219	bd dep add <design-task> <research-task>
220	bd dep add <implement-task> <design-task>
221	bd dep add <ui-task> <implement-task>
222	
223	# Check what's ready to work on
224	bd ready
225	
226	# Then start a specific tracked task
227	/start-task <task-id>
228	```
229	
230	**Team Mode**: If multiple Claude Code sessions are active on the same repository, metaswarm automatically detects this and enables Team Mode with persistent teammates and direct inter-agent messaging. No configuration needed — see `guides/agent-coordination.md` for details.
231	
232	**Development Guides**: The `guides/` directory contains 6 reference guides covering coding standards, testing patterns, git workflow, worktree development, build validation, and agent coordination. Agents load these automatically when relevant.
233	
234	## Step 4: Try the Design Review Gate
235	
236	For a more complex feature, trigger the parallel review manually:
237	
238	```text
239	> /review-design docs/specs/my-feature.md
240	```
241	
242	Six agents review in parallel:
243	- **PM**: Validates use cases and scope
244	- **Architect**: Checks service design and patterns
245	- **Designer**: Reviews API/UX design
246	- **Security**: Threat modeling (STRIDE)
247	- **UX Reviewer**: Verifies user flows, integration work units, and component wiring
248	- **CTO**: TDD readiness and alignment
249	
250	Each produces an APPROVE/REVISE verdict. All six must approve, or the plan iterates (max 3 rounds before human escalation).
251	
252	## Step 4.5: Orchestrated Execution (for Complex Tasks)
253	
254	When your task has a **written spec with Definition of Done items** (e.g., after the Design Review Gate approves a plan), use orchestrated execution for rigorous, verified implementation.
255	
256	The orchestrator breaks the plan into **work units** and runs each through a 4-phase loop:
257	
258	```text
259	IMPLEMENT → VALIDATE → ADVERSARIAL REVIEW → COMMIT
260	```
261	
262	What makes this different from just "implement and review":
263	
264	1. **Independent validation** — The orchestrator runs tests and type checks itself, never trusting the coding agent's self-report
265	2. **Adversarial review** — A fresh reviewer checks each DoD item with file:line evidence. Binary PASS/FAIL, not subjective quality assessment
266	3. **Fresh reviewers** — On re-review after failure, a new reviewer is spawned with zero memory of the previous review
267	4. **Human checkpoints** — Planned pauses at critical boundaries (schema changes, security code) where you review before the agent continues
268	
269	**When to use it**: Multi-unit features with a spec, risky changes, anything where "it works, trust me" isn't good enough.
270	
271	**When NOT to use it**: Single-file fixes, quick prototypes, tasks without a clear spec. For these, the simple task flow in Step 3 is the right choice.
272	
273	See `skills/orchestrated-execution/SKILL.md` for the full pattern, and `rubrics/adversarial-review-rubric.md` for how adversarial reviews differ from standard code reviews.
274	
275	## Step 5: Use Knowledge Priming
276	
277	Before any task, prime your agent with relevant context:
278	
279	```text
280	> /prime
281	```
282	
283	Or with specific filters:
284	
285	```bash
286	bd prime --files "src/api/**/*.ts" --keywords "authentication" --work-type implementation
287	```
288	
289	This outputs prioritized knowledge:
290	1. **MUST FOLLOW** — Critical rules
291	2. **GOTCHAS** — Common pitfalls
292	3. **PATTERNS** — Best practices
293	4. **DECISIONS** — Architectural constraints
294	
295	## Step 6: Set Up Coverage Enforcement
296	
297	The `/setup` command configures coverage enforcement automatically. It creates `.coverage-thresholds.json` in your project root with your coverage command and thresholds.
298	
299	When `.coverage-thresholds.json` exists, agents will be blocked from creating PRs or marking tasks complete until all coverage thresholds pass.
300	
301	For details on the three enforcement gates, see [docs/coverage-enforcement.md](docs/coverage-enforcement.md).
302	
303	## Step 7: Monitor a PR
304	
305	After creating a PR, let the PR Shepherd monitor it:
306	
307	```text
308	> /pr-shepherd 42
309	```
310	
311	The shepherd:
312	- Monitors CI status
313	- Handles review comments
314	- Resolves threads
315	- Reports when ready to merge
316	
317	## Step 8: Extract Learnings
318	
319	After a PR is merged, extract learnings for the knowledge base:
320	
321	```text
322	> /self-reflect
323	```
324	
325	This analyzes recent PR review comments and suggests new knowledge entries for patterns, gotchas, and decisions.
326	
327	## Customization
328	
329	### Adding Your Own Agents
330	
331	Create a new `.md` file in `agents/`:
332	
333	```markdown
334	# My Custom Agent
335	
336	## Role
337	Brief description of the agent's specialty.
338	
339	## Responsibilities
340	- What this agent does
341	- What it produces
342	
343	## Process
344	1. Step-by-step workflow
345	2. What to check
346	3. What to produce
347	
348	## Output Format
349	Description of expected deliverables.
350	```
351	
352	### Adding Rubrics
353	
354	Create a `.md` file in `rubrics/` with scoring criteria that agents reference during reviews.
355	
356	### Adding Knowledge
357	
358	Append JSONL entries to the appropriate file in `knowledge/`:
359	
360	```json
361	{"id": "gotcha-my-api", "type": "gotcha", "fact": "The payments API returns 200 even on failure", "recommendation": "Always check the response body status field, not HTTP status", "confidence": "high", "tags": ["payments", "api"]}
362	```
363	
364	## Optional: Set Up External Tools
365	
366	metaswarm can delegate work to Codex (OpenAI) and Gemini (Google) for cost savings and cross-model adversarial review. This is optional — skip this section if you only want to use Claude.
367	
368	1. **Install** the CLIs:
369	   ```bash
370	   npm i -g @openai/codex @google/gemini-cli
371	   ```
372	
373	2. **Authenticate** each tool:
374	   - Codex: set `OPENAI_API_KEY` in your `.env`
375	   - Gemini: run `gemini` once to log in with Google, or set `GEMINI_API_KEY`
376	   - See [`templates/external-tools-setup.md`](templates/external-tools-setup.md) for full details
377	
378	3. **Copy the config** into your project:
379	   ```bash
380	   mkdir -p .metaswarm && cp templates/external-tools.yaml .metaswarm/
381	   ```
382	
383	4. **Verify** everything works:
384	   ```text
385	   /external-tools-health
386	   ```
387	
388	## What's Next
389	
390	- [USAGE.md](USAGE.md) — Full reference for all agents, skills, and commands
391	- [ORCHESTRATION.md](ORCHESTRATION.md) — The complete orchestration workflow specification
392	- [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) — How to contribute back

1	# Installation
2	
3	metaswarm works with Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and Codex CLI. Install for one platform or all three.
4	
5	## Claude Code (Plugin Marketplace)
6	
7	```bash
8	claude plugin marketplace add dsifry/metaswarm-marketplace
9	claude plugin install metaswarm
10	```
11	
12	Then in Claude Code:
13	
14	```text
15	/setup
16	```
17	
18	## Gemini CLI (Extension)
19	
20	```bash
21	gemini extensions install https://github.com/dsifry/metaswarm.git
22	```
23	
24	Then in Gemini CLI:
25	
26	```text
27	/metaswarm:setup
28	```
29	
30	## Codex CLI (Skills)
31	
32	```bash
33	curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dsifry/metaswarm/main/.codex/install.sh | bash
34	```
35	
36	Then in Codex CLI:
37	
38	```text
39	$setup
40	```
41	
42	## Cross-Platform Installer
43	
44	Detect all installed CLIs and install metaswarm for each:
45	
46	```bash
47	npx metaswarm init
48	```
49	
50	Or target a specific platform:
51	
52	```bash
53	npx metaswarm init --claude
54	npx metaswarm init --codex
55	npx metaswarm init --gemini
56	```
57	
58	After installing, set up your project:
59	
60	```bash
61	npx metaswarm setup
62	```
63	
64	## Platform Comparison
65	
66	| Feature | Claude Code | Gemini CLI | Codex CLI |
67	|---|---|---|---|
68	| Install method | Plugin marketplace | `gemini extensions install` | Clone + symlink |
69	| Commands | `/start-task` | `/metaswarm:start-task` | `$start` |
70	| Instruction file | `CLAUDE.md` | `GEMINI.md` | `AGENTS.md` |
71	| Parallel agents | Full (`Task()`) | Experimental | Sequential only |
72	| Setup command | `/setup` | `/metaswarm:setup` | `$setup` |
73	
74	## Prerequisites
75	
76	1. **One of**: Claude Code, Gemini CLI, or Codex CLI
77	2. **BEADS CLI** (`bd`) — Git-native issue tracking (recommended)
78	   ```bash
79	   curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/steveyegge/beads/main/scripts/install.sh | bash
80	   ```
81	3. **GitHub CLI** (`gh`) — For PR automation (recommended)
82	   ```bash
83	   brew install gh   # macOS
84	   gh auth login
85	   ```
86	4. **Superpowers Plugin** (optional, Claude Code only) — See [External Dependencies](#external-dependencies)
87	
88	## External Dependencies
89	
90	metaswarm's skills reference these external skills from the [superpowers](https://github.com/obra/superpowers) Claude Code plugin:
91	
92	| Skill | Used By | Purpose |
93	|---|---|---|
94	| `superpowers:brainstorming` | Design Review Gate, Brainstorming Extension | Collaborative design ideation before implementation |
95	| `superpowers:test-driven-development` | PR Shepherd, Coder Agent | RED-GREEN-REFACTOR implementation cycle |
96	| `superpowers:systematic-debugging` | PR Shepherd | Four-phase bug investigation framework |
97	| `superpowers:writing-plans` | Design Review Gate, Brainstorming Extension | Detailed implementation plan generation |
98	| `superpowers:using-git-worktrees` | Design Review Gate | Isolated workspace creation for parallel dev |
99	
100	**Install superpowers** (follow their README for current instructions):
101	```bash
102	# See: https://github.com/obra/superpowers
103	claude plugin add obra/superpowers
104	```
105	
106	**Without superpowers**: metaswarm still works — the core orchestration (agents, BEADS, review gates, rubrics) is self-contained. The superpowers references are in skill trigger chains and can be removed or replaced with your own equivalents.
107	
108	## Optional: External AI Tools
109	
110	metaswarm can delegate implementation and review tasks to **Codex CLI** (OpenAI) and **Gemini CLI** (Google) for cost savings and cross-model adversarial review. This is entirely optional — metaswarm works fine without any external tools.
111	
112	**Quick setup:**
113	
114	```bash
115	npm i -g @openai/codex @google/gemini-cli
116	```
117	
118	After installing, see [`templates/external-tools-setup.md`](templates/external-tools-setup.md) for the full configuration guide (authentication, model selection, budget controls, and routing options).
119	
120	To verify your setup, run the health check command in Claude Code:
121	
122	```text
123	/external-tools-health
124	```
125	
126	This checks that each tool is installed, authenticated, and responsive.
127	
128	## Upgrading to v0.9.0
129	
130	v0.9.0 moved metaswarm from npm distribution to the Claude Code plugin marketplace. If you're on an older version, follow the instructions for your situation:
131	
132	### From v0.7.x or v0.8.x (npm-installed)
133	
134	This is the most common upgrade path. Your project has metaswarm files in `.claude/plugins/metaswarm/` that were copied there by `npx metaswarm init`.
135	
136	1. **Install the plugin:**
137	   ```bash
138	   claude plugin marketplace add dsifry/metaswarm-marketplace
139	   claude plugin install metaswarm
140	   ```
141	
142	2. **Run the migration** in Claude Code:
143	   ```text
144	   /migrate
145	   ```
146	   This detects old `.claude/plugins/metaswarm/` files, verifies content matches the plugin versions, and removes the redundant copies. Your project-specific files (CLAUDE.md, `.coverage-thresholds.json`, `.beads/`, `bin/`, `scripts/`) are never touched. All removals are staged with `git rm` — nothing is permanently deleted until you commit.
147	
148	3. **Verify the migration:**
149	   ```text
150	   /status
151	   ```
152	
153	4. **Review and commit** the cleanup when you're satisfied.
154	
155	**Command name changes:** The old `/metaswarm-setup` and `/metaswarm-update-version` commands have been renamed to `/setup` and `/update`. Legacy aliases are preserved, so old names still work, but new projects should use the short names.
156	
157	### From v0.6.x or earlier (npm-installed, no guided setup)
158	
159	These versions used `npx metaswarm init --full` without the guided setup skill. Follow the same steps as v0.7.x/v0.8.x above, then re-run `/setup` to take advantage of the interactive configuration:
160	
161	```text
162	/setup
163	```
164	
165	This re-detects your project and applies any configuration improvements from newer versions. It won't overwrite your existing customizations — it prompts before making changes.
166	
167	### Already on v0.9.0 (plugin-installed)
168	
169	Just update in Claude Code:
170	
171	```text
172	/update
173	```
174	
175	This checks for new versions, shows what changed, and updates all component files while preserving your customizations.
176	
177	### Automatic legacy detection
178	
179	If you skip the manual migration, the session-start hook will detect the old npm installation when you open Claude Code and prompt you to run `/migrate`. You can also run `/status` at any time to check for legacy files.
180	
181	## Check Installation Status
182	
183	```text
184	/status
185	```
186	
187	This runs 9 diagnostic checks: plugin version, project setup, command shims, legacy install detection, BEADS plugin, bd CLI, external tools, coverage thresholds, and Node.js.
188	
189	## npm Package (Cross-Platform Installer)
190	
191	The npm package (`npx metaswarm`) is now the cross-platform installer. It detects your installed CLIs and installs metaswarm for each.
192	
193	```bash
194	npx metaswarm init          # Auto-detect and install for all CLIs
195	npx metaswarm setup         # Set up project (writes instruction files)
196	npx metaswarm detect        # Show which CLIs are available
197	```
198	
199	## Customizing for Your Project
200	
201	After installation, the `/setup` command handles most customization automatically. For manual customization:
202	
203	### Agent Commands (in `agents/coder-agent.md`)
204	
205	| Placeholder | Example: TypeScript | Example: Python | Example: Rust |
206	|---|---|---|---|
207	| Test runner | `pnpm test` | `pytest` | `cargo test` |
208	| Linter | `pnpm lint` | `ruff check .` | `cargo clippy` |
209	| Formatter | `pnpm prettier --check .` | `ruff format --check .` | `cargo fmt --check` |
210	| Type checker | `pnpm typecheck` | `mypy .` | (built into `cargo check`) |
211	| Build | `pnpm build` | `python -m build` | `cargo build` |
212	
213	### Coverage Thresholds (in `.coverage-thresholds.json`)
214	
215	```json
216	{
217	  "thresholds": {
218	    "lines": 100,
219	    "branches": 100,
220	    "functions": 100,
221	    "statements": 100
222	  },
223	  "enforcement": {
224	    "command": "pnpm test:coverage",
225	    "blockPRCreation": true,
226	    "blockTaskCompletion": true
227	  }
228	}
229	```
230	
231	Set `enforcement.command` to your project's coverage command (e.g., `pytest --cov`, `cargo tarpaulin`, `go test -cover`). When this file exists, agents must pass all thresholds before pushing or creating PRs.
232	
233	## Verify Installation
234	
235	```bash
236	# Check BEADS is working
237	bd status
238	
239	# Check knowledge base
240	bd prime
241	
242	# In Claude Code, verify commands are available
243	# Type / and you should see start-task, review-design, etc.
244	```
245

1	# metaswarm
2	
3	A self-improving multi-agent orchestration framework for [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code), Gemini CLI, and Codex CLI. Coordinate 18 specialized AI agents and 13 orchestration skills through a complete software development lifecycle, from issue to merged PR, with recursive orchestration, parallel review gates, and a git-native knowledge base.
4	
5	## What Is This?
6	
7	metaswarm is an extraction of a production-tested agentic orchestration system. It has been proven in the field writing production-level code with 100% test coverage, mandatory TDD, multi-reviewed spec-driven development, and SDLC best practices across hundreds of PRs. It provides:
8	
9	- **18 specialized agent personas** (Researcher, Architect, Coder, Security Auditor, PR Shepherd, etc.)
10	- **A structured 9-phase workflow**: Research → Plan → Design Review Gate → Work Unit Decomposition → Orchestrated Execution → Final Review → PR Creation → PR Shepherd → Closure & Learning
11	- **4-Phase Orchestrated Execution Loop**: Each work unit runs through IMPLEMENT → VALIDATE → ADVERSARIAL REVIEW → COMMIT. The orchestrator validates independently (never trusts subagent self-reports), and adversarial reviewers check DoD compliance with file:line evidence
12	- **Parallel Design Review Gate**: 5 specialist agents (PM, Architect, Designer, Security, CTO) review in parallel with a 3-iteration cap before human escalation
13	- **Recursive orchestration**: Swarm Coordinators spawn Issue Orchestrators, which spawn sub-orchestrators for complex epics (swarm of swarms)
14	- **Git-native task tracking**: Uses [BEADS](https://github.com/steveyegge/beads) (`bd` CLI) for issue/task management, dependencies, and knowledge priming
15	- **Knowledge base**: JSONL-based fact store for patterns, gotchas, decisions, and anti-patterns — agents prime from this before every task
16	- **Quality rubrics**: Standardized review criteria for code, architecture, security, testing, planning, and adversarial spec compliance
17	- **External AI tool delegation**: Optionally delegate implementation and review tasks to OpenAI Codex CLI and Google Gemini CLI for cost savings and cross-model adversarial review
18	- **Visual review**: Playwright-based screenshot capture for reviewing web UIs, presentations, and rendered pages
19	- **PR lifecycle automation**: Autonomous CI monitoring, review comment handling, and thread resolution
20	- **Workflow enforcement**: Mandatory quality gate intercepts at every handoff point — agents cannot skip design review, plan review, or knowledge capture
21	- **Context recovery**: Approved plans and execution state persist to disk via BEADS, surviving context compaction and session interruption
22	
23	## Architecture
24	
25	```text
26	Your prompt (spec with DoD items) or GitHub Issue
27	        │
28	        ▼
29	┌─────────────────────────────────┐
30	│  Swarm Coordinator               │
31	│  - Assign to worktree            │
32	│  - Spawn Issue Orchestrator      │
33	└─────────────────────────────────┘
34	        │
35	        ▼
36	┌─────────────────────────────────┐
37	│  Issue Orchestrator              │
38	│  - Create BEADS epic             │
39	│  - Decompose into work units     │
40	└─────────────────────────────────┘
41	        │
42	        ▼
43	  Research → Plan → Design Review Gate (5 parallel reviewers)
44	        │
45	        ▼
46	  Work Unit Decomposition (DoD items, file scopes, dependency graph)
47	        │
48	        ▼
49	  Orchestrated Execution Loop (per work unit):
50	    IMPLEMENT → VALIDATE → ADVERSARIAL REVIEW → COMMIT
51	    (Optionally delegates IMPLEMENT to Codex/Gemini CLI)
52	    (Cross-model REVIEW: writer always reviewed by different model)
53	        │
54	        ▼
55	  Final Comprehensive Review (cross-unit integration)
56	        │
57	        ▼
58	  PR Creation → PR Shepherd (auto-monitors to merge)
59	        │
60	        ▼
61	  Closure → Knowledge Extraction (feedback loop)
62	```
63	
64	## Repository Structure

156	## Self-Learning System
157	
158	metaswarm doesn't just execute — it learns from every session and gets smarter over time.
159	
160	### Automatic Reflection
161	
162	After every PR merge, the self-reflect workflow (`/self-reflect`) analyzes what happened:
163	
164	- **Code review feedback** — Extracts patterns, gotchas, and anti-patterns from reviewer comments (both human and automated) and writes them back to the knowledge base as structured JSONL entries
165	- **Build and test failures** — Captures what broke and why, so agents avoid the same mistakes in future tasks
166	- **Architectural decisions** — Records the rationale behind choices so future agents understand the "why", not just the "what"
167	
168	### Conversation Introspection
169	
170	The reflection system also introspects into the Claude Code session itself, looking for:
171	
172	- **User repetition** — When a user corrects the same behavior multiple times or repeats instructions, this signals an opportunity for a new skill or command. The system flags these as candidates for automation.
173	- **User disagreements** — When a user rejects or overrides Claude's recommendation, the system captures the user's preferred approach as a knowledge base entry, so agents align with the user's intent in future sessions.
174	- **Friction points** — Repeated manual steps that could be codified into reusable workflows.
175	
176	These signals feed back into the knowledge base and can generate proposals for new skills, updated rubrics, or revised agent behaviors.
177	
178	### Selective Knowledge Priming
179	
180	The knowledge base grows continuously, but agents don't load all of it. The `bd prime` command uses **selective retrieval** — filtering by affected files, keywords, and work type to load only the relevant subset:
181	
182	```bash
183	# Only loads knowledge relevant to auth files and implementation work
184	bd prime --files "src/api/auth/**" --keywords "authentication" --work-type implementation
185	```
186	
187	This means the knowledge base can grow to hundreds or thousands of entries without consuming context window. Agents get precisely the facts they need — the 5 critical gotchas for the files they're about to touch, not the entire institutional memory.
188	
189	## Design Principles
190	
191	1. **Knowledge-Driven Development** — Agents prime from the knowledge base before every task, reducing repeated mistakes
192	2. **Trust Nothing, Verify Everything** — Orchestrators validate independently (run tests themselves, never trust subagent self-reports), review adversarially against written spec contracts, and optionally use cross-model review via external AI tools
193	3. **Parallel Review Gates** — Independent specialist reviewers run concurrently, not sequentially
194	4. **Recursive Orchestration** — Orchestrators spawn sub-orchestrators for any level of complexity
195	5. **Agent Ownership** — Each agent owns its lifecycle; the orchestrator delegates, not micromanages
196	6. **BEADS as Source of Truth** — All task state lives in BEADS; agents coordinate via database, not messages
197	7. **Test-First Always** — TDD is mandatory, not optional. Coverage thresholds are enforced as a blocking gate before PR creation via `.coverage-thresholds.json`
198	8. **Git-Native Everything** — Issues, knowledge, specs all in version control
199	9. **Human-in-the-Loop** — Proactive checkpoints at planned review points, plus automatic escalation after 3 failed iterations or ambiguous decisions

68	## Choosing a Workflow
69	
70	Not every task needs the full orchestration machinery. Use the right level of process for the task at hand:
71	
72	| Task Type | Examples | Workflow | Key Skill |
73	|---|---|---|---|
74	| **Quick fix** | Typo, config change, copy update | Edit → test → commit | None needed |
75	| **Simple task** | Bug fix, small feature, validation | `/start-task` → simple flow | TDD only |
76	| **Complex task** (no spec) | New feature, refactor, optimization | `/start-task` → BEADS epic → linear implementation | Design Review Gate |
77	| **Complex task** (with spec & DoD) | Multi-unit feature, risky change, multi-agent work | `/start-task` → BEADS epic → orchestrated execution | **Orchestrated Execution** |
78	
79	### Team Mode
80	
81	metaswarm automatically detects **Team Mode** when multiple Claude Code sessions are active on the same repository (e.g., in different worktrees or terminals). In Team Mode, agents behave as persistent teammates with context retention across sessions and direct inter-agent messaging for coordination. Mode detection is automatic — no configuration needed. See `guides/agent-coordination.md` for the full Team Mode protocol.
82	
83	**When to use Orchestrated Execution:**
84	
85	- The task has a **written spec** with enumerable Definition of Done items
86	- The implementation involves **multiple work units** (3+ logical changes)
87	- You need **independent verification** — subagent self-reports aren't sufficient
88	- The changes are **high-stakes** (schema changes, security, new architectural patterns)
89	- You want **proactive human checkpoints** at planned review points
90	
91	**When NOT to use Orchestrated Execution:**
92	
93	- Single-file bug fixes or copy changes
94	- Tasks without a written spec or DoD items
95	- Quick prototyping or exploratory work
96	- Tasks where the overhead of decomposition exceeds the work itself
97	
98	## Skills
99	
100	Skills are orchestration behaviors that coordinate multiple agents.
101	
102	### Orchestrated Execution
103	
104	**Path**: `skills/orchestrated-execution/SKILL.md`
105	
106	The 4-phase execution loop for rigorous, spec-driven implementation. Decomposes work into units and runs each through IMPLEMENT → VALIDATE → ADVERSARIAL REVIEW → COMMIT.
107	
108	- **Plan validation (pre-flight)**: Checklist catches structural issues (architecture, dependencies, API contracts, security, UI/UX, external deps) before design review
109	- **Work unit decomposition**: Break plan into discrete units with DoD items, file scopes, and dependency graphs
110	- **Independent validation**: Orchestrator runs tsc, eslint, vitest directly — never trusts subagent self-reports
111	- **Quality gate enforcement**: Gates are blocking state transitions, not advisory. FAIL means retry or escalate, never skip.
112	- **Coverage enforcement**: Reads `.coverage-thresholds.json` and runs the enforcement command. BLOCKING gate.
113	- **Adversarial review**: Fresh reviewer checks each DoD item with file:line evidence. Binary PASS/FAIL
114	- **Human checkpoints**: Planned pauses at critical boundaries (schema, security, external service credentials, new patterns)
115	- **Project context document**: Maintained by orchestrator, passed to each coder subagent to prevent context loss
116	- **Service inventory tracking**: `SERVICE-INVENTORY.md` updated after each commit to track services, factories, and shared modules
117	- **Final comprehensive review**: Cross-unit integration check after all units pass
118	- **Recovery protocol**: DIAGNOSE → CLASSIFY → RETRY (max 3) → ESCALATE with failure history
119	
120	Uses the `adversarial-review-rubric.md` for reviews (distinct from the collaborative `code-review-rubric.md`).
121	
122	### Design Review Gate
123	
124	**Path**: `skills/design-review-gate/SKILL.md`
125	
126	Spawns 6 agents in parallel to review a design document:
127	
128	```text
129	/review-design <path-to-spec>
130	```
131	
132	- PM, Architect, Designer, Security, UX Reviewer, CTO review simultaneously
133	- All 6 must APPROVE
134	- Max 3 iterations before human escalation
135	- Catches architectural issues before implementation begins
136	
137	### PR Shepherd
138	
139	**Path**: `skills/pr-shepherd/SKILL.md`
140	
141	Autonomously monitors a PR through to merge:
142	
143	```text
144	/pr-shepherd <pr-number>
145	```
146	
147	- Polls CI status
148	- Fixes linting/type errors
149	- Responds to review comments
150	- Resolves discussion threads
151	- Reports when ready for human approval
152	
153	### Handling PR Comments
154	
155	**Path**: `skills/handling-pr-comments/SKILL.md`
156	
157	Systematic workflow for addressing review feedback:
158	
159	```text
160	/handle-pr-comments <pr-number>
161	```
162	
163	- Categorizes comments by priority
164	- Addresses actionable items
165	- Marks out-of-scope items with rationale
166	- Resolves threads after addressing
167	
168	### Plan Review Gate
169	
170	**Path**: `skills/plan-review-gate/SKILL.md`
171	
172	Validates every implementation plan through 3 adversarial reviewers before execution begins:
173	
174	- **Feasibility Reviewer**: Assesses technical viability, dependency risks, and resource constraints
175	- **Completeness Reviewer**: Checks for missing work units, untested edge cases, and gaps in the Definition of Done
176	- **Scope & Alignment Reviewer**: Verifies the plan stays within the issue scope and aligns with codebase conventions
177	
178	All 3 reviewers must APPROVE before the plan proceeds to implementation. Sits between the planning phase and the Design Review Gate in the workflow pipeline.
179	
180	### External Tools
181	
182	**Path**: `skills/external-tools/SKILL.md`
183	
184	Delegates implementation and review tasks to external AI CLI tools (OpenAI Codex CLI, Google Gemini CLI) for cost savings and cross-model adversarial review:
185	
186	- **Adapter protocol**: Uniform shell adapter interface (health, implement, review) with structured JSON output
187	- **Cross-model review**: Writer is always reviewed by a different AI model, eliminating single-model blind spots
188	- **Availability-aware escalation**: Model A (2 tries) → Model B (2 tries) → Claude (1 try) → user alert. Degrades gracefully with 0, 1, or 2 tools
189	- **Sandboxed execution**: Isolated git worktrees, minimal environment (`env -i`), timeout protection
190	- **Budget enforcement**: Per-task and per-session USD circuit breakers
191	- **Health checks**: `/external-tools-health` verifies installation, authentication, and reachability
192	
193	Configuration: `.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml`. See `templates/external-tools-setup.md` for setup.
194	
195	### Visual Review
196	
197	**Path**: `skills/visual-review/SKILL.md`
198	
199	Captures screenshots of web pages and UIs using Playwright for visual inspection and iteration:
200	
201	- **Playwright screenshots**: Captures at configurable viewport sizes (desktop, tablet, mobile)
202	- **Reveal.js support**: Captures individual slides by hash navigation
203	- **Remote support**: HTTP file server for headless/SSH environments where `open` is unavailable
204	- **Review checklist**: Layout, typography, colors, spacing, content, responsiveness
205	- **Fix-and-recapture loop**: Make fixes, re-capture affected pages, verify visually
206	
207	Prerequisites: `npx playwright install chromium`

256	## Knowledge Base
257	
258	### File Schema
259	
260	All files in `knowledge/` use JSONL format:
261	
262	```json
263	{
264	  "id": "unique-identifier",
265	  "type": "pattern|gotcha|decision|anti-pattern|codebase-fact|api-behavior",
266	  "fact": "Clear description of the knowledge",
267	  "recommendation": "What to do about it",
268	  "confidence": "high|medium|low",
269	  "provenance": [{"source": "human|agent|review", "reference": "PR #123"}],
270	  "tags": ["relevant", "tags"],
271	  "affectedFiles": ["src/path/to/file.ts"],
272	  "createdAt": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z"
273	}
274	```
275	
276	### Knowledge Categories
277	
278	| File | Purpose | Example |
279	|---|---|---|
280	| `patterns.jsonl` | Reusable best practices | "Use factory functions for mock creation" |
281	| `gotchas.jsonl` | Common pitfalls | "API returns 200 on soft failures" |
282	| `decisions.jsonl` | Architectural choices | "Chose Zustand over Redux for simplicity" |
283	| `anti-patterns.jsonl` | What to avoid | "Never use `as any` for type casting" |
284	| `codebase-facts.jsonl` | Code-specific behaviors | "Auth middleware runs before route handlers" |
285	| `api-behaviors.jsonl` | External API quirks | "Rate limit is 100 req/min on free tier" |
286	
287	## Coverage Enforcement
288	
289	metaswarm supports configurable test coverage thresholds that block PR creation and task completion. This ensures agents cannot ship code that drops coverage below your project's standards.
290	
291	### Setup
292	
293	The `/setup` command configures coverage enforcement automatically. It creates `.coverage-thresholds.json` in your project root with your coverage command and thresholds.
294	
295	For manual setup, copy the template to your project root:
296	
297	```bash
298	cp templates/coverage-thresholds.json .coverage-thresholds.json
299	```
300	
301	### Configuration
302	
303	Edit `.coverage-thresholds.json` to set your thresholds and coverage command:
304	
305	```json
306	{
307	  "thresholds": {
308	    "lines": 100,
309	    "branches": 100,
310	    "functions": 100,
311	    "statements": 100
312	  },
313	  "enforcement": {
314	    "command": "pnpm test:coverage",
315	    "blockPRCreation": true,
316	    "blockTaskCompletion": true
317	  }
318	}
319	```
320	
321	| Field | Description |
322	|---|---|
323	| `thresholds.lines` | Minimum line coverage percentage |
324	| `thresholds.branches` | Minimum branch coverage percentage |
325	| `thresholds.functions` | Minimum function coverage percentage |
326	| `thresholds.statements` | Minimum statement coverage percentage |
327	| `enforcement.command` | Shell command to run coverage (e.g., `pytest --cov`, `cargo tarpaulin`, `go test -cover`) |
328	| `enforcement.blockPRCreation` | If `true`, agents cannot create a PR until all thresholds pass |
329	| `enforcement.blockTaskCompletion` | If `true`, agents cannot mark a task complete until all thresholds pass |
330	
331	### How Agents Use It
332	
333	When `.coverage-thresholds.json` exists in the project root:
334	
335	1. **Before pushing or creating a PR**, agents run the `enforcement.command`
336	2. If any threshold is not met, the agent **stops and fixes coverage** before proceeding
337	3. The task-completion-checklist enforces this as a blocking gate
338	
339	### Single Source of Truth
340	
341	Your test runner config (e.g., `vitest.config.ts`, `jest.config.js`, `pyproject.toml`) can read thresholds directly from `.coverage-thresholds.json` to avoid duplication. Example for Vitest:
342	
343	```typescript
344	import fs from "node:fs";
345	import path from "node:path";
346	
347	const coverageConfig = JSON.parse(
348	  fs.readFileSync(path.resolve(__dirname, ".coverage-thresholds.json"), "utf-8"),
349	);
350	
351	export default defineConfig({
352	  test: {
353	    coverage: {
354	      thresholds: coverageConfig.thresholds,
355	    },
356	  },
357	});
358	```
359	
360	This way both agents and CI enforce the same thresholds from a single file.
361	
362	### Automated Enforcement Gates
363	
364	Beyond agent checklists, metaswarm provides two automated enforcement mechanisms:
365	
366	- **CI Job**: A GitHub Actions job template (`templates/ci-coverage-job.yml`) that reads and runs `enforcement.command` from `.coverage-thresholds.json`. Add it to your workflow to block merges on coverage failures.
367	- **Pre-push Hook**: A Husky-compatible hook (`templates/pre-push`) that runs lint, typecheck, format checks, and coverage enforcement before every `git push`. Configured automatically by `/setup` when `.husky/` exists.
368	
369	See [docs/coverage-enforcement.md](docs/coverage-enforcement.md) for full setup instructions.
370	
371	## Rubrics
372	
373	Quality standards used by review agents:
374	
375	| Rubric | Used By | Covers |
376	|---|---|---|
377	| `code-review-rubric.md` | Code Review Agent (collaborative mode) | Style, patterns, error handling, testing |
378	| `adversarial-review-rubric.md` | Code Review Agent (adversarial mode) | Binary PASS/FAIL spec compliance, DoD verification with evidence |
379	| `architecture-rubric.md` | Architect Agent | Service design, coupling, scalability |
380	| `security-review-rubric.md` | Security Auditor | OWASP Top 10, auth, data handling |
381	| `plan-review-rubric.md` | CTO Agent | TDD readiness, completeness, alignment |
382	| `test-coverage-rubric.md` | Test Automator | Coverage, edge cases, mock quality |
383	| `external-tool-review-rubric.md` | Cross-model adversarial reviewers | Binary PASS/FAIL for cross-model review, file:line evidence |
384	
385	## Scripts
386	
387	| Script | Purpose |
388	|---|---|
389	| `scripts/beads-fetch-pr-comments.ts` | Fetch PR review comments from GitHub |
390	| `scripts/beads-fetch-conversation-history.ts` | Extract conversation history from Claude Code sessions |
391	| `bin/pr-comments-check.sh` | Verify all review comments addressed |
392	| `bin/pr-comments-filter.sh` | Filter actionable vs non-actionable comments |
393	| `bin/external-tools-verify.sh` | End-to-end verification of external tools setup (15 checks) |
394	
395	## Workflow Phases
396	
397	The full orchestration lifecycle:
398	
399	| Phase | Agent(s) | Output |
400	|---|---|---|
401	| 1. Research | Researcher | Codebase analysis, pattern inventory |
402	| 2. Planning | Architect | Implementation plan with tasks |
403	| 2a. External Dependency Detection | Issue Orchestrator | Identifies API keys/credentials, prompts user |
404	| 2b. Plan Validation | Issue Orchestrator | Pre-flight checklist (architecture, deps, API contracts, security, UI/UX) |
405	| 3. Design Review | PM + Architect + Designer + Security + UX Reviewer + CTO (parallel) | APPROVE/REVISE verdicts |
406	| 4. Work Unit Decomposition | Issue Orchestrator | Work units with DoD items, file scopes, dependency graph |
407	| 5. Orchestrated Execution | Coder (or External Tool) + Orchestrator + Adversarial Reviewer (per unit) | IMPLEMENT → VALIDATE → ADVERSARIAL REVIEW → COMMIT loop (optionally delegates to Codex/Gemini) |
408	| 6. Final Comprehensive Review | Issue Orchestrator | Cross-unit integration check, full test suite |
409	| 7. PR Creation | Issue Orchestrator | GitHub PR |
410	| 8. PR Shepherd | PR Shepherd | CI fixes, comment responses |
411	| 9. Closure | Knowledge Curator | Knowledge base updates |
412	
413	**Note**: For simple tasks without DoD items, phases 4-6 collapse into a linear implementation + code review flow. The orchestrated execution loop is only activated when the task has a written spec with enumerable DoD items.
414

1	# Architect Agent
2	
3	**Type**: `architect-agent`
4	**Role**: Implementation planning and architecture design
5	**Spawned By**: Issue Orchestrator
6	**Tools**: Codebase read, architecture-rubric, BEADS CLI
7	
8	---
9	
10	## Purpose
11	
12	The Architect Agent creates implementation plans that follow codebase architecture and patterns. It researches existing patterns, designs the solution structure, and documents the plan for CTO review.
13	
14	---
15	
16	## Responsibilities
17	
18	1. **Research**: Understand existing patterns and architecture
19	2. **Design**: Create implementation plan following conventions
20	3. **Documentation**: Document plan for CTO review
21	4. **Pattern Selection**: Choose appropriate design patterns
22	5. **Risk Identification**: Identify technical risks and dependencies
23	
24	---
25	
26	## Activation
27	
28	Triggered when:
29	
30	- Issue Orchestrator creates a "planning" task
31	- Research task is complete (blocked-by relationship cleared)
32	- Requirements are understood
33	
34	---
35	
36	## Workflow
37	
38	### Step 0: Knowledge Priming (CRITICAL)
39	
40	**BEFORE any other work**, prime your context with relevant knowledge:
41	
42	```bash
43	# Prime with planning-specific context and file patterns
44	bd prime --work-type planning --keywords "<feature-keywords>"
45	
46	# If you know which files will be affected:
47	bd prime --work-type planning --files "src/lib/services/*.ts" --keywords "<keywords>"
48	```
49	
50	Review the output and note:
51	
52	- **MUST FOLLOW** rules (NEVER use `as any`, TDD is mandatory, etc.)
53	- **GOTCHAS** to avoid in your design
54	- **PATTERNS** already established in this codebase
55	- **DECISIONS** that constrain architectural choices
56	
57	### Step 1: Gather Context
58	
59	```bash
60	# Get the task details
61	bd show <task-id> --json
62	
63	# Get research findings from Researcher Agent
64	bd show <research-task-id> --json
65	
66	# Get the GitHub Issue requirements
67	gh issue view <issue-number> --json title,body
68	```
69	
70	### Step 2: Study Architecture
71	
72	Load key architecture documents:
73	
74	```bash
75	# Current architecture
76	cat docs/ARCHITECTURE_CURRENT.md
77	
78	# Service creation guide
79	cat docs/SERVICE_CREATION_GUIDE.md
80	
81	# Backend patterns
82	cat docs/BACKEND_SERVICE_GUIDE.md
83	
84	# Existing services inventory
85	cat docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md
86	```
87	
88	### Step 3: Find Similar Implementations
89	
90	Search for similar features in the codebase:
91	
92	```bash
93	# Find related services
94	grep -r "<keyword>" src/lib/services/ --include="*.ts" -l
95	
96	# Find related API routes
97	grep -r "<keyword>" src/api/routes/ --include="*.ts" -l
98	
99	# Check how similar problems were solved
100	git log --oneline --all --grep="<similar-feature>" | head -10
101	```
102	
103	### Step 4: Design the Solution
104	
105	For each component, determine:
106	
107	#### Service Layer Placement
108	
109	```
110	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
111	│ API Routes (src/api/routes/)                                │
112	│ - Hono HTTP handling, request/response                      │
113	│ - Validation with Zod schemas                               │
114	│ - Auth checks with Clerk middleware                         │
115	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
116	                              ▼
117	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
118	│ Orchestrator Services (*-orchestrator.service.ts)           │
119	│ - Coordinate multiple services                              │
120	│ - Handle complex workflows                                  │
121	│ - Manage transactions                                       │
122	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
123	                              ▼
124	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
125	│ Pure Services (pure-*.service.ts)                           │
126	│ - Business logic only                                       │
127	│ - No side effects (database, external APIs)                 │
128	│ - Easily testable                                           │
129	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
130	                              ▼
131	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
132	│ Persistence Services (*-persistence.service.ts)             │
133	│ - Database operations                                       │
134	│ - Query building                                            │
135	│ - Data transformation                                       │
136	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
137	                              ▼
138	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
139	│ Adapters (*-adapter.ts)                                     │
140	│ - External API wrappers                                     │
141	│ - Error normalization                                       │
142	│ - Response mapping                                          │
143	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
144	```
145	
146	#### Pattern Selection
147	
148	| Situation                       | Pattern         | Example              |
149	| ------------------------------- | --------------- | -------------------- |
150	| Multiple implementations        | Strategy        | AI providers         |
151	| Complex object creation         | Factory         | Mock factories       |
152	| Common workflow with variations | Template Method | Pipeline processing  |
153	| External API integration        | Adapter         | Gmail, Stripe        |
154	| Data access                     | Repository      | Persistence services |
155	
156	### Step 5: Create Implementation Plan
157	
158	```markdown
159	# Implementation Plan: <Feature Name>
160	
161	## Overview
162	
163	<1-2 sentence description of what will be built>
164	
165	## Requirements Summary
166	
167	From GitHub Issue #<number>:
168	
169	- Requirement 1
170	- Requirement 2
171	- Requirement 3
172	
173	## Architecture Decisions
174	
175	### Service Structure
176	```
177	
178	src/lib/services/├── <feature>/
179	│ ├── <feature>.service.ts # Orchestrator
180	│ ├── <feature>.service.test.ts # Tests
181	│ ├── pure-<feature>.service.ts # Pure logic
182	│ └── <feature>-persistence.service.ts # DB ops
183	
184	````
185	
186	### Pattern Choices
187	- **Pattern**: <name>
188	- **Reason**: <why this pattern>
189	- **Similar to**: <existing example in codebase>
190	
191	## Components
192	
193	### 1. <Component Name>
194	**Location**: `src/lib/services/<path>`
195	**Type**: Pure Service | Persistence | Orchestrator | Adapter
196	**Purpose**: <what it does>
197	
198	**Interface**:
199	```typescript
200	interface <ComponentName> {
201	  methodOne(input: InputType): Promise<OutputType>;
202	  methodTwo(input: InputType): Promise<OutputType>;
203	}
204	````
205	
206	**Dependencies**:
207	
208	- Prisma (injected)
209	- OtherService (injected)
210	
211	### 2. <Component Name>
212	
213	...
214	
215	## API Changes
216	
217	### New Endpoints
218	
219	| Method | Path          | Purpose   |
220	| ------ | ------------- | --------- |
221	| POST   | `/api/<path>` | <purpose> |
222	| GET    | `/api/<path>` | <purpose> |
223	
224	### Request/Response Schemas
225	
226	```typescript
227	// Request
228	const RequestSchema = z.object({
229	  field: z.string(),
230	});
231	
232	// Response
233	const ResponseSchema = z.object({
234	  id: z.string(),
235	  result: z.string(),
236	});
237	```
238	
239	## Database Changes
240	
241	### Schema Changes
242	
243	```prisma
244	model NewModel {
245	  id        String   @id @default(cuid())
246	  userId    String
247	  field     String
248	  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
249	
250	  user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
251	}
252	```
253	
254	### Migration Required
255	
256	- [ ] Yes - describe changes
257	- [ ] No - no schema changes
258	
259	### Indexes Needed
260	
261	- `@@index([userId])` for user queries
262	- `@@index([field])` if searched
263	
264	## Testing Strategy
265	
266	### Unit Tests
267	
268	- [ ] Pure service logic
269	- [ ] Persistence operations (mocked Prisma)
270	- [ ] Orchestrator coordination
271	
272	### Integration Tests
273	
274	- [ ] API route behavior
275	- [ ] Database operations
276	
277	### Mocking Strategy
278	
279	- Use mock factories from `src/test-utils/factories/`
280	- Mock external APIs completely
281	
282	## Risks and Mitigations
283	
284	| Risk   | Likelihood   | Impact       | Mitigation   |
285	| ------ | ------------ | ------------ | ------------ |
286	| <risk> | Low/Med/High | Low/Med/High | <mitigation> |
287	
288	## Dependencies
289	
290	### Internal
291	
292	- Depends on: <existing service>
293	- Blocked by: <other task if any>
294	
295	### External
296	
297	- <External API if any>
298	
299	## Implementation Order
300	
301	1. Create types and interfaces
302	2. Write tests for pure service
303	3. Implement pure service
304	4. Write tests for persistence
305	5. Implement persistence
306	6. Write tests for orchestrator
307	7. Implement orchestrator
308	8. Create API route
309	9. Integration testing
310	
311	## Success Criteria
312	
313	- [ ] All tests passing
314	- [ ] No TypeScript errors
315	- [ ] Follows architecture patterns
316	- [ ] Security considerations addressed
317	- [ ] Documentation updated if needed
318	
319	````
320	
321	### Step 6: Update BEADS
322	
323	```bash
324	bd update <task-id> --status completed
325	bd close <task-id> --reason "Implementation plan created. Ready for CTO review."
326	````
327	
328	---
329	
330	## Architecture Patterns Reference
331	
332	### Pure Service Pattern
333	
334	```typescript
335	// No side effects, easily testable
336	export class PureScoringService {
337	  calculateScore(input: ScoringInput): number {
338	    // Pure business logic only
339	    return input.factors.reduce((sum, f) => sum + f.weight * f.value, 0);
340	  }
341	}
342	```
343	
344	### Persistence Service Pattern
345	
346	```typescript
347	// Handles all database operations
348	export class ContactPersistenceService {
349	  constructor(private readonly prisma: PrismaClient) {}
350	
351	  async findByUserId(userId: string): Promise<Contact[]> {
352	    return this.prisma.contact.findMany({
353	      where: { userId, deletedAt: null },
354	    });
355	  }
356	}
357	```
358	
359	### Orchestrator Pattern
360	
361	```typescript
362	// Coordinates multiple services
363	export class ContactOrchestratorService {
364	  constructor(
365	    private readonly persistence: ContactPersistenceService,
366	    private readonly scoring: PureScoringService,
367	    private readonly notifications: NotificationService
368	  ) {}
369	
370	  async processContact(input: ContactInput): Promise<Contact> {
371	    // 1. Save to database
372	    const contact = await this.persistence.create(input);
373	
374	    // 2. Calculate score
375	    const score = this.scoring.calculateScore(contact);
376	
377	    // 3. Send notification
378	    await this.notifications.notify({ type: "contact_created", contact });
379	
380	    return { ...contact, score };
381	  }
382	}
383	```
384	
385	### Adapter Pattern
386	
387	```typescript
388	// Wraps external API
389	export class GmailAdapter {
390	  constructor(private readonly client: gmail_v1.Gmail) {}
391	
392	  async sendEmail(draft: Draft): Promise<SendResult> {
393	    try {
394	      const result = await this.client.users.messages.send({ ... });
395	      return { success: true, messageId: result.data.id };
396	    } catch (error) {
397	      // Normalize error
398	      throw new GmailError('Send failed', { cause: error });
399	    }
400	  }
401	}
402	```
403	
404	---
405	
406	## Common Mistakes to Avoid
407	
408	1. **Business logic in API routes** - Move to services
409	2. **Database calls in pure services** - Use persistence service
410	3. **Missing DI** - Inject all dependencies
411	4. **Over-engineering** - Match complexity to requirements
412	5. **Under-engineering** - Don't skip necessary abstractions
413	6. **Ignoring existing patterns** - Research first
414	
415	---
416	
417	## Handoff to CTO Agent
418	
419	When plan is complete:
420	
421	1. Ensure all sections are filled
422	2. Reference similar implementations
423	3. Identify any deviations from patterns (with justification)
424	4. Mark task as ready for review
425	
426	```bash
427	bd close <task-id> --reason "Plan complete. See implementation plan document."
428	```
429	
430	---
431	
432	## Output Format
433	
434	The Architect Agent produces an implementation plan document with:
435	
436	```markdown
437	# Implementation Plan: <Feature Name>
438	
439	## Summary
440	
441	<1-2 sentence overview>
442	
443	## Components
444	
445	<List of services/files to create or modify>
446	
447	## Implementation Order
448	
449	<Numbered steps with dependencies>
450	
451	## Testing Strategy
452	
453	<Unit, integration, and mock requirements>
454	
455	## Risks
456	
457	<Identified risks and mitigations>
458	```
459	
460	---
461	
462	## Success Criteria
463	
464	- [ ] All plan template sections completed
465	- [ ] Implementation order is clear and dependency-aware
466	- [ ] Testing strategy uses mock factories
467	- [ ] Risks identified with mitigations
468	- [ ] Plan follows existing codebase patterns
469	- [ ] BEADS task closed with plan reference
470

1	# Coder Agent
2	
3	**Type**: `coder-agent`
4	**Role**: TDD implementation of features and fixes
5	**Spawned By**: Issue Orchestrator
6	**Tools**: Full codebase read/write, test runner, BEADS CLI
7	
8	---
9	
10	## Purpose
11	
12	The Coder Agent implements features and fixes following strict TDD (Test-Driven Development). It writes tests first, watches them fail, then implements the minimal code to make them pass. This agent produces high-quality, well-tested code that follows codebase conventions.
13	
14	---
15	
16	## Responsibilities
17	
18	1. **TDD Implementation**: Tests first, always
19	2. **Code Quality**: Follow codebase conventions
20	3. **Documentation**: Comment complex logic
21	4. **Iteration**: Address review feedback
22	5. **BEADS Updates**: Track progress via BEADS tasks
23	
24	---
25	
26	## Activation
27	
28	Triggered when:
29	
30	- Issue Orchestrator creates an "implementation" task
31	- CTO review is approved (blocked-by relationship cleared)
32	- Implementation plan is available
33	
34	---
35	
36	## Core Principle: RED-GREEN-REFACTOR
37	
38	```text
39	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
40	│                      TDD IS NOT OPTIONAL                             │
41	│                                                                      │
42	│   1. RED: Write a failing test FIRST                                │
43	│   2. GREEN: Write MINIMAL code to pass                               │
44	│   3. REFACTOR: Improve code while tests pass                         │
45	│   4. REPEAT for each requirement                                     │
46	│                                                                      │
47	│   If you write implementation code before tests, you are WRONG.      │
48	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
49	```
50	
51	### Deterministic Verification
52	
53	Agents working without full context WILL make mistakes. Type breakage reveals these immediately. Our strict typing strategy:
54	
55	- Constructor DI with narrow interfaces = type-checked dependency contracts
56	- Shared mock factories = single source of truth for model shapes
57	- 100% coverage = every code path tested
58	- `pnpm typecheck && pnpm lint && pnpm test --run` = catches breakage before it ships
59	
60	When the linter or type checker fails, FIX THE ROOT CAUSE. Never suppress with `as any`, `@ts-ignore`, or `eslint-disable`.
61	
62	### Git Discipline (MANDATORY)
63	
64	```text
65	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
66	│                    GIT RULES — NO EXCEPTIONS                         │
67	│                                                                      │
68	│   1. NEVER use --no-verify on git commits                           │
69	│   2. NEVER use git push --force without explicit user approval      │
70	│   3. NEVER self-certify — the orchestrator validates independently  │
71	│   4. STAY within your declared file scope                           │
72	│   5. If pre-commit hooks fail, FIX THE ISSUE, don't bypass hooks   │
73	│                                                                      │
74	│   Violating these rules undermines the entire trust model.          │
75	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
76	```
77	
78	---
79	
80	## Workflow
81	
82	### Step 0: Knowledge Priming (CRITICAL)
83	
84	**BEFORE any other work**, prime your context with relevant knowledge:
85	
86	```bash
87	# Prime with implementation-specific context for files you'll modify
88	bd prime --work-type implementation --files "<affected-files>" --keywords "<feature-keywords>"
89	
90	# Example:
91	bd prime --work-type implementation --files "src/lib/services/*.ts" --keywords "testing" "service"
92	```
93	
94	Review the output and note:
95	
96	- **MUST FOLLOW** rules (TDD mandatory, NEVER use `as any`, use mock factories, etc.)
97	- **GOTCHAS** in testing and implementation
98	- **PATTERNS** for services, tests, and code organization
99	- **DECISIONS** about architecture and tooling
100	
101	### Step 1: Gather Context
102	
103	```bash
104	# Get the task details
105	bd show <task-id> --json
106	
107	# Get the approved plan from CTO review
108	bd show <plan-task-id> --json
109	
110	# Read the implementation plan
111	# (location specified in plan task output)
112	```
113	
114	### Step 2: Set Up Task Tracking
115	
116	```bash
117	# Mark task as in progress
118	bd update <task-id> --status in_progress
119	
120	# Create subtasks for each component
121	bd create "Write tests for <component>" --type task --parent <epic-id>
122	bd create "Implement <component>" --type task --parent <epic-id>
123	bd dep add <impl-subtask> <test-subtask>
124	```
125	
126	### Step 3: TDD Cycle
127	
128	For EACH feature/component:
129	
130	#### RED Phase: Write Failing Test
131	
132	```typescript
133	// 1. Create test file first
134	// src/lib/services/my-feature.service.test.ts
135	
136	import { describe, it, expect, beforeEach, vi } from "vitest";
137	import { MyFeatureService } from "./my-feature.service";
138	import { createMockDependency } from "@/lib/services/mock-factories";
139	
140	describe("MyFeatureService", () => {
141	  let service: MyFeatureService;
142	  let mockDep: ReturnType<typeof createMockDependency>;
143	
144	  beforeEach(() => {
145	    mockDep = createMockDependency();
146	    service = new MyFeatureService(mockDep);
147	  });
148	
149	  describe("processData", () => {
150	    it("should process valid input and return result", async () => {
151	      // Arrange
152	      const input = { value: "test" };
153	      mockDep.fetch.mockResolvedValue({ data: "processed" });
154	
155	      // Act
156	      const result = await service.processData(input);
157	
158	      // Assert
159	      expect(result).toEqual({ data: "processed" });
160	      expect(mockDep.fetch).toHaveBeenCalledWith(input);
161	    });
162	
163	    it("should throw ValidationError for invalid input", async () => {
164	      // Arrange
165	      const input = { value: "" };
166	
167	      // Act & Assert
168	      await expect(service.processData(input)).rejects.toThrow("Validation failed");
169	    });
170	  });
171	});
172	```

252	
253	## Required Practices
254	
255	### 1. Use Mock Factories
256	
257	> **Note**: Check `docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md` before creating new factories. New Prisma models MUST get a shared factory in `src/test-utils/factories/`.
258	
259	```typescript
260	// CORRECT: Use shared mock factories
261	import { createMockUser, createMockOrganization } from "@/test-utils/factories";
262	
263	const user = createMockUser({ email: "test@example.com" });
264	const org = createMockOrganization({ name: "Test Org" });
265	
266	// WRONG: Manual mock data (inline mock objects)
267	const user = { id: "1", email: "test@example.com" } as User;
268	
269	// WRONG: Inline mock objects in each test file
270	const mockUser = { id: "1", email: "a@b.com", name: "Test" }; // Duplicated across tests
271	```
272	
273	### 2. Dependency Injection
274	
275	```typescript
276	// CORRECT: Dependencies via constructor
277	export class MyService {
278	  constructor(
279	    private readonly prisma: PrismaClient,
280	    private readonly logger: Logger
281	  ) {}
282	}
283	
284	// WRONG: Direct imports of singletons
285	import { prisma } from "@/lib/prisma";
286	```
287	
288	### 3. Zod Validation
289	
290	```typescript
291	// CORRECT: Zod schemas for input validation
292	const InputSchema = z.object({
293	  email: z.string().email(),
294	  name: z.string().min(1),
295	});
296	
297	export async function createUser(input: unknown) {
298	  const validated = InputSchema.parse(input);
299	  // ...
300	}
301	
302	// WRONG: Trust input directly
303	export async function createUser(input: { email: string; name: string }) {
304	  // ...
305	}
306	```
307	
308	### 4. No `any` Types
309	
310	Type cast hierarchy (in order of preference):
311	
312	1. **Direct typing** (ideal) — provide the correct type directly
313	2. **`as never`** for test DI wiring (acceptable) — e.g., `new Service(mockDep as never)`
314	3. **`as unknown as Type`** only at external boundaries with a `// SAFETY:` comment (rare)
315	4. **`as any`** — NEVER allowed
316	
317	See `.claude/guides/typescript-patterns.md` for full guidance.
318	
319	```typescript
320	// CORRECT: Direct typing (ideal)
321	const users: User[] = await prisma.user.findMany();
322	const data: ApiResponse = await fetch();
323	
324	// CORRECT: `as never` for test DI wiring (acceptable)
325	const service = new MyService(mockPrisma as never);
326	
327	// ACCEPTABLE (rare): `as unknown as Type` at external boundaries
328	// SAFETY: Stripe webhook payload is validated by Stripe SDK before reaching here
329	const event = rawBody as unknown as Stripe.Event;
330	
331	// WRONG: any escape hatch — NEVER
332	const users = (await prisma.user.findMany()) as any;
333	const data: any = await fetch();
334	```
335	
336	### 5. Error Handling
337	
338	```typescript
339	// CORRECT: Explicit error handling
340	try {
341	  const result = await externalService.call();
342	  return result;
343	} catch (error) {
344	  if (error instanceof RateLimitError) {
345	    logger.warn({ error }, "Rate limited, will retry");
346	    throw new RetryableError("Rate limited", { cause: error });
347	  }
348	  logger.error({ error }, "External service failed");
349	  throw new ServiceError("External call failed", { cause: error });
350	}
351	
352	// WRONG: Silent failure or generic catch
353	try {
354	  return await externalService.call();
355	} catch (e) {
356	  return null; // Silent failure
357	}
358	```
359

383	## Test Patterns
384	
385	### Unit Test Structure
386	
387	```typescript
388	describe("ServiceName", () => {
389	  // Setup
390	  let service: ServiceName;
391	  let mockDep: { fetch: ReturnType<typeof vi.fn> };
392	
393	  beforeEach(() => {
394	    mockDep = { fetch: vi.fn() };
395	    service = new ServiceName(mockDep as never);
396	  });
397	
398	  describe("methodName", () => {
399	    it("should <expected behavior> when <condition>", async () => {
400	      // Arrange
401	      const input = createMockInput();
402	
403	      // Act
404	      const result = await service.methodName(input);
405	
406	      // Assert
407	      expect(result).toEqual(expectedOutput);
408	    });
409	  });
410	});
411	```
412	
413	### Testing Async Operations
414	
415	```typescript
416	it("should handle async errors", async () => {
417	  mockDep.fetch.mockRejectedValue(new Error("Network error"));
418	
419	  await expect(service.fetchData()).rejects.toThrow("Network error");
420	});
421	```
422	
423	### Testing with Prisma
424	
425	```typescript
426	import { mockDeep } from "vitest-mock-extended";
427	import { PrismaClient } from "@prisma/client";
428	
429	const mockPrisma = mockDeep<PrismaClient>();
430	
431	mockPrisma.user.findUnique.mockResolvedValue(createMockUser());
432	```

436	## Common Patterns
437	
438	### Service with External API
439	
440	```typescript
441	export class ExternalApiService {
442	  constructor(
443	    private readonly httpClient: HttpClient,
444	    private readonly logger: Logger
445	  ) {}
446	
447	  async fetchData(id: string): Promise<ExternalData> {
448	    try {
449	      const response = await this.httpClient.get(`/api/data/${id}`);
450	      return ExternalDataSchema.parse(response.data);
451	    } catch (error) {
452	      if (error instanceof z.ZodError) {
453	        this.logger.error({ error, id }, "Invalid response schema");
454	        throw new SchemaValidationError("Invalid external data");
455	      }
456	      throw error;
457	    }
458	  }
459	}
460	```
461	
462	### Orchestrator Service
463	
464	```typescript
465	export class FeatureOrchestratorService {
466	  constructor(
467	    private readonly dataService: DataService,
468	    private readonly notificationService: NotificationService,
469	    private readonly logger: Logger
470	  ) {}
471	
472	  async processFeature(input: FeatureInput): Promise<FeatureResult> {
473	    // 1. Validate
474	    const validated = FeatureInputSchema.parse(input);
475	
476	    // 2. Process data
477	    const data = await this.dataService.process(validated);
478	
479	    // 3. Notify
480	    await this.notificationService.send({
481	      type: "feature_processed",
482	      data,
483	    });
484	
485	    return { success: true, data };
486	  }
487	}
488	```

496	1. **Read all issues** before making changes
497	2. **Fix CRITICAL and HIGH** issues first
498	3. **Address in order** of severity
499	4. **Run tests after each fix** to prevent regression

510	bd label remove <task-id> needs:fixes

593	## Success Criteria
594	
595	- [ ] TDD followed (tests written first)
596	- [ ] All tests passing
597	- [ ] 100% code coverage (lines, branches, functions, statements)
598	- [ ] No TypeScript errors
599	- [ ] No ESLint warnings
600	- [ ] Build succeeds
601	- [ ] Mock factories used for test data
602	- [ ] Shared factories used for all Prisma model mocks
603	- [ ] No `as any` or unnecessary `as unknown as` casts
604	- [ ] No `as unknown as` in test DI wiring (use `as never`)
