476	Write `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` with the following structure. Use the Write tool.
477	
478	```json
479	{
480	  "metaswarm_version": "0.7.0",
481	  "installed_at": "{current ISO 8601 timestamp}",
482	  "updated_at": "{current ISO 8601 timestamp}",
483	  "detection": {
484	    "language": "{detected language, e.g., 'typescript'}",
485	    "framework": "{detected framework or null}",
486	    "test_runner": "{detected test runner, e.g., 'vitest'}",
487	    "linter": "{detected linter or null}",
488	    "formatter": "{detected formatter or null}",
489	    "package_manager": "{detected package manager or null}",
490	    "type_checker": "{detected type checker or null}",
491	    "ci": "{detected CI system or null}",
492	    "git_hooks": "{detected hook system or null}"
493	  },
494	  "choices": {
495	    "coverage_threshold": {chosen threshold number, e.g., 100},
496	    "external_tools": {true or false},
497	    "visual_review": {true or false},
498	    "ci_pipeline": {true or false, only if CI question was asked},
499	    "git_hooks": {true or false, only if hooks question was asked},
500	    "beads": {true or false, only if BEADS question was asked}
501	  },
502	  "commands": {
503	    "test": "{resolved test command}",
504	    "coverage": "{resolved coverage command}",
505	    "lint": "{resolved lint command or null}",
506	    "typecheck": "{resolved typecheck command or null}",
507	    "format_check": "{resolved format check command or null}"
508	  }
509	}
510	```
511	
512	Fill in ALL values based on detection results and user answers. Use `null` for anything not detected or not applicable.
513	
514	For the `commands` section, derive the correct commands:
515	
516	| Tool | Command |
517	|---|---|
518	| eslint (pnpm) | `pnpm eslint .` |
519	| eslint (npm) | `npx eslint .` |
520	| biome (pnpm) | `pnpm biome check .` |
521	| ruff | `ruff check .` |
522	| golangci-lint | `golangci-lint run` |
523	| tsc (pnpm) | `pnpm tsc --noEmit` |
524	| tsc (npm) | `npx tsc --noEmit` |
525	| mypy | `mypy .` |
526	| pyright | `pyright` |
527	| prettier (pnpm) | `pnpm prettier --check .` |
528	| prettier (npm) | `npx prettier --check .` |
529	| biome format | `pnpm biome check --formatter-enabled=true .` |
530	| black | `black --check .` |
531	| ruff format | `ruff format --check .` |
532	
533	---
534	
535	## Phase 6: Verify & Summary
536	
537	### 6.1 — Health Checks
538	
539	If external tools were enabled, run health checks for each enabled tool:
540	
541	```bash
542	# For Codex
543	codex --version 2>/dev/null && echo "Codex: OK" || echo "Codex: NOT AVAILABLE"
544	
545	# For Gemini
546	gemini --version 2>/dev/null && echo "Gemini: OK" || echo "Gemini: NOT AVAILABLE"
547	```
548	
549	Report any tools that failed health checks and suggest remediation.
550	
551	### 6.2 — Final Summary
552	
553	Present a comprehensive summary to the user:
554	
555	```
556	Setup complete! Here's what was configured:
557	
558	  Project:         {project name from package.json or directory name}
559	  Language:        {language}
560	  Framework:       {framework or "None"}
561	  Test runner:     {test_runner} → `{test command}`
562	  Coverage:        {threshold}% → `{coverage command}`
563	  Linter:          {linter or "None"}
564	  Formatter:       {formatter or "None"}
565	  Type checker:    {type_checker or "None"}
566	  CI:              {ci or "None"}
567	  Git hooks:       {hooks or "None"}
568	  External tools:  {Enabled/Disabled}
569	  Visual review:   {Enabled/Disabled}
570	  BEADS:           {Enabled/Disabled}
571	
572	Files modified:
573	  - CLAUDE.md (test commands, code quality section)
574	  - .coverage-thresholds.json (enforcement command, thresholds)
575	  - .gitignore (language-specific entries)
576	  - .metaswarm/project-profile.json (created)
577	  {list any other files modified}
578	
579	You're all set! Try `/start-task` on a small task to see metaswarm in action.
580	```
581	
582	### 6.3 — Post-Setup Tips
583	
584	Based on what was configured, offer 1-2 relevant tips:
585	
586	- If external tools enabled: "Use `/external-tools-health` to check tool status anytime."
587	- If BEADS enabled: "Use `/prime` before starting work to load relevant knowledge."
588	- If no CI was set up: "Consider adding CI later — metaswarm includes a GitHub Actions template in `templates/ci.yml`."
589	- If visual review enabled: "The visual review skill can screenshot your app during development. Agents will use it automatically."
590	
591	---
592	
593	## Error Handling
594	
595	Throughout the setup process:
596	
597	- If any Bash command fails, report the error clearly and offer to skip that step or retry
598	- If a file can't be read (permissions, missing), note it and continue with other detection
599	- If AskUserQuestion times out or is dismissed, use sensible defaults (100% coverage, no external tools, no visual review)
600	- Never leave the project in a half-configured state — if setup is interrupted, the user should be able to re-run `/metaswarm-setup` to pick up where they left off (the Phase 1 re-setup check handles this)
601

1	# Update Metaswarm
2	
3	Update metaswarm to the latest version, refresh component files, and re-detect project context.
4	
5	## Usage
6	
7	```text
8	/metaswarm-update-version
9	```
10	
11	## Steps
12	
13	### 1. Check Current Version
14	
15	- Read `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` and extract `metaswarm_version`
16	- If the file doesn't exist, stop and tell the user:
17	  > No project profile found. Run `/metaswarm-setup` first to initialize metaswarm.
18	- Display the current version to the user
19	
20	### 2. Fetch Latest Version
21	
22	- Run `npm view metaswarm version` to get the latest published version
23	- Compare current vs. latest:
24	  - **If current == latest**: tell the user they're already up to date and stop
25	  - **If current < latest**: continue to the next step
26	
27	### 3. Show What's New
28	
29	- Run `npx metaswarm@latest changelog --from <current-version>` to show changes between versions
30	- If that fails, try `npx metaswarm@latest changelog` and filter entries after the current version
31	- Highlight any **BREAKING CHANGES** prominently if present
32	- Show a summary of new features, fixes, and new components
33	
34	### 4. Update Files
35	
36	- Run `npx metaswarm@latest install` to refresh all component files
37	- This uses skip-if-exists semantics — user customizations in CLAUDE.md, agents, etc. are preserved
38	- New files (new skills, commands, agents, guides) will be added
39	- Existing metaswarm-shipped files will be refreshed to latest versions
40	
41	### 5. Re-detect Project Context
42	
43	- Re-run project detection from the setup flow:
44	  - Language, framework, test runner, linter, formatter, package manager, type checker, CI, git hooks
45	- Compare new detection results against `detection` in the existing project profile
46	- If changes are detected (e.g., project switched from Jest to Vitest):
47	  - Show what changed
48	  - Ask user if they want to re-customize affected files (e.g., update coverage commands in CLAUDE.md)
49	  - If yes, apply the same customization logic from `/metaswarm-setup`
50	
51	### 6. Update Project Profile
52	
53	- Update `.metaswarm/project-profile.json`:
54	  - `metaswarm_version` → set to new version
55	  - `updated_at` → set to current ISO 8601 timestamp
56	  - `detection` → refresh if project context changed (preserve if unchanged)
57	- Preserve all other fields (`installed_at`, `choices`, `commands`, etc.)
58	
59	### 7. Summary
60	
61	- Print what was updated:
62	  - Version change (e.g., `0.6.0 → 0.7.0`)
63	  - Number of files refreshed or added
64	  - Any new skills, commands, or agents that were added
65	  - Any project detection changes
66	- If there were breaking changes, remind the user to review them
67	- Suggest reviewing the changelog for new features to take advantage of
68

1	---
2	description: Monitor a PR through to merge - handles CI failures, reviews, and thread resolution
3	---
4	
5	# PR Shepherd
6	
7	Monitor a PR from creation through merge, handling CI failures, review comments, and thread resolution automatically.
8	
9	## Usage
10	
11	```text
12	/pr-shepherd [pr-number]
13	```
14	
15	If no PR number is provided, uses the PR associated with the current branch.
16	
17	## What This Does
18	
19	1. **Monitors CI/CD** - Polls every 60 seconds for status changes
20	2. **Monitors Reviews** - Watches for new comments and unresolved threads
21	3. **Auto-fixes simple issues** - Lint, prettier, type errors
22	4. **Asks before complex fixes** - Presents options with pros/cons for approval
23	5. **Handles review comments** - Delegates to `handling-pr-comments` skill
24	6. **Checkpoints at 4 hours** - Asks if you want to continue or handoff
25	
26	## Steps
27	
28	1. **Get PR information**:
29	
30	   ```bash
31	   PR_NUMBER=${1:-$(gh pr view --json number -q .number 2>/dev/null)}
32	   if [ -z "$PR_NUMBER" ]; then
33	     echo "No PR found. Provide PR number: /pr-shepherd 123"
34	     exit 1
35	   fi
36	   ```
37	
38	2. **Activate the pr-shepherd skill**:
39	   Load and follow the pr-shepherd skill definition.
40	
41	3. **Begin monitoring loop** (background, every 60s):
42	   - Check CI status
43	   - Check for new review comments
44	   - Check unresolved thread count
45	   - Take action based on state machine
46	
47	4. **Handle issues as they arise**:
48	   - Simple CI failures -> auto-fix with TDD
49	   - Complex failures -> present options, wait for approval
50	   - New comments -> invoke `handling-pr-comments` skill
51	
52	5. **Exit when done**:
53	   - All CI green AND all threads resolved -> report success
54	   - 4-hour timeout -> checkpoint with user
55	
56	## Example
57	
58	```text
59	/pr-shepherd 695
60	
61	> I'm using the pr-shepherd skill to monitor PR #695 through to merge.
62	> I'll watch CI/CD, handle review comments, and fix issues as they arise.
63	>
64	> Current status:
65	> - CI: Running (2/5 checks complete)
66	> - Threads: 0 unresolved
67	>
68	> Monitoring... (will check every 60 seconds)
69	```
70	
71	## Notes
72	
73	- The agent stays active until the PR is ready to merge or you stop it
74	- All code changes use TDD process
75	- Complex issues always get user approval before fixing
76	- Uses `handling-pr-comments` skill for review comment handling
77

2	description: Load relevant knowledge base facts into context before starting work
3	---
4	
5	# BEADS Prime
6	
7	**CRITICAL**: Run this command at the START of any investigation, planning, or implementation work to load relevant knowledge into your context.
8	
9	## When to Use
10	
11	- Starting work on a GitHub Issue
12	- Beginning investigation/research
13	- Before writing a plan
14	- Before implementing changes
15	- When switching to a new area of the codebase
16	
17	## How It Works
18	
19	This command queries the BEADS knowledge base for facts relevant to your current context and injects them into the conversation, ensuring you:
20	
21	1. Follow established patterns and rules
22	2. Avoid known gotchas and pitfalls
23	3. Make decisions aligned with architectural choices
24	4. Don't repeat mistakes that have been learned from
25	
26	## Usage
27	
28	### Quick Prime (Most Common)
29	
30	For general context with automatic detection:
31	
32	```bash
33	bd prime
34	```
35	
36	### Prime for Specific Files
37	
38	When working on specific files:
39	
40	```bash
41	bd prime --files "src/lib/services/**/*.ts" "src/api/routes/**/*.ts"
42	```
43	
44	### Prime for Keywords
45	
46	When working on a specific topic:
47	
48	```bash
49	bd prime --keywords "authentication" "jwt" "security"
50	```
51	
52	### Prime for Work Type
53	
54	When doing a specific type of work:
55	
56	```bash
57	bd prime --work-type planning
58	bd prime --work-type implementation
59	bd prime --work-type review
60	bd prime --work-type debugging
61	```
62	
63	### Prime for Context Recovery
64	
65	When resuming after context compaction or in a new session:
66	
67	```bash
68	bd prime --work-type recovery
69	```
70	
71	This loads active plan, project context, execution state, and relevant knowledge base facts. See "Context Recovery" section below.
72	
73	### Combined
74	
75	```bash
76	bd prime \
77	  --files "src/lib/services/ai-*.ts" \
78	  --keywords "ai" "provider" "openai" \
79	  --work-type implementation
80	```
81	
82	## What Gets Loaded
83	
84	### 1. MUST FOLLOW (Critical Rules)
85	
86	Non-negotiable rules containing NEVER/ALWAYS/MUST:
87	
88	- "NEVER use `as any` type casting"
89	- "ALWAYS use centralized AI config"
90	- Security-critical patterns
91	
92	### 2. GOTCHAS (Common Pitfalls)
93	
94	Known issues to avoid:
95	
96	- "Truthy check fails for explicit zero values - use !== undefined"
97	- API behavior quirks
98	
99	### 3. PATTERNS (Best Practices)
100	
101	Established patterns in this codebase:
102	
103	- "Use mock factories from test utilities"
104	- "Services should follow TDD (Red-Green-Refactor)"
105	
106	### 4. DECISIONS (Architectural Choices)
107	
108	Team/architectural decisions:
109	
110	- "State management uses Zustand + TanStack Query"
111	- "AI providers implement Strategy Pattern"
112	
113	### 5. API BEHAVIORS
114	
115	External API quirks:
116	
117	- "Prisma findMany returns [] not null"
118	
119	## Auto-Priming
120	
121	The BEADS system should auto-prime in these scenarios:
122	
123	1. **Session Start**: When `.beads/` directory is detected
124	2. **File Touch**: When reading/editing files that match knowledge patterns
125	3. **Keyword Detection**: When task description matches known topics
126	
127	## Integration Points
128	
129	### In Planning Phase
130	
131	Before writing a plan, run:
132	
133	```bash
134	bd prime --work-type planning --keywords "<task-keywords>"
135	```
136	
137	### In Implementation Phase
138	
139	Before writing code, run:
140	
141	```bash
142	bd prime --files "<files-to-touch>" --work-type implementation
143	```
144	
145	### In Review Phase
146	
147	Before reviewing code, run:
148	
149	```bash
150	bd prime --work-type review --files "<files-changed>"
151	```
152	
153	## Manual Knowledge Check
154	
155	If you need to check for specific knowledge:
156	
157	```bash
158	# Search for specific topic
159	cat .beads/knowledge/*.jsonl | jq -r 'select(.fact | test("authentication"; "i")) | .fact'
160	
161	# Get all gotchas
162	cat .beads/knowledge/gotchas.jsonl | jq -r '.fact'
163	
164	# Get all patterns
165	cat .beads/knowledge/patterns.jsonl | jq -r '.fact'
166	```
167	
168	## Output Format
169	
170	The prime command outputs formatted knowledge that looks like:
171	
172	```markdown
173	# Relevant Knowledge Base Facts
174	
175	_25 facts loaded for this context_
176	
177	## MUST FOLLOW (Critical Rules)
178	
179	These are non-negotiable rules:
180	
181	- **[pattern]** NEVER use `as any` type casting...
182	- **[security]** Always validate JWT tokens server-side...
183	
184	## GOTCHAS (Common Pitfalls)
185	
186	Avoid these known issues:
187	
188	- **[gotcha]** Truthy check fails for explicit zero values...
189	
190	## PATTERNS (Best Practices)
191	
192	- **[pattern]** Use mock factories from test utilities...
193	```
194	
195	## Context Recovery
196	
197	When `--work-type recovery` is used (or when an orchestrator detects it lost context), the prime command loads additional context beyond the standard knowledge base:
198	
199	### What Gets Loaded
200	
201	1. **Active Plan** — reads `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` if it exists with `status: in-progress`
202	2. **Project Context** — reads `.beads/context/project-context.md` (completed work units, established patterns, tooling)
203	3. **Execution State** — reads `.beads/context/execution-state.md` (current work unit, phase, retry count)
204	4. **Standard Knowledge** — all the usual MUST FOLLOW, GOTCHAS, PATTERNS, DECISIONS facts
205	
206	### Recovery Flow
207	
208	```bash
209	# 1. Check for active execution
210	if [ -f .beads/plans/active-plan.md ]; then
211	  grep -q 'status: in-progress' .beads/plans/active-plan.md && echo "ACTIVE PLAN FOUND"
212	fi
213	
214	# 2. Load all context files
215	cat .beads/plans/active-plan.md           # The approved plan
216	cat .beads/context/project-context.md     # Completed work, patterns
217	cat .beads/context/execution-state.md     # Where we left off
218	
219	# 3. Load relevant knowledge base facts
220	cat .beads/knowledge/*.jsonl | jq -r '.fact'
221	```
222	
223	### When Recovery Triggers Automatically
224	
225	- Orchestrated execution starts and finds `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` with `status: in-progress` but has no plan in its current context
226	- A new session begins with `bd prime` and active execution state is detected
227	- After context compaction, when the agent recognizes it has lost plan/execution context
228	
229	### Output Format (Recovery Mode)
230	
231	```markdown
232	# Context Recovery
233	
234	_Recovered from BEADS persisted state_
235	
236	## Active Plan
237	<plan summary — title, work unit count, current position>
238	
239	## Execution State
240	- Current work unit: WU-<id> (<title>)
241	- Phase: <IMPLEMENT|VALIDATE|REVIEW|COMMIT>
242	- Completed: <N> of <total> work units
243	
244	## Project Context
245	<tooling, completed work units, established patterns>
246	
247	## Relevant Knowledge
248	<standard priming output: MUST FOLLOW, GOTCHAS, PATTERNS, DECISIONS>
249	```
250	
251	## Verification
252	
253	After priming, you should be able to answer:
254	
255	1. What are the critical rules I must follow?
256	2. What gotchas should I watch out for?
257	3. What patterns should I apply?
258	4. What architectural decisions constrain my options?
259	5. (If recovery mode) Where did execution stop and what comes next?
260

1	# Review Design Command
2	
3	Run the design review gate on a design document to get feedback from Product Manager, Architect, Designer, Security Design, and CTO agents.
4	
5	## Usage
6	
7	```bash
8	/review-design <path-to-design-doc>
9	```
10	
11	## Examples
12	
13	```bash
14	# Review a specific design document
15	/review-design docs/plans/2026-01-11-contact-assistant-design.md
16	
17	# Review the most recent design document
18	/review-design --latest
19	
20	# Re-run review after revisions
21	/review-design docs/plans/2026-01-11-contact-assistant-design.md --iteration 2
22	```
23	
24	## What This Does
25	
26	1. **Validates the design document exists** and is in the expected format
27	2. **Spawns five review agents in parallel**:
28	   - **Product Manager Agent** - Validates use cases and user benefits
29	   - **Architect Agent** - Reviews technical architecture
30	   - **Designer Agent** - Reviews UX, API design, developer experience
31	   - **Security Design Agent** - Reviews security threats and mitigations
32	   - **CTO Agent** - Reviews TDD readiness, codebase alignment
33	3. **Aggregates results** from all five agents
34	4. **Reports outcome** with blockers, suggestions, and next steps
35	
36	## Arguments
37	
38	| Argument              | Description                                                  |
39	| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
40	| `<path>`              | Path to design document (required unless --latest)           |
41	| `--latest`            | Review most recent design doc in docs/plans/                 |
42	| `--iteration N`       | Mark as iteration N of review cycle                          |
43	| `--skip-agent <name>` | Skip specific agent (pm, architect, designer, security, cto) |
44	
45	## Review Verdicts
46	
47	### APPROVED
48	
49	All agents approve. Output includes:
50	
51	- Summary of what each agent reviewed
52	- Non-blocking suggestions for future improvement
53	- Next steps for implementation
54	
55	### NEEDS_REVISION
56	
57	One or more agents found blocking issues. Output includes:
58	
59	- List of blocking issues by agent
60	- Questions requiring clarification
61	- Iteration count (max 3 before escalation)
62	
63	### ESCALATED
64	
65	After 3 iterations without approval:
66	
67	- Summary of remaining blockers
68	- Options: Override / Defer / Cancel
69	
70	## Integration
71	
72	### After Brainstorming
73	
74	This command is automatically invoked by the brainstorming extension skill after `superpowers:brainstorming` commits a design document.
75	
76	### Manual Invocation
77	
78	Use this command to:
79	
80	- Review an existing design document
81	- Re-run reviews after making revisions
82	- Run partial reviews (skip specific agents)
83	
84	### With BEADS
85	
86	When design is approved, offers to:
87	
88	- Create BEADS epic linked to design doc
89	- Create tasks for implementation phases
90	- Set up worktree for isolated development
91	
92	## Agent Details
93	
94	### Product Manager Agent
95	
96	Focuses on:
97	
98	- Use case clarity (WHO/WANTS/SO THAT format)
99	- Measurable user benefits
100	- Scope definition (MVP vs nice-to-have)
101	- Success metrics validation
102	
103	### Architect Agent
104	
105	Focuses on:
106	
107	- Service architecture patterns
108	- Dependency flow
109	- Technical correctness
110	- Integration points
111	
112	### Designer Agent
113	
114	Focuses on:
115	
116	- API/interface design quality
117	- User experience (if applicable)
118	- Developer experience
119	- Pattern consistency
120	
121	### Security Design Agent
122	
123	Focuses on:
124	
125	- Threat modeling (STRIDE)
126	- Authentication/authorization design
127	- Data protection and privacy
128	- OWASP Top 10 compliance
129	
130	### CTO Agent
131	
132	Focuses on:
133	
134	- TDD readiness (RED-GREEN-REFACTOR)
135	- Codebase alignment (CLAUDE.md)
136	- Completeness of specification
137	- Risk assessment
138	
139	## Success Criteria
140	
141	The review gate passes when:
142	
143	- [ ] All five agents return APPROVED
144	- [ ] All blocking issues resolved
145	- [ ] All clarifying questions answered
146	- [ ] Design document updated with revisions (if any)
147	
148	## Troubleshooting
149	
150	### "Design document not found"
151	
152	Ensure the path is correct and the file exists:
153	
154	```bash
155	ls -la docs/plans/
156	```
157	
158	### "Agent timed out"
159	
160	Individual agents have 3-minute timeout. If timeout occurs:
161	
162	1. Check design document isn't too large
163	2. Try running agents sequentially with `--sequential`
164	3. Check for network/API issues
165	
166	### "Stuck in review loop"
167	
168	After 3 iterations, use `--force-override` to proceed anyway (documents technical debt).
169	
170	## Related Commands
171	
172	- `/review-this` - General CTO review (more detailed, single reviewer)
173	- `/create-issue` - Create GitHub issue from approved design
174	- `/start-task` - Begin implementation of approved design
175	
176	## Related Skills
177	
178	- `your-project:design-review-gate` - The gate implementation
179	- `your-project:brainstorming-extension` - Auto-triggers this after brainstorming
180	- `superpowers:brainstorming` - The design creation skill
181

1	---
2	description: Extract learnings from recent PR reviews, conversations, and session patterns to update the knowledge base
3	---
4	
5	# BEADS Self-Reflect
6	
7	You are performing a self-reflection for the BEADS agent swarm. Your job is to analyze PR review comments, conversation history, and session patterns to extract high-quality, reusable learnings.
8	
9	**Philosophy**: Be judicious. Quality over quantity. Each learning should make future development measurably better.
10	
11	## Phase A: PR Comment Analysis
12	
13	### Step 1: Fetch PR Comments
14	
15	```bash
16	GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) npx tsx scripts/beads-fetch-pr-comments.ts --days 7
17	```
18	
19	This outputs PR comments to `.beads/temp/pr-comments.json`.
20	
21	### Step 2: Extract CodeRabbit's Structured Learnings
22	
23	```bash
24	cat .beads/temp/pr-comments.json | jq -r '.comments[].body' | grep -A5 "^Learnt from:" | grep "^Learning:" | sed 's/^Learning: //' | sort -u
25	```
26	
27	### Evaluate Each CodeRabbit Learning
28	
29	**NOT all CodeRabbit learnings are equal.** Evaluate each one:
30	
31	#### ACCEPT (High Value)
32	
33	- **Applies to patterns**: `Applies to **/*.test.ts: ...` - Actionable file-scoped rules
34	- **NEVER/ALWAYS rules**: Clear, enforceable constraints
35	- **Security/Performance**: Critical quality gates
36	- **Gotchas with context**: Explains WHY something is problematic
37	
38	#### REJECT or DEFER (Low Value)
39	
40	- **PR-specific context**: "In PR #X, someone did Y" - Too specific unless the pattern generalizes
41	- **Personality observations**: "Developer provides detailed updates" - Not actionable
42	- **Process descriptions**: Meta, not code
43	- **Duplicate with slight rewording**: Check if we already have this fact
44	- **Obvious/trivial**: Things any developer should know
45	
46	#### TRANSFORM (Medium Value -> Make High Value)
47	
48	- **Before**: "In PR #593, developer explains optional AIProvider methods support backward compatibility"
49	- **After**: "Optional interface methods (generateWithTools?, generateObject?) follow Interface Segregation Principle - add runtime guards before use"
50	
51	### Quality Filter Questions
52	
53	For each potential learning, ask:
54	
55	1. **Would this prevent a bug?** If yes, high priority.
56	2. **Would this save review cycles?** If yes, add it.
57	3. **Is this codebase-specific or universal?** Tag accordingly.
58	4. **Do we already have this?** Check for semantic duplicates.
59	5. **Can an agent act on this?** If not actionable, skip it.
60	6. **Would this confuse a naive agent?** If a future agent without context wouldn't benefit, skip it.
61	
62	## Phase B: Conversation & Session Mining (Optional)
63	
64	Analyze current context window and optionally historical sessions for implicit insights:
65	
66	**Strategic patterns to look for:**
67	
68	| Pattern                       | Usually indicates          | Value     |
69	| ----------------------------- | -------------------------- | --------- |
70	| "The problem was..."          | Debugging insight          | High      |
71	| "It turns out..."             | Discovery moment           | High      |
72	| "We decided to..."            | Architectural decision     | Very High |
73	| "The reason we..."            | Rationale worth preserving | Very High |
74	| "Unlike what you'd expect..." | Non-obvious behavior       | High      |
75	| "Never do X because..."       | Gotcha/pitfall             | High      |
76	
77	**Filter**: Only capture insights that are codebase-specific, non-obvious, actionable, and durable.
78	
79	## Phase C: Config Reflection (Optional)
80	
81	Review Claude instructions and config for improvement opportunities:
82	
83	- `.claude/commands/*` - Are commands still accurate?
84	- `CLAUDE.md` - Any outdated guidance?
85	- `.claude/settings.json` - Missing permissions or tools?
86	
87	Present findings interactively: explain issue -> propose change -> get feedback -> implement.
88	
89	## Step 3: Analyze Comment Patterns
90	
91	Beyond CodeRabbit's structured learnings, look for **recurring patterns** in review comments:
92	
93	```bash
94	cat .beads/temp/pr-comments.json | jq -r '.comments[] | select(.reviewerType == "coderabbit") | .body' | grep -i "nitpick\|issue\|suggestion\|consider\|should\|must\|avoid" | head -50
95	```
96	
97	### Pattern Detection Checklist
98	
99	Look for these patterns across multiple PRs:
100	
101	1. **Repeated corrections**: Same issue flagged in multiple PRs -> Create a learning
102	2. **Architectural feedback**: Comments about service design, dependencies -> Capture the principle
103	3. **Testing feedback**: Mock patterns, test structure issues -> Document the pattern
104	4. **Type safety issues**: Repeated type casting problems -> Create a gotcha
105	5. **Performance concerns**: N+1 queries, memory issues -> Document with context
106	
107	## Step 4: Present Candidates for Approval
108	
109	Present learnings as a numbered list for user selection:
110	
111	```markdown
112	## Candidate Learnings
113	
114	1. **<Brief title>** - <One sentence description>
115	
116	2. **<Brief title>** - <One sentence description>
117	
118	3. **<Brief title>** - <One sentence description>
119	
120	---
121	
122	Which numbers do you want to capture? (all / 1,2,3 / none)
123	```
124	
125	**After user selects**, classify each approved learning interactively:
126	
127	```text
128	**Fact**: [extracted core insight]
129	**Type**: [pattern|gotcha|decision|api_behavior|security|performance]
130	**Applies to**: [file patterns or components]
131	**Confidence**: [high|medium|low]
132	
133	Does this look right? (yes / edit)
134	```
135	
136	### Conflict Resolution
137	
138	When a new learning conflicts with an existing one:
139	
140	| Situation                              | Action                                |
141	| -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
142	| New learning is more specific/accurate | New supersedes old                    |
143	| Both capture different valid aspects   | Keep both, add distinguishing context |
144	| New learning contradicts old           | Ask user which is correct             |
145	| Old learning is a subset of new        | Merge into comprehensive learning     |
146	
147	**The key question**: Would having both learnings confuse a naive agent, or complement each other?
148	
149	## Step 5: Categorize and Store
150	
151	For each validated learning, add it to the appropriate knowledge base JSONL file following the schema in `.beads/knowledge/README.md`.
152	
153	### Type Guide
154	
155	| Type           | When to Use                  | Example                                                 |
156	| -------------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
157	| `pattern`      | Reusable code patterns       | "Use mock factories for test data"                      |
158	| `gotcha`       | Common mistakes/pitfalls     | "Truthy check fails for explicit zero values"           |
159	| `security`     | Security-sensitive patterns  | "Always validate JWT server-side"                       |
160	| `performance`  | Performance implications     | "Use database indexes for frequent queries"             |
161	| `decision`     | Team/architectural decisions | "Prefer Strategy over Template Method for AI providers" |
162	| `api_behavior` | External API quirks          | "Prisma findMany returns [] not null"                   |
163	| `code_quirk`   | Codebase-specific oddities   | "Thread model is for drafts only, not email threads"    |
164	
165	### Canonicalization Rules
166	
167	1. **Remove PR references**: "In PR #593..." -> Remove, keep the principle
168	2. **Remove names when not relevant**: Keep only if it's a team decision
169	3. **Generalize file paths**: `src/lib/services/foo.ts` -> `src/lib/services/**/*.ts`
170	4. **Use imperative mood**: "Consider using..." -> "Use..."
171	5. **Include the WHY**: "Use X" -> "Use X because Y"
172	6. **Keep under 200 chars** when possible
173	
174	### Confidence Assessment
175	
176	| Level    | Criteria                                                                       |
177	| -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
178	| `high`   | Explicit rules (NEVER/ALWAYS), security issues, repeated pattern across 3+ PRs |
179	| `medium` | Single PR observation, team preference, architectural guidance                 |
180	| `low`    | Speculative, context-dependent, might not always apply                         |
181	
182	## Step 6: Deduplication Check
183	
184	Before adding, check for semantic duplicates in existing knowledge files.
185	
186	## Step 7: Semantic Summarization via bd compact
187	
188	The standalone beads plugin (v0.63.3+) provides `bd compact` for semantic summarization of closed issues. After capturing learnings, run:
189	
190	```bash
191	bd compact
192	```
193	
194	This replaces the former `beads-self-reflect.ts` script — `bd compact` handles knowledge base statistics, summarization, and cleanup natively.
195	
196	## Step 8: Generate Report
197	
198	```markdown
199	## Self-Reflection Results
200	
201	### Summary
202	
203	- PRs Analyzed: X
204	- Comments Reviewed: Y
205	- CodeRabbit Learnings Evaluated: Z
206	  - Accepted: A
207	  - Rejected (low value): B
208	  - Transformed: C
209	- Conversation/Session Learnings: W
210	- Total New Facts Added: N
211	
212	### Learnings Added
213	
214	#### High-Value Learnings
215	
216	1. **[type]** <description>
217	   - Why accepted: <reason>
218	
219	#### Rejected/Deferred
220	
221	1. "<learning>" - <reason for rejection>
222	
223	### Knowledge Base Statistics
224	
225	- Total facts: N
226	- By type: pattern (X), decision (Y), gotcha (Z)...
227	- Quality: high (A), medium (B), low (C)
228	```
229	
230	## Anti-Patterns to Avoid
231	
232	1. **Quantity over quality**: Don't add 100 mediocre facts; add 10 great ones
233	2. **Blindly trusting CodeRabbit**: Evaluate each learning critically
234	3. **Ignoring duplicates**: Check before adding
235	4. **Missing the WHY**: Facts without reasoning are less useful
236	5. **Over-specificity**: "Use X in file Y" -> "Use X in files matching pattern"
237	6. **Under-specificity**: "Write good tests" -> Not actionable, skip it
238

1	# Setup
2	
3	Interactive project setup for metaswarm. Detects your project, configures metaswarm, and writes project-local files.
4	
5	## Usage
6	
7	```text
8	/setup
9	```
10	
11	## Behavior
12	
13	Invokes the `metaswarm:setup` skill, which:
14	
15	1. Detects your project's language, framework, test runner, and tools
16	2. Asks 3-5 configuration questions (coverage threshold, external tools, CI, etc.)
17	3. Writes project-local files (CLAUDE.md, coverage config, knowledge base, scripts)
18	4. Creates command shims for high-frequency commands
19	5. Generates `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` with your configuration
20	
21	This replaces the old `npx metaswarm init` workflow. No Node.js required.
22	
23	## Related
24	
25	- `/metaswarm:status` — check current setup state
26	- `/metaswarm:migrate` — migrate from npm-installed metaswarm
27

1	# Start Task
2	
3	Determine task complexity and use appropriate workflow for efficient development.
4	
5	## Usage
6	
7	```text
8	/start-task <task-description>
9	```
10	
11	## Steps
12	
13	### 0. Pre-Task Checklist
14	
15	Before starting any new task:
16	
17	**Context Recovery Check**:
18	
19	- [ ] Check if `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` exists with `status: in-progress`
20	- [ ] If YES: An interrupted execution exists. Ask user: "There's an active plan from a previous session. Resume it or start fresh?"
21	  - Resume → run `bd prime --work-type recovery` and pick up where execution stopped
22	  - Start fresh → mark the old plan as `status: abandoned` and proceed normally
23	
24	**Knowledge Priming (CRITICAL)**:
25	
26	- [ ] Run BEADS prime: `bd prime --keywords "<task-keywords>" --work-type planning`
27	- [ ] Review MUST FOLLOW rules and GOTCHAS before proceeding
28	- [ ] Note any relevant patterns or decisions that constrain the approach
29	
30	**PR Check**:
31	
32	- [ ] Check if there are active PRs with pending comments
33	- [ ] Ask: "Before we start the new task, should we check if there are any PR comments to address?"
34	- [ ] If yes, run: `gh pr list --author @me --state open`
35	- [ ] Check each PR for new CodeRabbit comments
36	
37	### 0.5. External Tools Check (informational only — never blocks the task)
38	
39	Check if external AI tools (Codex, Gemini) are available for cost savings and cross-model review:
40	
41	- Run the health check scripts to detect installed tools:
42	  ```bash
43	  command -v codex >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "codex: available" || echo "codex: not found"
44	  command -v gemini >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "gemini: available" || echo "gemini: not found"
45	  ```
46	- **If tools are detected but `.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml` does not exist**: Suggest the user enable them:
47	  > "External tools (Codex/Gemini) are installed but not configured. Run `mkdir -p .metaswarm && cp templates/external-tools.yaml .metaswarm/` to enable cost-saving delegation."
48	- **If no tools are detected**: Briefly mention they can be installed:
49	  > "Optional: Install Codex and Gemini CLIs for cost savings and cross-model review — see `templates/external-tools-setup.md`."
50	- **If tools are configured and working**: No message needed — proceed silently.
51	
52	This check is informational only. Always proceed to the task regardless of the result.
53	
54	### 1. Task Assessment
55	
56	**Use extended thinking** to analyze the task complexity before asking the user.
57	
58	Consider:
59	
60	- Number of files likely to be modified
61	- Whether database changes are needed
62	- Impact on existing functionality
63	- Testing requirements
64	- Integration points
65	
66	Then ask the user to confirm your assessment:
67	
68	> **Proposed complexity**: [Simple / Complex] - Does this match your expectation?
69	
70	**Simple Task (streamlined flow):**
71	
72	- Bug fixes
73	- Small UI tweaks
74	- Minor text/copy changes
75	- Simple configuration updates
76	- Adding basic validation
77	- Fixing linting/test issues
78	
79	**Complex Task (full checklist + BEADS epic):**
80	
81	- New features with database changes
82	- New API endpoints
83	- Complex UI components
84	- Background job modifications
85	- Onboarding flow changes
86	- Multi-file refactoring
87	- Performance optimizations
88	
89	### 1.5. Problem Definition Phase
90	
91	Before implementation, ensure the problem is well-defined:
92	
93	**If a GitHub Issue exists:**
94	
95	```bash
96	# Read the issue
97	gh issue view <number> --json title,body,labels,comments
98	
99	# Extract and verify:
100	# - Clear scope (what's in, what's out)
101	# - Definition of Done items (verifiable acceptance criteria)
102	# - File scope (which files will be affected)
103	# - Human checkpoints (where should we pause for review?)
104	```
105	
106	If the issue lacks DoD items or clear scope, ask the user to clarify before proceeding.
107	
108	**If no GitHub Issue exists:**
109	
110	- For **simple tasks**: No issue needed. Proceed directly.
111	- For **complex tasks**: An issue is ALWAYS needed. Ask the user:
112	  > "This is a complex task. Should I create a GitHub Issue to track it?"
113	
114	**If the problem is unclear:**
115	
116	- Route to `superpowers:brainstorming` first to refine the idea into a design
117	- **MANDATORY HANDOFF**: After brainstorming commits a design document, you MUST:
118	  1. STOP — do NOT proceed directly to `writing-plans` or implementation
119	  2. Run the Design Review Gate (`/review-design` or invoke the `design-review-gate` skill)
120	  3. Wait for all 5 review agents (PM, Architect, Designer, Security, CTO) to APPROVE
121	  4. Only after ALL APPROVED, proceed to planning
122	- This handoff is required even if the brainstorming skill instructs you to invoke `writing-plans` next. The design review gate catches issues before expensive implementation begins.
123	- After design review approval, create a GitHub Issue from the refined design
124	- The issue should contain: scope, DoD items, file scope, human checkpoints
125	
126	**Problem Definition outputs:**
127	
128	- [ ] Clear scope (what's in, what's out)
129	- [ ] DoD items (enumerated, independently verifiable)
130	- [ ] File scope (which files will be affected)
131	- [ ] Human checkpoints (where to pause for review)
132	
133	### 2. Simple Task Flow
134	
135	If user confirms it's a simple task:
136	
137	#### Essential Steps
138	
139	- [ ] Read relevant docs if unfamiliar with area
140	- [ ] Check existing patterns for similar functionality
141	- [ ] Make the change following existing patterns
142	- [ ] Write/update tests if logic changes
143	- [ ] Run tests, lint, and build
144	- [ ] Create simple PR with clear description
145	
146	### 3. Complex Task Flow
147	
148	If it's a complex task:
149	
150	- Create a BEADS epic: `bd create --title "<task>" --type epic --priority 2`
151	- Use the full task completion checklist
152	- Consider breaking into smaller tasks as BEADS sub-issues
153	- Use extended thinking for planning
154	- Create detailed implementation plan
155	
156	#### Orchestrated Execution (for tasks with DoD items)
157	
158	When the task has a spec with Definition of Done items, use the orchestrated execution pattern:
159	
160	1. **Create implementation plan** — decompose into work units with DoD items, file scopes, dependencies
161	2. **Plan Review Gate (BLOCKING)** — submit plan to adversarial review (3 reviewers: Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment must all PASS). This gate is MANDATORY — do NOT present the plan to the user or begin implementation until all 3 reviewers PASS. See `skills/plan-review-gate/SKILL.md`
162	3. **Execute** the 4-phase loop per work unit: IMPLEMENT → VALIDATE → ADVERSARIAL REVIEW → COMMIT
163	4. **Final review** after all work units: cross-unit integration check
164	5. **Self-reflect**: Run `/self-reflect` to extract learnings, then commit knowledge base updates
165	6. **Create PR** — knowledge base changes are included in the PR
166	
167	See the `orchestrated-execution` skill for the full pattern. Key principles:
168	- **Trust nothing, verify everything**: Run quality gates independently, never trust subagent self-reports
169	- **Adversarial review**: Fresh reviewer checks each DoD item with file:line evidence
170	- **Human checkpoints**: Pause at planned review points before continuing
171	- **Recovery**: Max 3 retries per work unit, then escalate with failure history
172	- **No shortcuts**: NEVER use `--no-verify` on commits, NEVER skip coverage gates, NEVER self-certify
173	
174	#### Multi-Agent Orchestration (for large features)
175	
176	For complex tasks requiring multiple phases, consider spawning sub-agents:
177	
178	**Model specialization guidance:**
179	
180	| Model  | Best For                                                  |
181	| ------ | --------------------------------------------------------- |
182	| Opus   | Orchestration, architecture, security analysis, synthesis |
183	| Sonnet | Code analysis, implementation, code review, feature work  |
184	| Haiku  | Metrics collection, simple analysis, data processing      |
185	
186	**Pattern**: Spawn parallel sub-agents for independent work (e.g., code review + security audit), sequential agents for dependent phases (research -> planning -> implementation).
187	
188	### 4. Task Escalation
189	
190	If a "simple task" becomes complex during implementation:
191	
192	- Stop and reassess
193	- Create a BEADS epic: `bd create --title "<task>" --type epic --priority 2`
194	- Switch to full checklist workflow
195	- Inform user of complexity change
196	- Consider breaking into multiple PRs
197

1	# Status
2	
3	Show metaswarm diagnostic information.
4	
5	## Usage
6	
7	```text
8	/status
9	```
10	
11	## Behavior
12	
13	Invokes the `metaswarm:status` skill, which reports:
14	
15	- Installed plugin version
16	- Project setup state
17	- Command shims status
18	- Legacy install detection
19	- BEADS plugin status
20	- External tools configuration
21	- Coverage threshold configuration
22	- Node.js availability
23	
24	Use this to troubleshoot installation or configuration issues.
25	
26	## Related
27	
28	- `/metaswarm:setup` — configure metaswarm for a project
29	- `/metaswarm:migrate` — migrate from npm-installed metaswarm

1	# Update
2	
3	Check for and apply metaswarm updates.
4	
5	## Usage
6	
7	```text
8	/update
9	```
10	
11	## Behavior
12	
13	1. Read current plugin version from `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`
14	2. Check if the marketplace has a newer version available
15	3. If an update is available, guide the user through applying it:
16	   - For marketplace installs: the plugin auto-updates via the marketplace system
17	   - For npm installs: suggest migrating to the plugin (`/metaswarm:migrate`)
18	4. After update, verify all skills load correctly
19	
20	## Notes
21	
22	- Marketplace-installed plugins auto-update when Claude Code syncs plugins
23	- This command is primarily useful for checking the current version and troubleshooting
24	- Replaces the old `/metaswarm-update-version` command

1	# metaswarm for Codex CLI
2	
3	Install metaswarm's 13 orchestration skills for [Codex CLI](https://github.com/openai/codex).
4	
5	## Install
6	
7	### Quick install
8	
9	```bash
10	curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dsifry/metaswarm/main/.codex/install.sh | bash
11	```
12	
13	### Manual install
14	
15	```bash
16	git clone https://github.com/dsifry/metaswarm.git ~/.codex/metaswarm
17	mkdir -p ~/.agents/skills
18	for d in ~/.codex/metaswarm/skills/*/; do
19	  ln -sf "$d" ~/.agents/skills/metaswarm-$(basename "$d")
20	done
21	```
22	
23	### Via npm (cross-platform installer)
24	
25	```bash
26	npx metaswarm init --codex
27	```
28	
29	## Project Setup
30	
31	In your project directory, invoke the setup skill:
32	
33	```text
34	$setup
35	```
36	
37	This detects your project's language, framework, test runner, and tools, then creates `AGENTS.md` and `.coverage-thresholds.json`.
38	
39	## How Codex Finds Skills
40	
41	Codex uses the `name` field from each skill's `SKILL.md` frontmatter — not the directory name. The directory prefix `metaswarm-` is for organization only. You invoke skills using `$name` syntax matching the SKILL.md `name` field.
42	
43	## Available Skills
44	
45	| Invoke with | SKILL.md name | Purpose |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| `$start` | `start` | Begin tracked work on a task |
48	| `$setup` | `setup` | Interactive guided setup |
49	| `$brainstorming-extension` | `brainstorming-extension` | Refine an idea with design review gate |
50	| `$design-review-gate` | `design-review-gate` | 5-reviewer design review |
51	| `$plan-review-gate` | `plan-review-gate` | 3-reviewer adversarial plan review |
52	| `$orchestrated-execution` | `orchestrated-execution` | 4-phase execution loop |
53	| `$pr-shepherd` | `pr-shepherd` | Monitor a PR through to merge |
54	| `$handling-pr-comments` | `handling-pr-comments` | Handle PR review comments |
55	| `$create-issue` | `create-issue` | Create a well-structured GitHub Issue |
56	| `$external-tools` | `external-tools` | Check/use external AI tools |
57	| `$status` | `status` | Run diagnostic checks |
58	| `$migrate` | `migrate` | Migrate from npm installation |
59	| `$visual-review` | `visual-review` | Playwright screenshot capture |
60	
61	## Limitations
62	
63	Codex CLI does not have a `Task()` equivalent for spawning subagents. This means:
64	
65	- **Design review** runs sequentially (each reviewer perspective one at a time) instead of in parallel
66	- **Adversarial review** uses rubrics as structured checklists rather than fresh isolated reviewers
67	- **Background tasks** are not available; all work runs in the current session
68	
69	The quality gates and rubric criteria are identical — the same review standards apply regardless of platform. The difference is execution mode (sequential vs parallel), not review thoroughness.
70	
71	## Updating
72	
73	```bash
74	cd ~/.codex/metaswarm && git pull
75	```
76	
77	Or re-run the install script — it detects existing installations and updates in-place.
78	
79	## Uninstall
80	
81	```bash
82	# Remove skill symlinks
83	for link in ~/.agents/skills/metaswarm-*; do rm -f "$link"; done
84	# Remove installation
85	rm -rf ~/.codex/metaswarm
86	```
87

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# .codex/install.sh
3	# Install metaswarm skills for Codex CLI
4	#
5	# Usage:
6	#   curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dsifry/metaswarm/main/.codex/install.sh | bash
7	#   # or
8	#   bash .codex/install.sh  (from cloned repo)
9	
10	set -euo pipefail
11	
12	INSTALL_DIR="${CODEX_HOME:-$HOME/.codex}/metaswarm"
13	SKILLS_DIR="$HOME/.agents/skills"
14	REPO_URL="https://github.com/dsifry/metaswarm.git"
15	
16	echo ""
17	echo "  metaswarm — Codex CLI installer"
18	echo "  ================================"
19	echo ""
20	
21	# Check if already installed
22	if [ -d "$INSTALL_DIR" ]; then
23	  echo "  Updating existing installation at $INSTALL_DIR..."
24	  cd "$INSTALL_DIR"
25	  git pull --rebase origin main 2>/dev/null || {
26	    echo "  Warning: git pull failed. Removing and re-cloning..."
27	    cd /
28	    rm -rf "$INSTALL_DIR"
29	    git clone "$REPO_URL" "$INSTALL_DIR"
30	  }
31	else
32	  echo "  Cloning metaswarm..."
33	  mkdir -p "$(dirname "$INSTALL_DIR")"
34	  git clone "$REPO_URL" "$INSTALL_DIR"
35	fi
36	
37	# Symlink skills
38	echo ""
39	echo "  Symlinking skills into $SKILLS_DIR..."
40	mkdir -p "$SKILLS_DIR"
41	
42	linked=0
43	for skill_dir in "$INSTALL_DIR/skills"/*/; do
44	  [ -d "$skill_dir" ] || continue
45	  skill_name="metaswarm-$(basename "$skill_dir")"
46	  target="$SKILLS_DIR/$skill_name"
47	
48	  if [ -L "$target" ]; then
49	    # Update existing symlink
50	    rm "$target"
51	  elif [ -d "$target" ]; then
52	    echo "  Warning: $target exists as a directory, skipping"
53	    continue
54	  fi
55	
56	  ln -sf "$skill_dir" "$target"
57	  linked=$((linked + 1))
58	done
59	
60	echo "  Linked $linked skills."
61	
62	# Copy AGENTS.md template if project doesn't have one
63	echo ""
64	if [ -f "AGENTS.md" ] && grep -q "metaswarm" "AGENTS.md" 2>/dev/null; then
65	  echo "  AGENTS.md already has metaswarm section."
66	elif [ -f "AGENTS.md" ]; then
67	  echo "  Note: AGENTS.md exists but doesn't reference metaswarm."
68	  echo "  Run \$setup in your project to configure it."
69	else
70	  echo "  Note: No project-level AGENTS.md created."
71	  echo "  Run \$setup in your project to set it up."
72	fi
73	
74	echo ""
75	echo "  Done! metaswarm installed for Codex CLI."
76	echo ""
77	echo "  Usage (Codex uses the 'name' field from SKILL.md frontmatter):"
78	echo "    \$start                   Begin tracked work"
79	echo "    \$setup                   Configure for your project"
80	echo "    \$brainstorming-extension Refine an idea"
81	echo "    \$design-review-gate      Run 5-reviewer design review"
82	echo ""
83	echo "  See .codex/README.md for the full skill list."
84	echo ""
85

1	#!/bin/bash
2	set -euo pipefail
3	
4	# --- Defaults ---
5	input_tokens=0
6	output_tokens=0
7	model=""
8	
9	# --- Argument Parsing ---
10	while [[ "$#" -gt 0 ]]; do
11	    case "$1" in
12	        --input-tokens)
13	            input_tokens="$2"
14	            shift 2
15	            ;;
16	        --output-tokens)
17	            output_tokens="$2"
18	            shift 2
19	            ;;
20	        --model)
21	            model="$2"
22	            shift 2
23	            ;;
24	        *)
25	            echo "Unknown parameter passed: $1"
26	            exit 1
27	            ;;
28	    esac
29	done
30	
31	# --- Validate Arguments ---
32	if [[ -z "$model" || "$input_tokens" -eq 0 || "$output_tokens" -eq 0 ]]; then
33	    echo "Error: --input-tokens, --output-tokens, and --model are required." >&2
34	    exit 1
35	fi
36	
37	# --- Model Pricing (per 1 million tokens) ---
38	input_cost_rate=""
39	output_cost_rate=""
40	
41	case "$model" in
42	    "gpt-5.3-codex")
43	        input_cost_rate="1.50"
44	        output_cost_rate="6.00"
45	        ;;
46	    "gemini-pro")
47	        input_cost_rate="1.25"
48	        output_cost_rate="5.00"
49	        ;;
50	    *)
51	        echo "Error: Unknown model '$model'." >&2
52	        exit 1
53	        ;;
54	esac
55	
56	# --- Cost Calculation ---
57	input_cost_per_token=$(awk -v rate="$input_cost_rate" 'BEGIN {print rate / 1000000}')
58	output_cost_per_token=$(awk -v rate="$output_cost_rate" 'BEGIN {print rate / 1000000}')
59	
60	total_input_cost=$(awk -v tokens="$input_tokens" -v cost="$input_cost_per_token" 'BEGIN {print tokens * cost}')
61	total_output_cost=$(awk -v tokens="$output_tokens" -v cost="$output_cost_per_token" 'BEGIN {print tokens * cost}')
62	
63	estimated_usd=$(awk -v ic="$total_input_cost" -v oc="$total_output_cost" 'BEGIN {print ic + oc}')
64	
65	# --- JSON Output ---
66	cat <<EOF
67	{
68	  "model": "$model",
69	  "input_tokens": $input_tokens,
70	  "output_tokens": $output_tokens,
71	  "estimated_usd": $estimated_usd
72	}
73	EOF
74

1	#!/bin/bash
2	# external-tools-verify.sh — End-to-end verification for the external-tools skill
3	#
4	# Tests adapter scripts, shared helpers, config templates, and documentation.
5	# Prints PASS/FAIL per check with a final summary and exit code.
6	#
7	# Usage: bin/external-tools-verify.sh
8	
9	set -uo pipefail
10	
11	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
12	# Resolve paths relative to the repo root
13	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
14	SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
15	REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
16	
17	ADAPTERS_DIR="${REPO_ROOT}/skills/external-tools/adapters"
18	COMMON_SH="${ADAPTERS_DIR}/_common.sh"
19	CODEX_SH="${ADAPTERS_DIR}/codex.sh"
20	GEMINI_SH="${ADAPTERS_DIR}/gemini.sh"
21	
22	PASS_COUNT=0
23	FAIL_COUNT=0
24	
25	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
26	# Helpers
27	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
28	pass() {
29	  printf 'PASS: %s\n' "$1"
30	  PASS_COUNT=$(( PASS_COUNT + 1 ))
31	}
32	
33	fail() {
34	  printf 'FAIL: %s\n' "$1"
35	  FAIL_COUNT=$(( FAIL_COUNT + 1 ))
36	}
37	
38	check() {
39	  local label="$1"
40	  shift
41	  if "$@" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
42	    pass "$label"
43	  else
44	    fail "$label"
45	  fi
46	}
47	
48	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
49	# 1. _common.sh tests
50	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
51	printf '\n=== _common.sh ===\n'
52	
53	# Syntax check
54	check "_common.sh syntax" bash -n "$COMMON_SH"
55	
56	# create_secure_tmp: directory exists and has mode 700
57	TMP_DIR="$(bash -c "source '$COMMON_SH' && create_secure_tmp")"
58	if [[ -d "$TMP_DIR" ]]; then
59	  PERMS="$(stat -f '%Lp' "$TMP_DIR" 2>/dev/null || stat -c '%a' "$TMP_DIR" 2>/dev/null || printf '')"
60	  if [[ "$PERMS" == "700" ]]; then
61	    pass "create_secure_tmp permissions (700)"
62	  else
63	    fail "create_secure_tmp permissions (got $PERMS, expected 700)"
64	  fi
65	  rm -rf "$TMP_DIR"
66	else
67	  fail "create_secure_tmp did not create a directory"
68	fi
69	
70	# classify_error: timeout (exit 124) -> "timeout"
71	RESULT="$(bash -c "source '$COMMON_SH' && classify_error 124 ''")"
72	if [[ "$RESULT" == "timeout" ]]; then
73	  pass "classify_error 124 -> timeout"
74	else
75	  fail "classify_error 124 -> expected 'timeout', got '$RESULT'"
76	fi
77	
78	# classify_error: command not found (exit 127) -> "tool_not_installed"
79	RESULT="$(bash -c "source '$COMMON_SH' && classify_error 127 ''")"
80	if [[ "$RESULT" == "tool_not_installed" ]]; then
81	  pass "classify_error 127 -> tool_not_installed"
82	else
83	  fail "classify_error 127 -> expected 'tool_not_installed', got '$RESULT'"
84	fi
85	
86	# classify_error: generic error -> "tool_crash"
87	RESULT="$(bash -c "source '$COMMON_SH' && classify_error 1 ''")"
88	if [[ "$RESULT" == "tool_crash" ]]; then
89	  pass "classify_error 1 -> tool_crash"
90	else
91	  fail "classify_error 1 -> expected 'tool_crash', got '$RESULT'"
92	fi
93	
94	# emit_error: produces valid JSON
95	ERROR_JSON="$(bash -c "source '$COMMON_SH' && emit_error codex implement gpt-5.3-codex 1 1 '' 0 ''")"
96	if printf '%s' "$ERROR_JSON" | jq . >/dev/null 2>&1; then
97	  pass "emit_error produces valid JSON"
98	else
99	  fail "emit_error does not produce valid JSON"
100	fi
101	
102	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
103	# 2. codex.sh tests
104	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
105	printf '\n=== codex.sh ===\n'
106	
107	# Syntax check
108	check "codex.sh syntax" bash -n "$CODEX_SH"
109	
110	# Health produces valid JSON
111	HEALTH_JSON="$(bash "$CODEX_SH" health 2>/dev/null || true)"
112	if printf '%s' "$HEALTH_JSON" | jq . >/dev/null 2>&1; then
113	  pass "codex.sh health produces valid JSON"
114	else
115	  fail "codex.sh health does not produce valid JSON"
116	fi
117	
118	# Health JSON contains required keys
119	if printf '%s' "$HEALTH_JSON" | jq -e '.tool and .status and .model' >/dev/null 2>&1; then
120	  pass "codex.sh health JSON has required keys (tool, status, model)"
121	else
122	  fail "codex.sh health JSON missing required keys"
123	fi
124	
125	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
126	# 3. gemini.sh tests
127	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
128	printf '\n=== gemini.sh ===\n'
129	
130	# Syntax check
131	check "gemini.sh syntax" bash -n "$GEMINI_SH"
132	
133	# Health produces valid JSON
134	HEALTH_JSON="$(bash "$GEMINI_SH" health 2>/dev/null || true)"
135	if printf '%s' "$HEALTH_JSON" | jq . >/dev/null 2>&1; then
136	  pass "gemini.sh health produces valid JSON"
137	else
138	  fail "gemini.sh health does not produce valid JSON"
139	fi
140	
141	# Health JSON contains required keys
142	if printf '%s' "$HEALTH_JSON" | jq -e '.tool and .status and .model' >/dev/null 2>&1; then
143	  pass "gemini.sh health JSON has required keys (tool, status, model)"
144	else
145	  fail "gemini.sh health JSON missing required keys"
146	fi
147	
148	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
149	# 4. File existence checks
150	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
151	printf '\n=== File existence ===\n'
152	
153	check "templates/external-tools.yaml exists" test -f "${REPO_ROOT}/templates/external-tools.yaml"
154	check "skills/external-tools/SKILL.md exists" test -f "${REPO_ROOT}/skills/external-tools/SKILL.md"
155	check "rubrics/external-tool-review-rubric.md exists" test -f "${REPO_ROOT}/rubrics/external-tool-review-rubric.md"
156	
157	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
158	# Summary
159	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
160	TOTAL=$(( PASS_COUNT + FAIL_COUNT ))
161	printf '\n=== Summary ===\n'
162	printf '%d/%d passed, %d failed\n' "$PASS_COUNT" "$TOTAL" "$FAIL_COUNT"
163	
164	if [[ "$FAIL_COUNT" -gt 0 ]]; then
165	  exit 1
166	else
167	  exit 0
168	fi
169

1	#!/bin/bash
2	# PR Comments Check - Verifies all review comments have been addressed
3	# Usage: bin/pr-comments-check.sh <PR_NUMBER>
4	# Returns: 0 if all addressed, 1 if unaddressed comments exist, 2 if API error
5	
6	set -e
7	
8	PR_NUMBER=${1:-$(gh pr view --json number -q .number 2>/dev/null || true)}
9	
10	if [ -z "$PR_NUMBER" ]; then
11	  echo "Usage: bin/pr-comments-check.sh <PR_NUMBER>"
12	  echo "Or run from a branch with an open PR"
13	  exit 1
14	fi
15	
16	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
17	REPO_NAME=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
18	
19	echo "Checking PR #$PR_NUMBER for unaddressed comments..."
20	echo ""
21	
22	UNADDRESSED=0
23	
24	# Check inline code review comments
25	echo "=== Inline Code Review Comments ==="
26	
27	# Fetch all comments first, fail if API error
28	COMMENTS_JSON=$(gh api --paginate repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments 2>&1) || {
29	  echo "API ERROR: Failed to fetch PR comments"
30	  echo "   $COMMENTS_JSON"
31	  exit 2
32	}
33	
34	# Extract top-level comment IDs
35	COMMENT_IDS=$(echo "$COMMENTS_JSON" | jq -r '.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | .id')
36	
37	if [ -z "$COMMENT_IDS" ]; then
38	  echo "No inline code review comments found"
39	else
40	  for comment_id in $COMMENT_IDS; do
41	    author=$(echo "$COMMENTS_JSON" | jq -r ".[] | select(.id == $comment_id) | .user.login")
42	    body=$(echo "$COMMENTS_JSON" | jq -r ".[] | select(.id == $comment_id) | .body[:80]")
43	
44	    # Count replies - default to 0 if empty or error
45	    reply_count=$(echo "$COMMENTS_JSON" | jq "[.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == $comment_id)] | length")
46	    reply_count=${reply_count:-0}
47	
48	    # Ensure reply_count is a valid integer
49	    if ! [[ "$reply_count" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then
50	      echo "WARNING: Could not determine reply count for comment $comment_id, treating as unaddressed"
51	      reply_count=0
52	    fi
53	
54	    if [ "$reply_count" -eq 0 ]; then
55	      echo "UNADDRESSED: Comment $comment_id by $author"
56	      echo "   $body..."
57	      UNADDRESSED=$((UNADDRESSED + 1))
58	    else
59	      echo "OK: Comment $comment_id by $author - $reply_count reply(s)"
60	    fi
61	  done
62	fi
63	
64	echo ""
65	echo "=== General PR Discussion Comments ==="
66	# List discussion comments (bot comments typically don't need replies)
67	ISSUE_COMMENTS=$(gh api --paginate repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/issues/$PR_NUMBER/comments 2>&1) || {
68	  echo "WARNING: Could not fetch discussion comments"
69	  echo "   $ISSUE_COMMENTS"
70	}
71	
72	if [ -n "$ISSUE_COMMENTS" ] && [ "$ISSUE_COMMENTS" != "[]" ]; then
73	  echo "$ISSUE_COMMENTS" | jq -r '.[] | "INFO: \(.user.login): \(.body[:60])..."' 2>/dev/null || echo "No discussion comments"
74	else
75	  echo "No discussion comments"
76	fi
77	
78	echo ""
79	if [ $UNADDRESSED -gt 0 ]; then
80	  echo "BLOCKED: $UNADDRESSED unaddressed comment(s) found"
81	  echo "   Address each comment before declaring PR ready"
82	  exit 1
83	else
84	  echo "All inline review comments have been addressed"
85	  exit 0
86	fi
87

1	#!/bin/bash
2	#
3	# PR Comments Filter Script
4	# Filters actionable vs non-actionable PR review comments
5	#
6	# Usage: bin/pr-comments-filter.sh <PR_NUMBER>
7	#
8	# Outputs:
9	#   - Count of non-actionable comments (confirmations, acknowledgments)
10	#   - Count and details of actionable comments by priority
11	#
12	# Requires: gh CLI (authenticated), jq
13	
14	set -e
15	
16	# Load environment variables if .env exists
17	if [ -f .env ]; then
18	  set -a
19	  source .env
20	  set +a
21	fi
22	
23	# === ARGUMENT VALIDATION ===
24	if [ -z "$1" ]; then
25	  echo "Usage: $0 <PR_NUMBER>"
26	  echo "Example: $0 746"
27	  exit 1
28	fi
29	
30	PR_NUMBER="$1"
31	
32	# Validate PR number is a positive integer
33	if ! [[ "$PR_NUMBER" =~ ^[1-9][0-9]*$ ]]; then
34	  echo "Error: PR_NUMBER must be a positive integer"
35	  exit 1
36	fi
37	
38	# === DEPENDENCY CHECKS ===
39	if ! command -v gh &> /dev/null; then
40	  echo "Error: GitHub CLI (gh) not installed"
41	  echo "Install from: https://cli.github.com/"
42	  exit 1
43	fi
44	
45	if ! command -v jq &> /dev/null; then
46	  echo "Error: jq not installed"
47	  echo "Install: brew install jq (Mac) or apt install jq (Linux)"
48	  exit 1
49	fi
50	
51	if ! gh auth status &> /dev/null; then
52	  echo "Error: Not authenticated with GitHub CLI"
53	  echo "Run: gh auth login"
54	  exit 1
55	fi
56	
57	# === GET REPO INFO ===
58	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
59	REPO_NAME=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
60	
61	# Verify PR exists
62	if ! gh pr view "$PR_NUMBER" &> /dev/null; then
63	  echo "Error: PR #$PR_NUMBER not found in $OWNER/$REPO_NAME"
64	  exit 1
65	fi
66	
67	echo "=== PR COMMENT FILTER ==="
68	echo "Repository: $OWNER/$REPO_NAME"
69	echo "PR Number: $PR_NUMBER"
70	echo ""
71	
72	# === FETCH COMMENTS ===
73	TEMP_COMMENTS=$(mktemp)
74	TEMP_RAW=$(mktemp)
75	trap 'rm -f "$TEMP_COMMENTS" "$TEMP_RAW"' EXIT
76	
77	# Paginated API returns separate JSON arrays per page - merge them with jq --slurp
78	gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments" --paginate > "$TEMP_RAW"
79	jq --slurp 'add // []' "$TEMP_RAW" > "$TEMP_COMMENTS"
80	
81	TOTAL_COUNT=$(jq 'length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
82	echo "Total comments: $TOTAL_COUNT"
83	
84	# === NON-ACTIONABLE PATTERNS ===
85	echo ""
86	echo "--- Non-Actionable Comments (will be skipped) ---"
87	
88	# CodeRabbit confirmations
89	NA_CONFIRMATIONS=$(jq '[.[] | select(.body | test("review_comment_addressed"; "i"))] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
90	echo "  CodeRabbit confirmations: $NA_CONFIRMATIONS"
91	
92	# Bot confirmations
93	NA_BOT_CONFIRMS=$(jq '[.[] | select(.body | test("Confirmed"; "i"))] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
94	echo "  Bot confirmations: $NA_BOT_CONFIRMS"
95	
96	# Thank you replies
97	NA_THANK_YOU=$(jq '[.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id != null) | select(.body | test("Thank you for"; "i"))] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
98	echo "  Bot thank-you replies: $NA_THANK_YOU"
99	
100	# Learnings context (no severity marker)
101	NA_LEARNINGS=$(jq '[.[] | select(.body | test("Learnings used"; "i")) | select(.body | test("_⚠️|_🔴|_🟠|_🟡|_🔵|_🧹|_🛠️") | not)] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
102	echo "  Learnings context: $NA_LEARNINGS"
103	
104	# Fingerprinting
105	NA_FINGERPRINT=$(jq '[.[] | select(.body | test("fingerprinting:"; "i"))] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
106	echo "  CodeRabbit fingerprinting: $NA_FINGERPRINT"
107	
108	# Human "Fixed in commit" responses
109	NA_HUMAN_FIXED=$(jq '[.[] | select(.user.login | test("bot"; "i") | not) | select(.body | test("Fixed in commit"; "i"))] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
110	echo "  Human 'Fixed in commit': $NA_HUMAN_FIXED"
111	
112	# === ACTIONABLE COMMENTS BY PRIORITY ===
113	echo ""
114	echo "--- Actionable Comments by Priority ---"
115	
116	# Critical
117	CRITICAL_COUNT=$(jq '[.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.body | test("_🔴 Critical_"))] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
118	if [ "$CRITICAL_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
119	  echo "CRITICAL: $CRITICAL_COUNT - FIX IMMEDIATELY"
120	else
121	  echo "CRITICAL: 0"
122	fi
123	
124	# High
125	HIGH_COUNT=$(jq '[.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.body | test("_⚠️ Potential issue_.*_🟠 Major_"))] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
126	if [ "$HIGH_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
127	  echo "HIGH: $HIGH_COUNT - Fix before merge"
128	else
129	  echo "HIGH: 0"
130	fi
131	
132	# Medium
133	MEDIUM_COUNT=$(jq '[.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.body | test("_🟡 Minor_|_🛠️ Refactor suggestion_.*_🟠 Major_"))] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
134	if [ "$MEDIUM_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
135	  echo "MEDIUM: $MEDIUM_COUNT - Should fix"
136	else
137	  echo "MEDIUM: 0"
138	fi
139	
140	# Low
141	LOW_COUNT=$(jq '[.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.body | test("_🔵 Trivial_|_🧹 Nitpick_"))] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
142	if [ "$LOW_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
143	  echo "LOW: $LOW_COUNT - Fix if quick"
144	else
145	  echo "LOW: 0"
146	fi
147	
148	# Human reviewer comments
149	HUMAN_COUNT=$(jq '[.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.user.login | test("bot"; "i") | not)] | length' "$TEMP_COMMENTS")
150	if [ "$HUMAN_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
151	  echo "HUMAN: $HUMAN_COUNT - Always process"
152	else
153	  echo "HUMAN: 0"
154	fi
155	
156	# === DETAILED ACTIONABLE COMMENTS ===
157	echo ""
158	echo "--- Actionable Comment Details ---"
159	
160	# Critical details
161	if [ "$CRITICAL_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
162	  echo ""
163	  echo "CRITICAL COMMENTS:"
164	  jq -r '.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.body | test("_🔴 Critical_")) |
165	    "  ID: \(.id) | \(.path):\(.line // .original_line // "?")\n  @\(.user.login): \(.body[0:100])...\n"' "$TEMP_COMMENTS"
166	fi
167	
168	# High priority details
169	if [ "$HIGH_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
170	  echo ""
171	  echo "HIGH PRIORITY COMMENTS:"
172	  jq -r '.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.body | test("_⚠️ Potential issue_.*_🟠 Major_")) |
173	    "  ID: \(.id) | \(.path):\(.line // .original_line // "?")\n  @\(.user.login): \(.body[0:100])...\n"' "$TEMP_COMMENTS"
174	fi
175	
176	# Medium priority details
177	if [ "$MEDIUM_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
178	  echo ""
179	  echo "MEDIUM PRIORITY COMMENTS:"
180	  jq -r '.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.body | test("_🟡 Minor_|_🛠️ Refactor suggestion_.*_🟠 Major_")) |
181	    "  ID: \(.id) | \(.path):\(.line // .original_line // "?")\n  @\(.user.login): \(.body[0:100])...\n"' "$TEMP_COMMENTS"
182	fi
183	
184	# Low priority details
185	if [ "$LOW_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
186	  echo ""
187	  echo "LOW PRIORITY COMMENTS:"
188	  jq -r '.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.body | test("_🔵 Trivial_|_🧹 Nitpick_")) |
189	    "  ID: \(.id) | \(.path):\(.line // .original_line // "?")\n  @\(.user.login): \(.body[0:100])...\n"' "$TEMP_COMMENTS"
190	fi
191	
192	# Human reviewer details
193	if [ "$HUMAN_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
194	  echo ""
195	  echo "HUMAN REVIEWER COMMENTS:"
196	  jq -r '.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.user.login | test("bot"; "i") | not) |
197	    "  ID: \(.id) | \(.path):\(.line // .original_line // "?")\n  @\(.user.login): \(.body[0:100])...\n"' "$TEMP_COMMENTS"
198	fi
199	
200	echo ""
201	echo "=== SUMMARY ==="
202	TOTAL_ACTIONABLE=$((CRITICAL_COUNT + HIGH_COUNT + MEDIUM_COUNT + LOW_COUNT))
203	echo "Total actionable (bot): $TOTAL_ACTIONABLE"
204	echo "Total actionable (human): $HUMAN_COUNT"
205	echo "Total to process: $((TOTAL_ACTIONABLE + HUMAN_COUNT))"
206

1	---
2	name: brainstorming-extension
3	description: Enforces design review gate after brainstorming — bridges superpowers:brainstorming into the metaswarm quality pipeline
4	auto_activate: true
5	triggers:
6	  - after:superpowers:brainstorming
7	  - "design document committed"
8	---
9	
10	# Brainstorming Extension - Mandatory Review Gate Bridge
11	
12	## Purpose
13	
14	This skill bridges `superpowers:brainstorming` into the metaswarm quality pipeline by enforcing the Design Review Gate after any design document is created. Without this bridge, brainstorming flows directly into `writing-plans`, bypassing the 5-agent design review that catches architectural, security, and requirements issues before expensive implementation begins.
15	
16	**This is a critical workflow enforcement point, not a passive extension.**
17	
18	---
19	
20	## The Problem This Solves
21	
22	`superpowers:brainstorming` has a built-in terminal state: "The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans." This creates a pipeline bypass:
23	
24	```text
25	WITHOUT this extension (broken flow):
26	
27	superpowers:brainstorming
28	    └── Commits design doc
29	          └── writing-plans (DIRECTLY — no review!)
30	                └── executing-plans
31	                      └── Implementation begins on UNREVIEWED design
32	```
33	
34	```text
35	WITH this extension (correct flow):
36	
37	superpowers:brainstorming
38	    └── Commits design doc
39	          │
40	          ▼
41	    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
42	    │  MANDATORY DESIGN REVIEW GATE           │
43	    │  (Enforced by CLAUDE.md + start-task)   │
44	    │                                         │
45	    │  5 parallel review agents:              │
46	    │  • Product Manager (use cases)          │
47	    │  • Architect (architecture)             │
48	    │  • Designer (UX/API)                    │
49	    │  • Security Design (threats)            │
50	    │  • CTO (TDD readiness)                 │
51	    │                                         │
52	    │  ALL FIVE must APPROVE                  │
53	    └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
54	          │
55	          ▼
56	    ALL APPROVED? ────No────► Iterate on design (max 3)
57	          │
58	         Yes
59	          │
60	          ▼
61	    writing-plans → plan-review-gate → orchestrated-execution
62	```
63	
64	---
65	
66	## How Enforcement Works
67	
68	YAML frontmatter triggers (`auto_activate`, `triggers`) are metadata hints — Claude Code does not enforce them automatically. Instead, this enforcement works through three redundant mechanisms:
69	
70	### 1. CLAUDE.md Template (Primary)
71	
72	The CLAUDE.md template contains a "Workflow Enforcement (MANDATORY)" section that explicitly states:
73	
74	> After brainstorming: STOP → Run Design Review Gate → Wait for all 5 agents to APPROVE → Only then proceed
75	
76	This instruction is loaded into every conversation and overrides conflicting skill instructions.
77	
78	### 2. start-task Command (Secondary)
79	
80	The `/start-task` command's Problem Definition Phase includes an explicit "MANDATORY HANDOFF" block that requires the design review gate after brainstorming.
81	
82	### 3. This Skill Document (Reference)
83	
84	When this skill is loaded (either by name or auto-activation), it provides the detailed procedure below.
85	
86	---
87	
88	## Procedure: After Brainstorming Completes
89	
90	When `superpowers:brainstorming` commits a design document:
91	
92	### Step 1: Announce the Gate
93	
94	```markdown
95	## Design Review Gate Activated
96	
97	Your design document has been committed. Before proceeding to implementation,
98	I'll run it through our 5-agent review panel.
99	
100	Spawning reviews:
101	- Product Manager Agent (use case/requirements validation)
102	- Architect Agent (technical architecture)
103	- Designer Agent (UX/API design)
104	- Security Design Agent (threat modeling/security review)
105	- CTO Agent (TDD readiness)
106	
107	This typically takes 2-3 minutes...
108	```
109	
110	### Step 2: Invoke the Design Review Gate
111	
112	Invoke the `design-review-gate` skill with the path to the design document. This spawns all 5 review agents in parallel.
113	
114	### Step 3: Report Results
115	
116	**If ALL APPROVED:**
117	
118	```markdown
119	## Design Review Gate: PASSED
120	
121	All five reviewers have approved your design!
122	
123	| Agent           | Verdict  | Notes                                 |
124	| --------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------- |
125	| Product Manager | APPROVED | Clear use cases, measurable benefits  |
126	| Architect       | APPROVED | Clean architecture, follows patterns  |
127	| Designer        | APPROVED | Good API design, clear error states   |
128	| Security Design | APPROVED | No high-risk threats, mitigations OK  |
129	| CTO             | APPROVED | TDD specs present, ready to implement |
130	
131	### Next Steps
132	1. Create epic for this feature
133	2. Create implementation plan (writing-plans)
134	3. Run plan through Plan Review Gate (3 adversarial reviewers)
135	4. Set up worktree for isolated development
136	5. Begin orchestrated execution
137	
138	Ready to proceed? [Yes / No]
139	```
140	
141	**If ANY NEEDS_REVISION:**
142	
143	```markdown
144	## Design Review Gate: NEEDS REVISION
145	
146	Some reviewers found issues that need to be addressed.
147	
148	### Blocking Issues
149	[Agent-specific issues listed here]
150	
151	### Questions Requiring Answers
152	[Questions listed here]
153	
154	---
155	
156	Please revise the design document and I'll re-run the review gate.
157	(Iteration 1 of 3)
158	```
159	
160	### Step 4: Iterate or Proceed
161	
162	- If NEEDS_REVISION: Help user address issues, re-run gate (max 3 iterations)
163	- If ALL APPROVED: Proceed to planning phase
164	- After 3 failed iterations: Escalate to human decision (Override / Defer / Cancel)
165	
166	---
167	
168	## Skip Conditions
169	
170	The review gate can be skipped ONLY when:
171	
172	- The user explicitly requests it ("skip the review gate")
173	- AND the agent confirms with the user before skipping
174	- AND the design is genuinely simple (< 1 day of work, < 3 files)
175	
176	---
177	
178	## Related Skills
179	
180	- `superpowers:brainstorming` — The upstream skill this bridges from
181	- `design-review-gate` — The 5-agent review implementation
182	- `plan-review-gate` — The next gate in the pipeline (after writing-plans)
183	- `orchestrated-execution` — The execution framework after planning
184

4	---
5	
6	# Create GitHub Issue Skill
7	
8	Create comprehensive, well-structured GitHub issues with embedded agent instructions for autonomous PR lifecycle management.
9	
10	## Overview
11	
12	This skill generates GitHub issues that are self-contained work packets. Each issue includes:
13	
14	1. **Problem/Feature specification** with clear scope
15	2. **Technical specification** with types, schemas, and API design
16	3. **TDD Implementation Plan** with test-first cycles
17	4. **Acceptance Criteria** checklist
18	5. **Agent Instructions** for complete PR lifecycle (implementation → review → merge)
19	
20	## Usage
21	
22	```
23	/create-issue
24	```
25	
26	## Interactive Flow
27	
28	### Step 1: Issue Type
29	
30	Ask the user:
31	
32	```
33	What type of issue is this?
34	1. Feature - New functionality
35	2. Bug - Something broken
36	3. Refactor - Improving existing code
37	```
38	
39	### Step 2: Brief Description
40	
41	```
42	Brief description of the problem or feature? (1-2 sentences)
43	```
44	
45	### Step 3: Complexity Assessment
46	
47	```
48	What's the estimated complexity?
49	1. Simple (< 1 day)
50	2. Medium (2-5 days)
51	3. Complex (> 1 week)
52	```
53	
54	### Step 4: Context Gathering
55	
56	```
57	Any related files, services, or issues I should reference?
58	(Or say "explore codebase" and I'll investigate)
59	```
60	
61	### Step 5: Generate Issue
62	
63	Based on inputs, generate the full issue using the appropriate template below.
64	
65	---
66	
67	## Issue Templates
68	
69	### Feature Template
70	
71	````markdown
72	## Summary
73	
74	[1-2 sentence description]
75	
76	**Complexity**: [simple|medium|complex]
77	**Estimated Effort**: [X days]
78	
79	---
80	
81	## Problem
82	
83	[What problem does this solve? Why is it needed?]
84	
85	### Evidence
86	
87	[Logs, metrics, user reports, or reasoning]
88	
89	### Scope
90	
91	- IN SCOPE: [what's included]
92	- OUT OF SCOPE: [what's explicitly NOT included]
93	
94	---
95	
96	## Architecture Decision
97	
98	**Decision**: [What approach will be taken]
99	**Rationale**: [Why this approach, why NOT alternatives]
100	
101	---
102	
103	## Technical Specification
104	
105	### Types/Interfaces
106	
107	```typescript
108	// Key type definitions
109	```
110	````
111	
112	### Zod Schemas (if applicable)
113	
114	```typescript
115	// Validation schemas
116	```
117	
118	### API Endpoints (if applicable)
119	
120	| Method | Path | Description |
121	| ------ | ---- | ----------- |
122	
123	### Database Changes (if applicable)
124	
125	[Schema changes, migrations needed]
126	
127	---
128	
129	## TDD Implementation Plan
130	
131	### Cycle 1: [Happy Path]
132	
133	```typescript
134	it("should [expected behavior]", async () => {
135	  // Test code
136	});
137	```
138	
139	### Cycle 2: [Error Handling]
140	
141	```typescript
142	it("should handle [error case]", async () => {
143	  // Test code
144	});
145	```
146	
147	### Cycle 3: [Edge Cases]
148	
149	### Cycle 4: [Integration]
150	
151	---
152	
153	## Error Handling
154	
155	| Scenario | Behavior | Logging |
156	| -------- | -------- | ------- |
157	
158	---
159	
160	## Implementation Phases
161	
162	### Phase 1: [Core] (High Priority)
163	
164	- [ ] Task 1
165	- [ ] Task 2
166	
167	### Phase 2: [Enhancement] (Medium Priority)
