170	
171	---
172	
173	## Files to Create/Modify
174	
175	- **NEW**: `path/to/new/file.ts`
176	- `path/to/existing/file.ts` - [what changes]
177	
178	---
179	
180	## Acceptance Criteria
181	
182	- [ ] All TDD cycles implemented with passing tests
183	- [ ] Zero TypeScript errors (`pnpm typecheck`)
184	- [ ] Zero lint warnings (`pnpm lint`)
185	- [ ] All tests pass (`pnpm test --run`)
186	- [ ] **JSDoc documentation** added to all new/modified services
187	- [ ] **`docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md`** updated (if new/modified service)
188	- [ ] **Mock factories** added/updated (if new service)
189	- [ ] PR created and all CI checks pass
190	- [ ] **ALL code review comments addressed** (see Agent Instructions)
191	- [ ] All review threads resolved
192	- [ ] PR approved and merged
193	
194	---
195	
196	## Agent Instructions
197	
198	**CRITICAL**: Follow this protocol when implementing this issue. A PR is NOT complete until ALL steps are done.
199	
200	### Phase 0: Research (BEFORE writing any code)
201	
202	**Read these files first to avoid reinventing the wheel:**
203	
204	1. **Service Inventory** - Check for existing services that might already do what you need:
205	
206	   ```bash
207	   # Read the service inventory
208	   cat docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md
209	   # Or search for specific functionality
210	   grep -i "embedding\|search\|cache" docs/SERVICE_INVENTORY.md
211	   ```
212	
213	2. **Mock Factories** - Review existing mocks to reuse patterns:
214	
215	   ```bash
216	   ls src/test-utils/factories/
217	   ```
218	
219	3. **Similar Services** - Find services with similar patterns:
220	   ```bash
221	   # Find orchestrator patterns
222	   ls src/lib/services/*-orchestrator*.ts
223	   # Find persistence patterns
224	   ls src/lib/services/*-persistence*.ts
225	   ```
226	
227	**Why this matters**: Reusing existing services and patterns saves time, maintains consistency, and prevents duplicate code.
228	
229	### Phase A: Implementation
230	
231	1. Create feature branch from main: `git checkout -b feat/[short-name]`
232	2. Follow TDD cycles exactly as specified above
233	3. **Add JSDoc documentation** to all new/modified services
234	4. Run local validation: `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run`
235	5. **Update service inventory** (if new service)
236	6. Create PR with comprehensive description using `/create-pr`
237	
238	### Phase B: Code Review Iteration
239	
240	**Wait for code reviews before proceeding. Do NOT mark complete after pushing code.**
241	
242	For EACH code review iteration:
243	
244	1. **Fetch ALL comments** (including trivial, nitpicks, and out-of-scope):
245	
246	   ```bash
247	   PR_NUMBER=XXX
248	   OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
249	   REPO=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
250	   gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments" --paginate
251	   ```
252	
253	2. **Address ALL comments by category**:
254	
255	   | Type                   | Action                                               |
256	   | ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
257	   | Critical/Major         | Fix immediately                                      |
258	   | Medium/Minor           | Fix before merge                                     |
259	   | **Trivial/Nitpick**    | **Fix these too!** Do NOT skip                       |
260	   | **Out-of-scope**       | **Investigate thoroughly** - often the BEST insights |
261	
262	3. **For each comment**:
263	   - **< 1 day of work** → Implement the fix in this PR
264	   - **> 1 day OR architectural** → Create a new GitHub issue, link in response
265	   - **Disagree** → Reply explaining why with technical reasoning
266	
267	4. **After making changes**:
268	
269	   ```bash
270	   pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run
271	   git add . && git commit -m "fix: address review feedback" && git push
272	   ```
273	
274	5. **Respond to EVERY comment thread individually**:
275	
276	   ```bash
277	   COMMENT_ID=XXX
278	   CURRENT_USER=$(gh api user -q '.login')
279	   gh api "/repos/$OWNER/$REPO/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments/$COMMENT_ID/replies" \
280	     -X POST \
281	     -f body="Fixed in commit $(git rev-parse --short HEAD). [explanation]
282	
283	   *(Response by Claude on behalf of @$CURRENT_USER)*"
284	   ```
285	
286	6. **Wait for next review cycle** - Do NOT resolve threads yourself
287	
288	7. **Repeat steps 1-6** until reviewer confirms satisfaction
289	
290	### Phase C: Thread Resolution
291	
292	Only AFTER reviewer approval:
293	
294	1. Re-fetch all threads to get current IDs
295	2. Resolve each thread:
296	
297	   ```bash
298	   THREAD_ID="PRRT_xxx"
299	   gh api graphql -f threadId="$THREAD_ID" -f query='
300	     mutation($threadId: ID!) {
301	       resolveReviewThread(input: {threadId: $threadId}) {
302	         thread { id isResolved }
303	       }
304	     }'
305	   ```
306	
307	3. Verify all threads resolved before proceeding
308	
309	### Phase D: Merge Readiness
310	
311	PR is ready for merge ONLY when ALL are true:
312	
313	- [ ] All CI checks pass (green)
314	- [ ] **EVERY** code review comment addressed (including trivial/out-of-scope)
315	- [ ] **EVERY** thread has individual response
316	- [ ] All threads are resolved (after reviewer approval)
317	- [ ] GitHub issues created for any deferred work (> 1 day)
318	- [ ] No pending reviewer comments awaiting response
319	
320	### Phase E: Squash Merge and Cleanup
321	
322	**IMPORTANT**: Always use squash merge, never regular merge.
323	
324	1. **Squash and merge** the PR (combines all commits into one clean commit):
325	
326	   ```bash
327	   # Via GitHub CLI
328	   gh pr merge $PR_NUMBER --squash --delete-branch
329	
330	   # Or via GitHub web UI:
331	   # Click "Squash and merge" (NOT "Merge" or "Rebase and merge")
332	   ```
333	
334	2. **Delete the feature branch** (automatically done with --delete-branch flag):
335	
336	   ```bash
337	   # If branch wasn't auto-deleted:
338	   git push origin --delete feat/[branch-name]
339	   git branch -d feat/[branch-name]
340	   ```
341	
342	3. **Update local main**:
343	
344	   ```bash
345	   git checkout main
346	   git pull origin main
347	   ```
348	
349	### Reference
350	
351	See the `/metaswarm:handle-pr-comments` command for detailed protocol.
352	
353	---
354	
355	## Related Issues
356	
357	- [Link to related issues]
358	
359	## References
360	
361	- [Link to relevant docs, specs, or PRs]
362	
363	````
364	
365	---
366	
367	### Bug Template
368	
369	Use the Feature template with these modifications:
370	
371	**Title format**: `fix: [brief description]`
372	
373	**Replace "Problem" section with**:
374	
375	```markdown
376	## Bug Report
377	
378	**Priority**: [P0-Critical|P1-High|P2-Medium|P3-Low]
379	**Affected Users**: [scope of impact]
380	**First Reported**: [date/source]
381	
382	### Expected Behavior
383	
384	[What should happen]
385	
386	### Actual Behavior
387	
388	[What actually happens]
389	
390	### Steps to Reproduce
391	
392	1. Step 1
393	2. Step 2
394	3. Step 3
395	
396	### Root Cause Analysis
397	
398	[If known, explain why this is happening]
399	
400	### Evidence
401	
402	[Logs, stack traces, screenshots]
403	````
404	
405	**Replace "TDD Implementation Plan" with**:
406	
407	````markdown
408	## Test Cases
409	
410	### Test 1: Verify fix for reported issue
411	
412	```typescript
413	it("should [correct behavior] when [condition]", async () => {
414	  // Reproduce the bug scenario
415	  // Verify it's now fixed
416	});
417	```
418	````
419	
420	### Test 2: Regression test
421	
422	```typescript
423	it("should not regress [related functionality]", async () => {
424	  // Ensure fix doesn't break other things
425	});
426	```
427	
428	````
429	
430	---
431	
432	### Refactor Template
433	
434	Use the Feature template with these modifications:
435	
436	**Title format**: `refactor: [brief description]`
437	
438	**Add after "Summary"**:
439	
440	```markdown
441	## Background
442	
443	[Why is this refactor needed? What triggered it?]
444	
445	### Review Feedback Being Addressed
446	
447	> [Quote the review comment that prompted this work]
448	
449	- Original PR: #XXX
450	- Comment by: @reviewer
451	````
452	
453	**Add to "Acceptance Criteria"**:
454	
455	```markdown
456	- [ ] No breaking changes to public APIs
457	- [ ] All existing tests still pass without modification
458	- [ ] Performance not degraded (if applicable)
459	```
460	
461	---
462	
463	## After Generation
464	
465	After generating the issue content:
466	
467	1. **Show preview** to user
468	2. **Ask for modifications** if needed
469	3. **Create issue** with appropriate labels:
470	
471	   ```bash
472	   # Feature
473	   gh issue create --title "feat: [title]" --body "[content]" \
474	     --label "enhancement" --label "complexity:[level]"
475	
476	   # Bug
477	   gh issue create --title "fix: [title]" --body "[content]" \
478	     --label "bug" --label "priority:[level]"
479	
480	   # Refactor
481	   gh issue create --title "refactor: [title]" --body "[content]" \
482	     --label "refactor" --label "complexity:[level]"
483	   ```
484	
485	4. **Report issue number** to user
486	
487	---
488	
489	## Key Principles
490	
491	1. **Self-contained**: Issues should have everything an agent needs to work autonomously
492	2. **TDD-first**: Always include test specifications before implementation
493	3. **Complete lifecycle**: Agent Instructions cover implementation through merge
494	4. **No shortcuts**: ALL review comments must be addressed, including trivial/nitpicks
495	5. **Out-of-scope = gold**: Comments marked out-of-scope often contain the best insights
496	6. **Iterate until done**: PR is not complete until ALL threads are resolved

2	name: design-review-gate
3	description: Automatic review gate that runs after brainstorming completes - spawns PM, Architect, Designer, Security, and CTO agents in parallel, iterates until all approve
4	auto_activate: true
5	triggers:
6	  - "design document created"
7	  - "docs/plans/*-design.md committed"
8	  - after:superpowers:brainstorming
9	---
10	
11	# Design Review Gate
12	
13	## Purpose
14	
15	This skill automatically activates after a design document is created (typically via `superpowers:brainstorming`). It ensures complex features receive proper review before implementation begins by spawning five specialist agents:
16	
17	1. **Product Manager Agent** - Use case validation and user benefit review
18	2. **Architect Agent** - Technical architecture review
19	3. **Designer Agent** - UX/API design quality review
20	4. **Security Design Agent** - Security threat modeling and protection review
21	5. **CTO Agent** - Codebase alignment and TDD readiness review
22	
23	All five must approve before proceeding to implementation.
24	
25	---
26	
27	## Coordination Mode Note
28	
29	This skill supports both coordination modes:
30	
31	- **Task Mode** (default): Spawn 5 parallel `Task()` subagents for each review round. Fresh instances per round.
32	- **Team Mode**: `TeamCreate("review-{design-doc-name}")`, spawn 5 reviewers as named teammates (`pm`, `architect`, `designer`, `security`, `cto`). Reviewers retain context through revision cycles — saves 5 cold starts per re-review round. After approval, send `shutdown_request` to all, then `TeamDelete`.
33	
34	In either mode, the review criteria, iteration protocol, and escalation rules are identical. See `./guides/agent-coordination.md` for mode detection.
35	
36	---
37	
38	## Activation Triggers
39	
40	This skill auto-activates when:
41	
42	1. A design document is committed to `docs/plans/*-design.md`
43	2. The `superpowers:brainstorming` skill completes
44	3. User explicitly requests: `/review-design <path-to-design.md>`
45	
46	---
47	
48	## Workflow
49	
50	### Phase 1: Spawn Review Agents (Parallel)
51	
52	```typescript
53	// Spawn all five agents in parallel for efficiency
54	const [pmResult, architectResult, designerResult, securityResult, ctoResult] = await Promise.all([
55	  Task({
56	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
57	    description: "PM review",
58	    prompt: pmReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
59	  }),
60	  Task({
61	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
62	    description: "Architect review",
63	    prompt: architectReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
64	  }),
65	  Task({
66	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
67	    description: "Designer review",
68	    prompt: designerReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
69	  }),
70	  Task({
71	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
72	    description: "Security design review",
73	    prompt: securityDesignReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
74	  }),
75	  Task({
76	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
77	    description: "CTO review",
78	    prompt: ctoReviewPrompt(designDocPath),
79	  }),
80	]);
81	```
82	
83	### Phase 2: Aggregate Results
84	
85	Each agent returns a structured review:
86	
87	```typescript
88	interface ReviewResult {
89	  agent: "product-manager" | "architect" | "designer" | "security-design" | "cto";
90	  verdict: "APPROVED" | "NEEDS_REVISION";
91	  blockers: string[]; // MUST fix before implementation
92	  suggestions: string[]; // Nice to have
93	  questions: string[]; // Clarifications needed
94	  use_case_analysis?: {
95	    // PM agent only
96	    total_use_cases: number;
97	    clear: number;
98	    needs_work: number;
99	    missing_scenarios: string[];
100	  };
101	  threat_model?: {
102	    // Security agent only
103	    high_risk: string[];
104	    medium_risk: string[];
105	    mitigations_required: string[];
106	  };
107	}
108	```
109	
110	### Phase 3: Check Gate
111	
112	```
113	IF all five agents return APPROVED:
114	  → Proceed to implementation
115	  → Create epic with approved design
116	
117	ELSE:
118	  → Consolidate feedback
119	  → Present to user
120	  → Iterate on design
121	  → Re-run gate (max 3 iterations)
122	```
123	
124	### Phase 4: Human Escalation
125	
126	After 3 failed iterations:
127	
128	1. Create summary of remaining blockers
129	2. Ask human to decide: override, defer, or cancel
130	
131	---
132	
133	## Agent Prompts
134	
135	### Product Manager Agent Prompt
136	
137	````markdown
138	You are the PRODUCT MANAGER AGENT reviewing a design document.
139	
140	## Your Task
141	
142	Review this design for use case clarity and user benefit validation.
143	
144	## Design Document
145	
146	<path>: {designDocPath}
147	
148	## Review Criteria
149	
150	### 1. Use Case Validation
151	
152	- [ ] Each use case follows WHO/WANTS/SO THAT/WHEN format
153	- [ ] User personas are clearly defined
154	- [ ] Use cases are realistic and based on user needs
155	- [ ] Edge cases and error scenarios considered from user perspective
156	
157	### 2. User Benefit Review
158	
159	- [ ] Value proposition clearly articulated
160	- [ ] Benefits are measurable (time saved, success rate, etc.)
161	- [ ] User journey impact understood
162	- [ ] Improvement is significant enough to build
163	
164	### 3. Scope Assessment
165	
166	- [ ] MVP clearly defined (must have vs nice to have)
167	- [ ] No feature creep detected
168	- [ ] Scope matches user needs, not technical possibilities
169	- [ ] "Solution looking for problem" anti-pattern avoided
170	
171	### 4. Success Criteria
172	
173	- [ ] Success metrics are user-focused (not just technical)
174	- [ ] Metrics are measurable and have thresholds
175	- [ ] Evaluation timeline defined
176	- [ ] Failure criteria also defined
177	
178	## Output Format
179	
180	Return JSON:
181	
182	```json
183	{
184	  "agent": "product-manager",
185	  "verdict": "APPROVED" | "NEEDS_REVISION",
186	  "use_case_analysis": {
187	    "total_use_cases": <number>,
188	    "clear": <number>,
189	    "needs_work": <number>,
190	    "missing_scenarios": ["list of gaps"]
191	  },
192	  "blockers": ["list of MUST fix issues"],
193	  "suggestions": ["nice to have improvements"],
194	  "questions": ["clarifications needed"]
195	}
196	```
197	````
198	
199	````
200	
201	### Architect Agent Prompt
202	
203	```markdown
204	You are the ARCHITECT AGENT reviewing a design document.
205	
206	## Your Task
207	Review this design for technical architecture soundness.

427	## Iteration Protocol
428	
429	### Round 1: Initial Review
430	
431	1. Spawn all five agents in parallel
432	2. Wait for all results
433	3. If all APPROVED → proceed
434	4. If any NEEDS_REVISION → consolidate and present
435	
436	### Rounds 2-3: Revision Cycles
437	
438	1. Present consolidated feedback to human
439	2. Human revises design document
440	3. Re-run all five agents on updated doc
441	4. Repeat until all approve
442	
443	### Round 4: Escalation
444	
445	If still not approved after 3 iterations:
446	
447	```markdown
448	## Design Review Gate: Escalation Required
449	
450	After 3 review cycles, the following blockers remain unresolved:
451	
452	### Architect Agent
453	- [blocker 1]
454	- [blocker 2]
455	
456	### Designer Agent
457	- [blocker 1]
458	
459	### CTO Agent
460	- [blocker 1]
461	- [blocker 2]
462	
463	### Options
464	1. **Override** - Proceed anyway (document technical debt)
465	2. **Defer** - Shelve this feature for later
466	3. **Revise** - Continue iterating on design
467	4. **Cancel** - Abandon this feature
468	
469	Please choose an option or provide additional context.
470	````

1	---
2	name: external-tools
3	description: Delegate implementation and review tasks to external AI CLI tools (Codex, Gemini) with cross-model adversarial review
4	auto_activate: false
5	triggers:
6	  - "use external tools"
7	  - "delegate to codex"
8	  - "delegate to gemini"
9	  - "cross-model review"
10	---
11	
12	# External Tools Skill
13	
14	## Purpose
15	
16	This skill delegates implementation and review tasks to external AI CLI tools (OpenAI Codex CLI, Google Gemini CLI), enabling cost savings through cheaper models and cross-model adversarial review that eliminates single-model blind spots. Three core principles govern every interaction: **one job per invocation** (an external tool implements OR reviews, never self-validates), **minimal permissions** (sandboxed execution scoped to the task's working directory with only the tool's own API key), and **the orchestrator verifies independently** (external tools report facts only, the orchestrator judges pass/fail).
17	
18	---
19	
20	## Prerequisites
21	
22	### Required Tools
23	
24	| Tool | Install | Auth |
25	|------|---------|------|
26	| OpenAI Codex CLI | `npm i -g @openai/codex` | API key or ChatGPT subscription |
27	| Google Gemini CLI | `npm i -g @google/gemini-cli` | Google login (free 1K req/day) or API key |
28	
29	Neither tool is strictly required. The skill adapts based on what is available (see Escalation Model below).
30	
31	### Configuration
32	
33	Per-project config at `.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml` (optional -- if absent, external tools are not used):
34	
35	```yaml
36	adapters:
37	  codex:
38	    enabled: true
39	    model: "gpt-5.3-codex"
40	    timeout_seconds: 300
41	    sandbox: docker          # docker | platform | none
42	  gemini:
43	    enabled: true
44	    model: "pro"
45	    timeout_seconds: 300
46	    sandbox: docker
47	
48	routing:
49	  default_implementer: "cheapest-available"
50	  escalation_order: ["codex", "gemini", "claude"]
51	
52	budget:
53	  per_task_usd: 2.00        # circuit breaker per task
54	  per_session_usd: 20.00    # circuit breaker per session
55	```
56	
57	### Fallback Behavior
58	
59	- **Both tools available**: Full cross-model delegation and review
60	- **One tool available**: Reduced chain with mutual review between the tool and Claude
61	- **No tools available**: Existing metaswarm behavior unchanged; skill is a no-op
62	
63	---
64	
65	## Quick Reference
66	
67	### Health Check
68	
69	```bash
70	# Check if Codex is installed, authenticated, and reachable
71	adapters/codex.sh health
72	
73	# Check Gemini
74	adapters/gemini.sh health
75	```
76	
77	Returns JSON with `status: "ready|degraded|unavailable"`.
78	
79	### Implement
80	
81	```bash
82	adapters/codex.sh implement \
83	  --worktree "/tmp/ext-codex-task-42" \
84	  --prompt-file "/private/tmp/xt-abc123/prompt.md" \
85	  --attempt 1 \
86	  --timeout 300
87	```
88	
89	### Review
90	
91	```bash
92	adapters/gemini.sh review \
93	  --worktree "/tmp/ext-codex-task-42" \
94	  --rubric-file "./rubrics/external-tool-review-rubric.md" \
95	  --spec-file "/private/tmp/xt-abc123/spec.md" \
96	  --timeout 300
97	```
98	
99	All commands return a uniform JSON envelope with facts only (exit code, files changed, cost, duration). The orchestrator interprets results -- adapters never self-judge.
100	
101	---
102	
103	## Workflow: Routing & Dispatch
104	
105	### Phase 0: Tool Discovery (Health Checks)
106	
107	Before dispatching any task, the orchestrator runs health checks to determine which tools are available:
108	
109	```bash
110	# Run health checks for all configured adapters
111	for adapter in adapters/*.sh; do
112	  result=$("$adapter" health)
113	  # Parse status: ready | degraded | unavailable
114	done
115	```
116	
117	Health is checked **per task dispatch**, not just at session start. Auth tokens can expire mid-session, network conditions change, and rate limits reset. A tool that was `ready` five minutes ago may now be `unavailable`.
118	
119	Build the available tools list:
120	
121	```python
122	available_tools = [t for t in adapters if t.health() == "ready"]
123	```
124	
125	### Phase 1: IMPLEMENT (External Tool or Claude)
126	
127	If an external tool is selected as implementer:
128	
129	1. **Create worktree** -- `git worktree add "$WORKTREE_PATH" -b "external/$TOOL/$TASK_ID"` for isolation
130	2. **Package context** -- Gather relevant files, spec, acceptance criteria into a prompt file in a secure temp dir (mode 700, trap cleanup). Token-budget the context to fit the model's window.
131	3. **Invoke** -- `safe_invoke()` with timeout wrapper; capture JSON output
132	4. **Scope check** -- Verify all file changes are within the declared file scope; revert any out-of-scope changes
133	5. **Capture output** -- Facts only: exit code, files changed, diff stats, cost, duration
134	
135	If Claude is selected (escalation or no external tools), use the existing `Task()` mechanism unchanged.
136	
137	### Phase 2: VALIDATE (Always Orchestrator -- Unchanged)
138	
139	The orchestrator independently runs all quality gates:
140	
141	- `npm test` / `npx vitest run`
142	- `npx tsc --noEmit`
143	- `npx eslint <changed-files>`
144	- Coverage enforcement via `.coverage-thresholds.json`
145	- File scope verification via `git diff --name-only`
146	
147	This phase is identical whether the implementer was an external tool or Claude. **Never trust the implementer's self-report.**
148	
149	### Phase 3: ADVERSARIAL REVIEW (Cross-Model)
150	
151	The key advantage of external tools: the writer is always reviewed by a **different model**.
152	
153	| Implementer | Reviewer 1 | Reviewer 2 |
154	|-------------|-----------|-----------|
155	| Codex | Gemini (via adapter) | Claude (via Task) |
156	| Gemini | Codex (via adapter) | Claude (via Task) |
157	| Claude | Codex (via adapter) | Gemini (via adapter) |
158	
159	Review is invoked via the adapter's `review` command. The orchestrator reads the reviewer's raw log and evaluates it independently -- the adapter never returns a pass/fail verdict.
160	
161	### Phase 4: COMMIT (Unchanged)
162	
163	After passing adversarial review:
164	
165	1. Merge the worktree branch into the working branch
166	2. Clean up the worktree (`git worktree remove`)
167	3. Log the session for self-reflection
168	4. Commit with DoD verification in the commit message
169	
170	---
171	
172	## Escalation Model (Availability-Aware)
173	
174	The orchestrator adapts its escalation chain based on which tools passed their health checks.
175	
176	### Scenario 1: Two External Tools Available (5 attempts max)
177	
178	```
179	Model A implements (attempt 1)
180	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review (B + Claude)
181	  FAIL -> feedback to Model A
182	
183	Model A implements (attempt 2, with review feedback)
184	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review
185	  FAIL -> escalate to Model B
186	
187	Model B implements (attempt 1, with Model A's branch as reference)
188	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review (A + Claude)
189	  FAIL -> feedback to Model B
190	
191	Model B implements (attempt 2, with review feedback)
192	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review
193	  FAIL -> escalate to Claude
194	
195	Claude implements (with both branches as reference)
196	  Orchestrator validates + cross-model review (A + B)
197	  FAIL -> alert user with all branches, findings, CI results
198	```
199	
200	**Worst case: A(2) + B(2) + Claude(1) = 5 attempts before user alert.**
201	
202	### Scenario 2: One External Tool Available (3 attempts max)
203	
204	```
205	Model A implements (attempt 1)
206	  Orchestrator validates + review (Claude reviews)
207	  FAIL -> feedback to Model A
208	
209	Model A implements (attempt 2, with review feedback)
210	  Orchestrator validates + review (Claude reviews)
211	  FAIL -> escalate to Claude
212	
213	Claude implements (with Model A's branch as reference)
214	  Orchestrator validates + review (Model A reviews)
215	  FAIL -> alert user
216	```
217	
218	**Worst case: A(2) + Claude(1) = 3 attempts before user alert.**
219	
220	### Scenario 3: No External Tools Available
221	
222	```
223	Existing metaswarm behavior unchanged.
224	Claude implements via Task() mechanism.
225	Standard adversarial review (fresh Task() instance).
226	```
227	
228	No change to the current workflow. The skill is a no-op.
229	
230	---
231	
232	## Routing Logic
233	
234	The orchestrator selects the implementer for each task using cheapest-available selection:
235	
236	```python
237	available_tools = [t for t in adapters if t.health() == "ready"]
238	
239	if len(available_tools) == 2:
240	    implementer = cheapest(available_tools)
241	    reviewers = [other_tool, claude]
242	    escalation = [implementer, other_tool, claude, user]
243	elif len(available_tools) == 1:
244	    implementer = available_tools[0]
245	    reviewers = [implementer, claude]  # mutual review
246	    escalation = [implementer, claude, user]
247	else:
248	    # pure metaswarm, no change
249	    implementer = claude
250	    reviewers = [claude_fresh_task]
251	    escalation = [claude, user]
252	```
253	
254	**Selection criteria for `cheapest()`**:
255	
256	1. Pick the tool with lower estimated cost per invocation (based on model pricing and average token usage from session logs)
257	2. On tie, prefer the tool with higher historical success rate for this task type
258	3. If no historical data, prefer Gemini (free tier) over Codex
259	
260	---
261	
262	## Prompt File Format
263	
264	The prompt file is the key interface between the orchestrator and external tools. It must be **self-contained** -- the external tool has no access to BEADS, knowledge base, or conversation history.
265	
266	```markdown
267	# Task: [task title]
268	
269	## Acceptance Criteria
270	- [ ] criterion 1
271	- [ ] criterion 2
272	- [ ] criterion 3
273	
274	## Context
275	[relevant file contents, token-budgeted]
276	[prioritized: changed files > test files > imports > surrounding context]
277	
278	## Coding Standards
279	[extracted from project's CLAUDE.md / coding standards guide]
280	[language-specific rules, naming conventions, patterns]
281	
282	## Test Expectations
283	[what tests should pass after implementation]
284	[coverage requirements from .coverage-thresholds.json]
285	[test runner command: e.g., npx vitest run]
286	
287	## Previous Attempt (if retry)
288	[review feedback from last attempt -- what to fix]
289	[DO NOT include full prior output, only the actionable feedback summary]
290	
291	## Prior Model's Attempt (if escalation)
292	[diff summary from previous model's branch -- what was tried, why it failed]
293	[DO NOT include full branch, only the relevant diff summary]
294	```
295	
296	**Token budgeting rules** (handled by `package_context()` in `_common.sh`):
297	
298	- Estimate tokens per file using chars/4 heuristic
299	- Prioritize context: changed files > test files > imports > surrounding context
300	- Truncate to the model's context budget (configurable per adapter)
301	- On retry: include review feedback summary, not full prior output
302	- On escalation: include prior branch diff summary, not full branch
303	
304	---
305	
306	## Error Handling
307	
308	When an adapter returns `exit_code != 0`, the `error_type` field determines the orchestrator's response:
309	
310	| Error Type | Meaning | Orchestrator Action |
311	|---|---|---|
312	| `tool_not_installed` | Binary not found | Skip adapter, try next in escalation chain |
313	| `auth_expired` | API key invalid or expired | Escalate to user with setup instructions |
314	| `auth_missing` | No API key configured | Escalate to user with setup instructions |
315	| `network_error` | Cannot reach API | Retry with exponential backoff, then skip adapter |
316	| `rate_limited` | API rate limit hit | Wait and retry (respect Retry-After if provided) |
317	| `timeout` | Tool exceeded time limit | Retry once with increased timeout, then skip adapter |
318	| `context_too_large` | Input exceeds context window | Reduce context via `package_context()`, retry |
319	| `cost_limit_exceeded` | Would exceed budget | Skip adapter, alert user about budget status |
320	| `tool_crash` | Unexpected exit | Retry once, then skip adapter |
321	| `output_parse_error` | Malformed JSON output | Log raw output for debugging, treat as failure |
322	
323	**General rules:**
324	
325	- "Skip adapter" means move to the next tool in the escalation chain, not abort the task
326	- All errors are logged to `~/.claude/sessions/` for self-reflection
327	- Auth errors always escalate to the user -- the orchestrator cannot fix credentials
328	- Network and rate-limit errors get transient-failure treatment (backoff + retry)
329	
330	---
331	
332	## Budget Enforcement
333	
334	Two circuit breakers prevent runaway costs:
335	
336	### Per-Task Budget (`per_task_usd`)
337	
338	Before each adapter invocation, the orchestrator checks cumulative cost for the current task:
339	
340	```
341	if task_cost_so_far + estimated_cost > per_task_usd:
342	    error_type = "cost_limit_exceeded"
343	    skip adapter, try next in escalation chain (or Claude)
344	```
345	
346	Default: `$2.00` per task.
347	
348	### Per-Session Budget (`per_session_usd`)
349	
350	Before each adapter invocation, the orchestrator checks cumulative cost across all tasks in the session:
351	
352	```
353	if session_cost_so_far + estimated_cost > per_session_usd:
354	    error_type = "cost_limit_exceeded"
355	    alert user: "Session budget exhausted. External tools disabled for remaining tasks."
356	    fall back to Claude for all remaining work
357	```
358	
359	Default: `$20.00` per session.
360	
361	### Cost Tracking
362	
363	Cost is extracted post-execution from the adapter's JSON output (`cost.input_tokens`, `cost.output_tokens`) and converted to USD using known model pricing. Cost estimation is approximate (~15-30% variance due to different tokenizers per model). There is no pre-execution cost prediction in v1 -- cost is measured after execution, not capped before.
364	
365	---
366	
367	## Anti-Patterns
368	
369	| # | Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | What to Do Instead |
370	|---|---|---|---|
371	| 1 | **Trusting external tool self-reports** -- tool says "all tests pass" and orchestrator believes it | External tools can hallucinate, skip steps, or misinterpret results; same reasoning as the no-self-certification rule for subagents | Orchestrator runs Phase 2 (VALIDATE) independently; adapter output contains facts only, never verdicts |
372	| 2 | **Skipping cross-model review** -- "the external tool's code looks fine, committing directly" | Single-model blind spots are the whole reason this skill exists; bypassing review defeats the purpose | Always run Phase 3 with a different model as reviewer; cross-model review is mandatory |
373	| 3 | **Running without timeout** -- invoking an adapter without `safe_invoke()` timeout wrapper | External CLIs can hang on rate limits, network issues, or infinite loops; an unmonitored invocation blocks the entire pipeline | Always use `safe_invoke()` with the configured `timeout_seconds`; handle timeout as a retryable error |
374	| 4 | **Passing the full environment** -- running the external tool with the orchestrator's full env vars | Leaks API keys, session tokens, and internal state to an external process that only needs its own credentials | Use `env -i` to pass only `HOME`, `PATH`, and the tool's own API key; see Minimal Environment in the design doc |
375	| 5 | **Working in the main repo** -- running external tools directly in the project working directory instead of a worktree | Concurrent invocations corrupt each other's state; failed attempts leave dirty files; no clean rollback path | Always create an isolated git worktree per invocation; merge only after all phases pass |
376	
377	---
378	
379	## Logging
380	
381	All adapter invocations produce structured session log entries in `~/.claude/sessions/`:
382	
383	```
384	Session log entry:
385	  - timestamp, tool, command, model, attempt
386	  - prompt content hash (what we asked)
387	  - output JSON (facts: exit code, files changed, cost)
388	  - CI gate results (from orchestrator's Phase 2)
389	  - review output (from cross-model Phase 3)
390	  - outcome: success | retry | escalated | user_alert
391	  - cost (tokens used, estimated USD)
392	```
393	
394	These logs feed the existing `/self-reflect` pipeline. Over time, the knowledge base accumulates routing intelligence:
395	
396	- **Patterns**: "Codex excels at single-file TypeScript implementations"
397	- **Gotchas**: "Gemini tends to skip error handling in async functions"
398	- **Routing decisions**: "Route database migration tasks directly to Claude (external tools fail 80%)"
399	
400	Future routing decisions improve automatically as the knowledge base grows from session log analysis.
401

1	---
2	name: handling-pr-comments
3	description: Address PR review feedback systematically — fetch inline comments, review bodies, handle outside-diff-range comments, resolve threads with proper attribution
4	---
5	
6	# handling-pr-comments
7	
8	Use when addressing PR review feedback, after receiving review comments from CodeRabbit, Cursor, or human reviewers - ensures systematic responses to each comment thread with proper attribution and thread resolution.
9	
10	## When to Activate
11	
12	Activate this skill when ANY of these conditions are true:
13	
14	- User asks to "address PR comments" or "handle review feedback"
15	- User mentions CodeRabbit, Cursor bot, or reviewer comments
16	- User is working on fixes requested in a PR review
17	- User asks to "check PR comments" or "respond to reviewers"
18	- After making fixes to address review feedback
19	
20	## CRITICAL: The Complete Workflow
21	
22	**Most developers forget steps 4-6. This skill ensures they happen.**
23	
24	### Phase 1: Discover and Filter Comments
25	
26	Run the filtering script to identify actionable comments:
27	
28	```bash
29	# Filter actionable vs non-actionable comments
30	bin/pr-comments-filter.sh <PR_NUMBER>
31	```
32	
33	This script:
34	
35	- Filters out non-actionable comments (confirmations, acknowledgments, fingerprinting)
36	- Categorizes actionable comments by priority (Critical → Low)
37	- Shows comment IDs and details for processing
38	
39	### Phase 2: Triage Actionable Comments
40	
41	The filter script categorizes by priority:
42	
43	| Priority        | Marker                                                   | Action           |
44	| --------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------- |
45	| CRITICAL        | `_⚠️ Potential issue_ \| _🔴 Critical_`                  | Fix immediately  |
46	| HIGH            | `_⚠️ Potential issue_ \| _🟠 Major_`                     | Fix before merge |
47	| MEDIUM          | `_🟡 Minor_` or `_🛠️ Refactor suggestion_ \| _🟠 Major_` | Should fix       |
48	| LOW             | `_🔵 Trivial_` / `_🧹 Nitpick_`                          | Fix if quick     |
49	| HUMAN           | Non-bot comments                                         | Always process   |
50	
51	For each actionable comment, further categorize as:
52	
53	1. **Bug/Issue** - Must fix
54	2. **Enhancement** - Should fix
55	3. **Nitpick** - Nice to fix
56	4. **Question** - Needs clarification
57	5. **Intentional** - Decline with explanation
58	6. **Out-of-Scope** - See Phase 2b
59	
60	### Phase 2b: Extract "Outside Diff Range" Comments from Review Bodies (CRITICAL)
61	
62	**COMMONLY MISSED**: CodeRabbit posts "Outside diff range" comments in the **review body**, not as inline threads. These are actionable feedback that MUST be addressed.
63	
64	```bash
65	# Extract Outside diff range comments from review bodies
66	PR_NUMBER=<number>
67	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
68	REPO_NAME=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
69	
70	echo "=== OUTSIDE DIFF RANGE COMMENTS ==="
71	gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/reviews" --paginate | \
72	  jq -r '.[] | select(.body | test("Outside diff range"; "i")) |
73	    "Review ID: \(.id)\n\(.body)\n---"'
74	```
75	
76	**These comments are NOT in threads** - they cannot be replied to inline. You must:
77	
78	1. Parse the file paths and line numbers from the review body
79	2. Address the feedback in your code
80	3. Commit and push
81	4. Leave a general PR comment acknowledging you addressed them
82	
83	### Phase 2c: Handle Other Out-of-Scope Comments
84	
85	Run the out-of-scope detection script:
86	
87	```bash
88	# Detect out-of-scope comments
89	bin/pr-comments-out-of-scope.sh <PR_NUMBER>
90	```
91	
92	This script detects comments that:
93	
94	- Reference lines NOT in the PR diff
95	- Are marked "outdated" by GitHub (GraphQL `isOutdated` flag)
96	- Are general PR discussion comments
97	
98	**IMPORTANT**: Treat out-of-scope comments as **IN SCOPE** by default.
99	
100	#### Evaluation Process
101	
102	Use **ultrathink** to evaluate each out-of-scope comment:
103	
104	```text
105	ultrathink: Analyze this out-of-scope review comment:
106	- What is the reviewer asking for?
107	- How complex is this change? (lines of code, files affected)
108	- Does it require refactoring other systems?
109	- Can I complete this in under 30 minutes?
110	- Are there any risks or dependencies?
111	
112	Recommend: FIX_NOW or CREATE_ISSUE
113	```
114	
115	#### Decision Matrix
116	
117	| Criteria                                      | Action                                    |
118	| --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
119	| Simple fix (< 30 min, < 3 files, no refactor) | **FIX_NOW** - Make the change immediately |
120	| Medium complexity (unclear scope)             | **ASK_USER** - Present options            |
121	| Major refactor (multiple systems, risky)      | **CREATE_ISSUE** - Document for follow-up |
122	
123	#### For FIX_NOW
124	
125	1. Make the fix
126	2. Commit with descriptive message
127	3. Push to the PR branch
128	4. Reply: "Fixed in commit <hash>. This was outside the original PR scope but straightforward to address."
129	5. Resolve the thread
130	
131	#### For CREATE_ISSUE
132	
133	1. Create GitHub issue with context
134	2. Reply: "Created issue #<number> to track this."
135	3. Resolve the thread
136	
137	### Phase 3: Make Fixes
138	
139	Fix the actual code issues. Commit and push.
140	
141	### Phase 4: RESPOND TO EACH THREAD (Often Forgotten!)
142	
143	**After pushing fixes, respond to EACH comment thread individually:**
144	
145	```bash
146	PR_NUMBER=<number>
147	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
148	REPO_NAME=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
149	CURRENT_USER=$(gh api user -q '.login')
150	COMMENT_ID=<id-from-filter-script>
151	
152	gh api "/repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments/$COMMENT_ID/replies" \
153	  -X POST \
154	  -f body="Fixed in commit $(git rev-parse --short HEAD).
155	
156	*(Response by Claude on behalf of @$CURRENT_USER)*"
157	```
158	
159	### Phase 5: Resolve ALL Threads
160	
161	**Every thread must be resolved after responding.** Use GraphQL to resolve:
162	
163	```bash
164	THREAD_ID="PRRT_kwDOK-xA485..."  # From GraphQL query
165	
166	gh api graphql -f query='mutation {
167	  resolveReviewThread(input: {threadId: "'"$THREAD_ID"'"}) {
168	    thread { id isResolved }
169	  }
170	}'
171	```
172	
173	### Phase 6: Handle Threads That Can't Be Resolved
174	
175	1. **Query the comment author** asking for specific follow-up
176	2. **Do NOT leave unresolved** - either resolve after responding, or ask for clarification
177	3. If waiting for author response, mark as needing user input
178	
179	### Phase 7: Post-Push Iteration Check (MANDATORY)
180	
181	**THE #1 WORKFLOW FAILURE: Stopping after Phase 5-6 without checking for NEW comments.**
182	
183	Automated reviewers (CodeRabbit, Cursor) analyze EVERY commit you push. They post NEW comments during/after their check runs.
184	
185	```bash
186	# STEP 1: Wait for ALL CI/CD checks to complete
187	PR_NUMBER=<number>
188	echo "Waiting for CI/CD checks to complete..."
189	gh pr checks $PR_NUMBER --watch
190	
191	# STEP 2: Check for NEW comments since your last response
192	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
193	REPO_NAME=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
194	CURRENT_USER=$(gh api user -q '.login')
195	
196	# Get timestamp of your last reply
197	LAST_REPLY=$(gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments" --paginate | \
198	  jq -r "[.[] | select(.user.login == \"$CURRENT_USER\") | select(.in_reply_to_id)] | sort_by(.created_at) | last | .created_at")
199	
200	# Handle case where user has no previous replies
201	if [ -z "$LAST_REPLY" ] || [ "$LAST_REPLY" = "null" ]; then
202	  echo "No previous replies found - checking all comments as new"
203	  LAST_REPLY="1970-01-01T00:00:00Z"  # Unix epoch - treat all comments as new
204	fi
205	
206	# Check for new comments after that time
207	NEW_COUNT=$(gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/comments" --paginate | \
208	  jq -r --arg time "$LAST_REPLY" '[.[] | select(.in_reply_to_id == null) | select(.created_at > $time)] | length')
209	
210	# STEP 3: Also check review bodies for new "Outside diff range" comments
211	NEW_REVIEWS=$(gh api "repos/$OWNER/$REPO_NAME/pulls/$PR_NUMBER/reviews" --paginate | \
212	  jq -r --arg time "$LAST_REPLY" '[.[] | select(.submitted_at > $time) | select(.body | test("Outside diff range"; "i"))] | length')
213	
214	TOTAL_NEW=$((NEW_COUNT + NEW_REVIEWS))
215	
216	if [ "$TOTAL_NEW" -gt 0 ]; then
217	  echo "$NEW_COUNT NEW INLINE COMMENT(S) + $NEW_REVIEWS NEW REVIEW BODY COMMENT(S) DETECTED"
218	  echo "ACTION: Return to Phase 1 and iterate"
219	else
220	  echo "No new comments - safe to proceed to verification"
221	fi
222	```
223	
224	**If NEW comments found**: Return to Phase 1. DO NOT proceed to verification.
225	
226	**Iteration Loop**:
227	
228	```text
229	REPEAT:
230	  Phase 1: Discover comments
231	  Phase 2: Triage
232	  Phase 3: Fix
233	  Phase 4: Respond
234	  Phase 5: Resolve threads
235	  Phase 6: Handle unclear threads
236	  Phase 7: Check for NEW comments after push
237	
238	  IF new comments found → GO TO Phase 1
239	  IF no new comments → proceed to verification
240	```
241	
242	## Response Templates
243	
244	### For Fixes Made
245	
246	```text
247	Fixed in commit <hash>.
248	
249	*(Response by Claude on behalf of @username)*
250	```
251	
252	### For Acknowledged Nitpicks
253	
254	```text
255	Acknowledged - this is a valid suggestion. Deferring to a future cleanup PR to keep this PR focused.
256	
257	*(Response by Claude on behalf of @username)*
258	```
259	
260	### For Intentional Decisions
261	
262	```text
263	This is intentional because [reason]. The [thing] is designed to [explanation].
264	
265	*(Response by Claude on behalf of @username)*
266	```
267	
268	## Mandatory Pre-Completion Check
269	
270	**BLOCKING: You MUST run this script and show its output before declaring ANY PR ready:**
271	
272	```bash
273	bin/pr-comments-check.sh <PR_NUMBER>
274	```
275	
276	This script:
277	
278	- Returns exit code 0 if all comments addressed
279	- Returns exit code 1 if ANY unaddressed comments exist
280	- Shows status for each comment
281	
282	**If the script shows ANY unaddressed comments, you are NOT done.** Address each unaddressed comment:
283	
284	1. If actionable → Fix it and reply confirming the fix
285	2. If out-of-scope → Reply explaining deferral (create issue if needed)
286	3. If disagree → Reply with reasoning
287	4. **NEVER ignore silently**
288	
289	**You must show the script output in your response** as proof that all comments are addressed. Example:
290	
291	```
292	Checking PR #908 for unaddressed comments...
293	
294	=== Inline Code Review Comments ===
295	Comment 123 by cursor[bot] - 1 reply(s) [OK]
296	Comment 456 by coderabbitai[bot] - 1 reply(s) [OK]
297	
298	=== General PR Discussion Comments ===
299	coderabbitai[bot]: <!-- summary -->...
300	
301	All inline review comments have been addressed
302	```
303	
304	A PR is NOT ready until this script returns success.
305	
306	## Verification Checklist
307	
308	Before declaring PR comments handled:
309	
310	- [ ] Ran `bin/pr-comments-filter.sh <PR>` to identify actionable comments
311	- [ ] **CRITICAL**: Extracted "Outside diff range" comments from review bodies (Phase 2b)
312	- [ ] Ran `bin/pr-comments-out-of-scope.sh <PR>` to find other out-of-scope feedback
313	- [ ] Code fixes have been made and pushed
314	- [ ] Each comment thread has a response posted
315	- [ ] "Outside diff range" comments addressed with a general PR comment
316	- [ ] **POST-PUSH CHECK**: Waited for CI/CD to complete, checked for NEW comments
317	- [ ] **NO new comments found** after the post-push check (iterate if found)
318	- [ ] **ALL threads have been resolved** (no unresolved threads remaining)
319	- [ ] All responses include proper attribution
320	- [ ] Out-of-scope comments have been either fixed OR have GitHub issues created
321	
322	**DO NOT skip the "Outside diff range" check (Phase 2b) - this is the #2 cause of incomplete PR handling.**
323	
324	## Reference
325	
326	For the complete detailed workflow with all edge cases and troubleshooting, see:
327	the `/metaswarm:handle-pr-comments` command
328

1	---
2	name: migrate
3	description: Migrate from npm-installed metaswarm to the marketplace plugin — removes redundant files with safety checks
4	---
5	
6	# Migration Skill
7	
8	Migrate a project from npm-installed metaswarm (`npx metaswarm init`) to the marketplace plugin. Removes redundant embedded files with a safety protocol that prevents data loss.
9	
10	**When to use**: The SessionStart hook detects `.claude/plugins/metaswarm/.claude-plugin/plugin.json` (legacy embedded plugin) and recommends running this skill.
11	
12	---
13	
14	## IMPORTANT: Safety & Messaging Guidelines
15	
16	**The user MUST understand this before any file list is shown:**
17	
18	Before presenting any migration preview or file list, ALWAYS lead with this framing:
19	
20	> **What this migration does**: The marketplace plugin now provides all the skills, commands, rubrics, and guides that were previously copied into your project directory. This migration simply removes those redundant copies — the originals now live in the plugin itself.
21	>
22	> **Nothing is lost**: Your project-specific files (CLAUDE.md, .coverage-thresholds.json, .beads/, bin/, scripts/) are NEVER touched. Only duplicate metaswarm framework files are removed.
23	>
24	> **Fully reversible**: All removals are staged with `git rm` (not permanently deleted). Before you commit:
25	> - Undo everything: `git restore --staged . && git checkout -- .`
26	> - After committing: `git revert HEAD`
27	>
28	> **No commit is made automatically** — you review and commit when you're satisfied.
29	
30	**Tone**: Be reassuring, not alarming. Say "cleaning up XX redundant copies" not "deleting XX files". Say "these files now live in the plugin" not "these files will be removed". Frame the migration as housekeeping, not destruction.
31	
32	**When showing file counts**: If there are many files (e.g., 40+), explain that the large count is because the old npm installer copied the entire plugin framework into `.claude/plugins/metaswarm/` — that one directory accounts for most of the count, and it's all framework code that's now served directly by the plugin.
33	
34	---
35	
36	## Step 1: Pre-flight Check
37	
38	1. Confirm this skill is running from the marketplace plugin (if this skill loaded, the plugin is active)
39	2. Read `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` -- if `"distribution": "plugin"` is already set, inform the user migration was already completed and exit
40	3. Verify `.claude/plugins/metaswarm/.claude-plugin/plugin.json` exists -- if not, there is nothing to migrate; inform the user and exit
41	
42	If the plugin is not loaded, the user needs to install it first: `/plugin marketplace add dsifry/metaswarm-marketplace`
43	
44	---
45	
46	## Step 2: Inventory Legacy Files
47	
48	Scan for files installed by `npx metaswarm init` that are now provided by the marketplace plugin.
49	
50	**Candidates for removal:**
51	
52	| Category | Path pattern |
53	|---|---|
54	| Embedded plugin | `.claude/plugins/metaswarm/` (entire directory) |
55	| Rubrics | `.claude/rubrics/*.md` |
56	| Guides | `.claude/guides/*.md` |
57	| Old commands | `.claude/commands/metaswarm-setup.md`, `.claude/commands/metaswarm-update-version.md` |
58	
59	**NEVER removed** (project-local files): `CLAUDE.md`, `.coverage-thresholds.json`, `.metaswarm/project-profile.json`, `.beads/`, `bin/`, `scripts/`, `.github/workflows/`, `.claude/commands/` shims.
60	
61	---
62	
63	## Step 3: Content Verification
64	
65	For each removal candidate, verify it is an unmodified metaswarm file using SHA-256 hash comparison.
66	
67	**Hash protocol:**
68	1. Read file content
69	2. Normalize line endings to LF (`\r\n` -> `\n`, `\r` -> `\n`)
70	3. Strip trailing whitespace from each line
71	4. Strip trailing newlines
72	5. Compute SHA-256 of normalized content
73	6. Compare against hash of the corresponding file from the marketplace plugin's own directories (rubrics/, guides/, etc.)
74	
75	Computing hashes from the plugin's live files ensures the hash list stays current -- no hardcoded hashes that drift.
76	
77	**Classification:**
78	
79	| Result | Action |
80	|---|---|
81	| Hash matches | **Unmodified** -- add to deletion list |
82	| Hash differs | **User-modified** -- flag for user decision, never auto-delete |
83	| Not in hash list | **Unknown file** -- skip entirely |
84	
85	---
86	
87	## Step 4: Dry Run Preview
88	
89	Display the complete migration plan before any changes. **Lead with the safety framing from the guidelines above**, then show the preview:
90	
91	```
92	## Migration Preview
93	
94	These are redundant copies of framework files that are now provided by the plugin.
95	All removals are staged (not committed) — you can undo everything before committing.
96	
97	### Redundant framework files to clean up (XX files)
98	These are unmodified copies that the plugin now provides directly:
99	- .claude/plugins/metaswarm/ (embedded plugin copy — XX files, now served by marketplace plugin)
100	- .claude/rubrics/<each matching file> (now in plugin's rubrics/)
101	- .claude/guides/<each matching file> (now in plugin's guides/)
102	- .claude/commands/metaswarm-setup.md (replaced by plugin command)
103	- .claude/commands/metaswarm-update-version.md (replaced by plugin command)
104	
105	### Files you've customized (require your decision)
106	- .claude/rubrics/code-review-rubric.md (MODIFIED — your changes are preserved until you decide)
107	
108	### Your project files (NEVER touched)
109	- CLAUDE.md, .coverage-thresholds.json, .metaswarm/, .beads/, bin/, scripts/
110	
111	### What gets added
112	- 6 command shims in .claude/commands/ (thin wrappers that route to plugin commands)
113	- .metaswarm/project-profile.json updated with "distribution": "plugin"
114	
115	### How to undo (before committing)
116	git restore --staged . && git checkout -- .
117	```
118	
119	---
120	
121	## Step 5: User Confirmation
122	
123	Use `AskUserQuestion`:
124	- Remind the user: "This stages the cleanup — nothing is committed yet. You can undo with `git restore --staged . && git checkout -- .`"
125	- Ask: "Ready to clean up the redundant framework files?" (not "Ready to delete files?")
126	- For each user-modified file, ask individually: keep, remove, or show diff
127	- If the user chooses "diff", display the difference between their version and the plugin's, then re-ask
128	
129	---
130	
131	## Step 6: Git Safety
132	
133	Before executing removals:
134	1. Run `git status` to check for uncommitted changes
135	2. If uncommitted changes exist, warn: "Recommended to commit or stash before migrating so changes are in their own commit. Continue anyway?"
136	3. If the user declines, exit
137	
138	---
139	
140	## Step 7: Cleanup
141	
142	**Announce what you're doing**: Before running any commands, say:
143	> "Staging the redundant files for removal. These are all framework copies — your project files are untouched. Nothing is committed yet."
144	
145	**Git-tracked files** -- use `git rm` (staged, reversible via `git checkout`):
146	```bash
147	git rm -rf .claude/plugins/metaswarm/
148	git rm .claude/rubrics/<each confirmed file>
149	git rm .claude/guides/<each confirmed file>
150	git rm .claude/commands/metaswarm-setup.md
151	git rm .claude/commands/metaswarm-update-version.md
152	```
153	
154	**After running**: Reassure: "Done — XX files staged for removal. These are only staged, not committed. Run `git status` to see the staged changes, or `git restore --staged . && git checkout -- .` to undo."
155	
156	**Untracked files** -- use `rm -f` (unlikely but handle gracefully).
157	
158	**Empty directories** -- remove `.claude/rubrics/` and `.claude/guides/` if empty after cleanup. Do NOT remove `.claude/commands/` (shims remain).
159	
160	---
161	
162	## Step 8: Command Shim Creation
163	
164	Write 6 shims to `.claude/commands/` (same as setup skill):
165	
166	| Shim | Routes to |
167	|---|---|
168	| `start-task.md` | `/metaswarm:start-task` |
169	| `prime.md` | `/metaswarm:prime` |
170	| `review-design.md` | `/metaswarm:review-design` |
171	| `self-reflect.md` | `/metaswarm:self-reflect` |
172	| `pr-shepherd.md` | `/metaswarm:pr-shepherd` |
173	| `brainstorm.md` | `/metaswarm:brainstorm` |
174	
175	Each shim:
176	```markdown
177	<!-- Created by metaswarm setup. Routes to the metaswarm plugin. Safe to delete if you uninstall metaswarm. -->
178	Invoke the `/metaswarm:<command-name>` skill with any arguments the user provided.
179	```
180	
181	If a shim already exists with different content, ask before overwriting.
182	
183	---
184	
185	## Step 9: Profile Update
186	
187	Merge these fields into `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` (preserve existing fields):
188	```json
189	{
190	  "distribution": "plugin",
191	  "migrated_at": "<ISO 8601 timestamp>",
192	  "migrated_from": "npm"
193	}
194	```
195	
196	---
197	
198	## Step 10: Post-Migration Summary
199	
200	Display what was done and next steps:
201	```
202	## Migration Complete
203	
204	Cleaned up XX redundant framework files that are now provided by the marketplace plugin.
205	Your project files (CLAUDE.md, .coverage-thresholds.json, .beads/, etc.) were not touched.
206	
207	### What changed
208	- Removed XX redundant framework copies (now served by plugin)
209	- Added 6 command shims to .claude/commands/
210	- Updated project profile to "distribution": "plugin"
211	
212	### Next steps
213	1. Review staged changes: `git diff --cached --stat`
214	2. Commit when satisfied: `git commit -m "chore: migrate metaswarm from npm to marketplace plugin"`
215	3. Verify everything works: try `/start-task`
216	
217	### If anything seems wrong
218	- Undo before committing: `git restore --staged . && git checkout -- .`
219	- Undo after committing: `git revert HEAD`
220	- Full re-install: `npx metaswarm install` (npm package still available)
221	```
222	
223	---
224	
225	## Rollback (Nothing Is Permanent)
226	
227	All removals use `git rm`, which only stages changes — files are NOT deleted from git history. The migration never auto-commits, so the user always has a chance to review and undo:
228	
229	- **Before committing** (full undo): `git restore --staged . && git checkout -- .`
230	- **After committing** (full undo): `git revert HEAD`
231	- **Single file recovery**: `git checkout HEAD~1 -- <path>`
232	- **Full re-install of old approach**: `npx metaswarm install` (npm package still published)
233	
234	---
235	
236	## Error Handling
237	
238	| Error | Action |
239	|---|---|
240	| `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` missing | Create with minimal fields, proceed |
241	| `git rm` fails on a file | Log error, skip file, continue |
242	| Permission denied | Warn user, skip file, continue |
243	| Plugin not loaded | Exit with install instructions |
244	| `metaswarm_version < 0.8.0` | Warn manual intervention may be needed |
245	
246	---
247	
248	## Anti-Patterns
249	
250	| Anti-Pattern | Do Instead |
251	|---|---|
252	| Auto-deleting modified files | Flag and ask explicitly |
253	| Deleting before confirming plugin works | Pre-flight check first |
254	| Using `rm -rf` on tracked files | Use `git rm` (reversible) |
255	| Skipping dry run preview | Always show full preview |
256	| Removing project-local files | Never touch CLAUDE.md, .beads/, bin/, scripts/ |
257	| Saying "deleting XX files" or "removing XX files" | Say "cleaning up XX redundant copies" |
258	| Showing file list without safety context | Always lead with the safety framing |
259	| Jumping straight to `git rm` commands | Explain what you're doing and why it's safe first |
