1	---
2	name: orchestrated-execution
3	description: 4-phase execution loop for work units - IMPLEMENT, VALIDATE, ADVERSARIAL REVIEW, COMMIT
4	auto_activate: false
5	triggers:
6	  - "orchestrated execution"
7	  - "4-phase loop"
8	  - "adversarial review"
9	---
10	
11	# Orchestrated Execution Skill
12	
13	**Core principle**: Trust nothing. Verify everything. Review adversarially.
14	
15	This skill defines a generalized 4-phase execution loop that any orchestrator can invoke when implementing work units. It replaces linear "implement then review" flows with a rigorous cycle that independently validates results and adversarially reviews against a written spec contract.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Coordination Mode Note
20	
21	This skill is mode-agnostic — the 4-phase execution loop works identically in both Task Mode and Team Mode. The differences:
22	
23	- **Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT)**: In Team Mode, the coding subagent may be a persistent teammate (retains context across work units). In Task Mode, it's a fresh `Task()` per work unit.
24	- **Phase 3 (ADVERSARIAL REVIEW)**: ALWAYS a fresh `Task()` instance in BOTH modes. Never a teammate, never resumed.
25	- **All quality gates**: Unchanged regardless of mode.
26	
27	See `./guides/agent-coordination.md` for full mode detection and coordination details.
28	
29	---
30	
31	## Plan Review Gate
32	
33	After drafting an implementation plan (Step 1: Plan Validation), submit it to the **Plan Review Gate** before presenting to the user. The gate spawns 3 adversarial reviewers (Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment) — all must PASS. See `skills/plan-review-gate/SKILL.md` for details.
34	
35	---
36	
37	## When to Use This Skill
38	
39	- **Complex tasks** decomposed into multiple work units
40	- **Tasks with a written spec** containing Definition of Done (DoD) items
41	- **Multi-agent orchestration** where subagents produce work that needs verification
42	- **High-stakes changes** where self-reported "it works" is insufficient
43	
44	**Do NOT use for**: Single-file bug fixes, copy changes, or tasks without a spec.
45	
46	---
47	
48	## 1. Plan Validation (Pre-Flight Checklist)
49	
50	Before submitting a plan to the Design Review Gate, the orchestrator MUST verify every item on this checklist. This prevents expensive design review cycles on fundamentally broken plans.
51	
52	> **Note**: The `Plan` subagent type cannot write files (it has read-only access by design). If you spawn an Architect as a Plan subagent, it will return the plan as text in its response. The orchestrator must write the plan to `PLAN.md` itself.
53	
54	### Architecture Checklist
55	- [ ] Every data access goes through a service layer (no direct DB calls from routes/handlers)
56	- [ ] Each work unit has a single responsibility (max ~5 files created/modified)
57	- [ ] Error handling strategy specified (typed error hierarchy, how errors cross layer boundaries)
58	- [ ] No hard-coded configuration — environment variables for all external service config
59	
60	### Dependency Graph Checklist
61	- [ ] Each WU's dependencies are minimal (only depends on what it actually imports/uses)
62	- [ ] No unnecessary serialization — WUs that CAN be parallel ARE marked parallel
63	- [ ] No circular dependencies
64	- [ ] Integration WUs exist to wire components into the app shell (not just built in isolation)
65	
66	### API Contract Checklist
67	If the plan includes HTTP endpoints or WebSocket protocols, verify:
68	- [ ] Every HTTP endpoint specifies: method, path, request schema, all response status codes, error response shapes
69	- [ ] WebSocket message types fully specified (client-to-server AND server-to-client message type tables)
70	- [ ] Protocol concerns documented: heartbeat, reconnection, acknowledgment strategy
71	- [ ] Response codes are explicit (not "returns the todo" but "returns 201 with the created todo")
72	
73	### Security Checklist
74	- [ ] Trust boundaries identified (which inputs are untrusted?)
75	- [ ] Input validation specified for every endpoint/handler (schema, max size)
76	- [ ] Rate limiting specified for expensive operations (AI API calls, file uploads)
77	- [ ] Authentication/authorization requirements documented
78	- [ ] Secrets management documented (.env pattern, .gitignore verification)
79	
80	### UI/UX Checklist
81	If the plan includes a user interface:
82	- [ ] User flows documented with trigger, steps, and visible outcome (see UI-FLOWS.md template)
83	- [ ] Text-based wireframes for each screen showing layout and interactive elements
84	- [ ] Empty states, loading states, and error states defined for each view
85	- [ ] Integration work units explicitly created to wire components into the app shell
86	- [ ] Component hierarchy documented (what renders what, where)
87	
88	### External Dependencies Checklist
89	- [ ] All external services identified (APIs, SDKs, third-party services)
90	- [ ] Required credentials/config documented (env var names, how to obtain)
91	- [ ] Human checkpoint planned BEFORE work units that depend on external services
92	- [ ] Graceful degradation specified for when credentials are missing
93	- [ ] `.env.example` includes all required env vars
94	
95	### Completeness Checklist
96	- [ ] All human checkpoints from the spec are included
97	- [ ] All features from the spec have at least one work unit
98	- [ ] Tooling is consistent (one package manager, matching config files across WUs)
99	- [ ] No WU exceeds ~5 files or ~3 distinct concerns
100	- [ ] WUs that are too large are split with explicit dependencies between parts
101	
102	**If any checklist item fails, fix the plan BEFORE submitting to Design Review Gate.**
103	
104	### Required Plan Sections
105	
106	Every plan submitted for Design Review MUST include these sections:
107	
108	1. **Work Unit Decomposition** — WU list with DoD items, file scopes, dependencies
109	2. **API Contract** (if applicable) — structured endpoint/protocol specs:
110	   ```markdown
111	   ### POST /api/todos
112	   - **Request Body**: `{ title: string }` (required, 1-500 chars, trimmed)
113	   - **Success**: `201 Created` -> `{ id, title, completed, createdAt, updatedAt }`
114	   - **Errors**: `400` (validation) / `500` (internal)
115	   ```
116	3. **Security Considerations** — trust boundaries, input validation table, rate limiting table, secrets management
117	4. **User Flows** (if UI exists) — text wireframes and interaction flows (use UI-FLOWS.md template)
118	5. **External Dependencies** — services, credentials, setup instructions
119	6. **Human Checkpoints** — named pause points with review criteria
120	
121	---
122	
123	## 2. Work Unit Decomposition
124	
125	A **work unit** is the atomic unit of orchestrated execution. Before entering the 4-phase loop, decompose the implementation plan into work units.
126	
127	### Work Unit Structure
128	
129	Each work unit contains:
130	
131	| Field | Description | Example |
132	| --- | --- | --- |
133	| **ID** | Unique identifier (BEADS task ID) | `bd-wu-001` |
134	| **Title** | Human-readable name | "Implement auth middleware" |
135	| **Spec** | Written specification with acceptance criteria | Link to design doc section |
136	| **DoD Items** | Enumerated, verifiable done criteria | `[ ] Middleware rejects expired tokens` |
137	| **Dependencies** | Other work units that must complete first | `[bd-wu-000]` |
138	| **File Scope** | Files this work unit may touch | `src/middleware/auth.ts, src/middleware/auth.test.ts` |
139	| **Human Checkpoint** | Whether to pause for human review after completion | `true` for risky changes |
140	
141	### Constructing Dependency Graphs
142	
143	Work units form a directed acyclic graph (DAG):
144	
145	```text
146	wu-001 (schema changes) ───┐
147	                            ├──→ wu-003 (API endpoints)  ───→ wu-005 (integration tests)
148	wu-002 (shared utilities) ──┘                                        │
149	                                                                     ▼
150	wu-004 (UI components)  ────────────────────────────────────→ wu-006 (e2e tests)
151	```
152	
153	**Rules for decomposition:**
154	
155	1. Each work unit has a **single responsibility** — one logical change
156	2. File scopes should **not overlap** between parallel work units
157	3. Dependencies must be **explicit** — no implicit ordering assumptions
158	4. Work units at the same depth with no interdependencies **run in parallel**
159	5. Each DoD item must be **independently verifiable** (not "code looks good")
160	
161	### Decomposition Template
162	
163	```bash
164	# Create work units as BEADS tasks under the epic
165	bd create "WU-001: <title>" --type task --parent <epic-id> \
166	  --description "Spec: <spec-section>\nDoD:\n- [ ] <item-1>\n- [ ] <item-2>\nFile scope: <files>\nCheckpoint: <yes/no>"
167	
168	# Set up dependencies
169	bd dep add <wu-003> <wu-001>
170	bd dep add <wu-003> <wu-002>
171	```
172	
173	---
174	
175	## 3. The 4-Phase Execution Loop
176	
177	For each work unit, execute these four phases in sequence. **Do not skip phases.** Do not combine phases. Do not proceed to the next phase until the current phase produces a clear outcome.
178	
179	```text
180	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
181	│                    4-PHASE EXECUTION LOOP                       │
182	│                                                                 │
183	│   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────┐ │
184	│   │ IMPLEMENT│───→│ VALIDATE │───→│  ADVERSARIAL │───→│COMMIT│ │
185	│   │          │    │          │    │    REVIEW     │    │      │ │
186	│   └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────┬───────┘    └──────┘ │
187	│        ▲                                 │                      │
188	│        │              FAIL               │                      │
189	│        └─────────────────────────────────┘                      │
190	│                                                                 │
191	│   On FAIL: fix → re-validate → FRESH review → max 3 → escalate │
192	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
193	```
194	
195	### Phase 1: IMPLEMENT
196	
197	The coding subagent executes against the work unit spec.
198	
199	**Orchestrator actions:**
200	
201	1. Spawn a coding subagent with the work unit spec, DoD items, file scope, and the **Project Context Document**
202	2. The subagent implements the change following TDD (test first, then implementation)
203	3. The subagent reports completion — **but the orchestrator does NOT trust this report**
204	
205	**Subagent spawn template:**
206	
207	```text
208	You are the CODER AGENT for work unit ${wuId}.
209	
210	## Spec
211	${spec}
212	
213	## Definition of Done
214	${dodItems.map((item, i) => `${i+1}. ${item}`).join('\n')}
215	
216	## File Scope
217	You may ONLY modify these files: ${fileScope.join(', ')}
218	
219	## Project Context
220	${projectContext}
221	
222	## Rules
223	- Follow TDD: write failing test first, then implement to make it pass
224	- Do NOT modify files outside your file scope
225	- Do NOT self-certify — the orchestrator will validate independently
226	- When complete, report what you changed and what tests you added
227	- NEVER use --no-verify on git commits — pre-commit hooks are mandatory
228	- NEVER use git push --force
229	- NEVER suppress linter/type errors with eslint-disable, @ts-ignore, or as any
230	- NEVER skip tests or claim "tests pass" without actually running them
231	```
232	
233	**Phase 1 output:** List of changed files and new tests.
234	
235	### Phase 2: VALIDATE
236	
237	The **orchestrator independently** runs quality gates. Never trust subagent self-reports.
238	
239	**Orchestrator actions (run these yourself, NOT via the coding subagent):**
240	
241	```bash
242	# 1. Type checking
243	npx tsc --noEmit
244	
245	# 2. Linting
246	npx eslint <changed-files>
247	
248	# 3. Run tests (full suite, not just new tests)
249	npx vitest run
250	
251	# 4. Coverage enforcement (BLOCKING — read .coverage-thresholds.json)
252	# If .coverage-thresholds.json exists, read the enforcement command and run it
253	# This is NOT optional. Coverage below threshold = VALIDATION FAIL.
254	if [ -f .coverage-thresholds.json ]; then
255	  CMD=$(node -e "console.log(JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('.coverage-thresholds.json','utf-8')).enforcement.command)")
256	  eval "$CMD"
257	fi
258	
259	# 5. Verify file scope was respected
260	git diff --name-only | while read file; do
261	  echo "$file" # Check each file is within the work unit's declared scope
262	done
263	```
264	
265	**Phase 2 outcomes:**
266	
267	- **All gates pass** → proceed to Phase 3
268	- **Any gate fails** → return to Phase 1 (the coding subagent fixes the issue)
269	- **File scope violated** → return to Phase 1 (subagent must revert out-of-scope changes)
270	
271	**Critical rule:** The orchestrator runs validation commands directly. The orchestrator does NOT ask the coding subagent "did the tests pass?" and accept the answer.
272	
273	### Phase 3: ADVERSARIAL REVIEW
274	
275	A **separate review subagent** checks the implementation against the spec contract. This is NOT the same as a collaborative code review — it's adversarial verification.
276	
277	**Key differences from collaborative review:**
278	
279	| Collaborative Review | Adversarial Review |
280	| --- | --- |
281	| APPROVED / CHANGES REQUIRED | PASS / FAIL |
282	| Subjective quality assessment | Binary spec compliance check |
283	| Reviewer suggests improvements | Reviewer finds contract violations |
284	| Same reviewer can re-review | Fresh reviewer required on re-review |
285	| Uses `code-review-rubric.md` | Uses `adversarial-review-rubric.md` |
286	
287	**Orchestrator actions:**
288	
289	1. Spawn a **new** review subagent in adversarial mode
290	2. Pass: the spec, the DoD items, and the diff (NOT the coding subagent's self-assessment)
291	3. The reviewer checks each DoD item with evidence (file:line references)
292	
293	**Reviewer spawn template:**
294	
295	```text
296	You are the ADVERSARIAL REVIEWER for work unit ${wuId}.
297	
298	## Mode
299	Adversarial — your job is to FIND FAILURES, not to approve.
300	
301	## Rubric
302	Read and follow: ./rubrics/adversarial-review-rubric.md
303	
304	## Spec
305	${spec}
306	
307	## Definition of Done
308	${dodItems.map((item, i) => `${i+1}. ${item}`).join('\n')}
309	
310	## What to Review
311	Run: git diff main..HEAD -- ${fileScope.join(' ')}
312	
313	## Rules
314	- Check EACH DoD item. Cite file:line evidence for PASS or expected-vs-found for FAIL.
315	- Any single BLOCKING issue means overall FAIL.
316	- You have NO context from previous reviews. Judge fresh.
317	- Do NOT suggest improvements. Only report PASS or FAIL with evidence.
318	```
319	
320	**Phase 3 outcomes:**
321	
322	- **PASS** (zero BLOCKING issues) → proceed to Phase 4
323	- **FAIL** (any BLOCKING issue) → return to Phase 1 with the failure report
324	
325	**Fresh reviewer rule:** On re-review after FAIL, the orchestrator MUST spawn a **new** review subagent. Never pass previous findings to the new reviewer. Never reuse the same reviewer instance. This prevents anchoring bias and ensures independent verification.
326	
327	### Phase 4: COMMIT
328	
329	Only after PASS from adversarial review.
330	
331	**Orchestrator actions:**
332	
333	```bash
334	# Stage only files within the work unit's file scope
335	git add <file-scope-files>
336	
337	# Commit with reference to work unit
338	git commit -m "feat(wu-${wuId}): <description>
339	
340	DoD items verified:
341	$(dodItems.map((item, i) => `- [x] ${item}`).join('\n'))
342	
343	Reviewed-by: adversarial-review (PASS)"
344	```
345	
346	**After commit:**
347	- Update BEADS task status: `bd close <wu-task-id> --reason "4-phase loop complete. PASS."`
348	- If this work unit has a **human checkpoint** flag, pause and report before continuing
349	- Update the **Project Context Document** with completed work unit details
350	
351	**After commit, update SERVICE-INVENTORY.md:**
352	If this work unit created or modified services, factories, database tables, or shared modules, update `SERVICE-INVENTORY.md` with the new entries. This document is read by subsequent coder agents to avoid duplicating existing services.
353	
354	---
355	
356	## 4. Quality Gate Enforcement
357	
358	Quality gates are BLOCKING STATE TRANSITIONS, not advisory recommendations. The orchestrator CANNOT advance to the next phase without gate passage.
359	
360	### State Machine
361	
362	```text
363	IMPLEMENT ──→ VALIDATE ──→ REVIEW ──→ COMMIT
364	                 │            │
365	                 ↓            ↓
366	              FAIL:         FAIL:
367	           fix + re-run   fix + re-validate
368	                          + FRESH re-review
369	                 │            │
370	              (max 3)      (max 3)
371	                 │            │
372	                 ↓            ↓
373	              ESCALATE     ESCALATE
374	             (to human)   (to human)
375	```
376	
377	### Transition Rules (MUST, not SHOULD)
378	
379	1. **IMPLEMENT → VALIDATE**: Always. No exceptions.
380	2. **VALIDATE → REVIEW**: ONLY if ALL validation checks pass:
381	   - Tests pass (exit code 0)
382	   - Coverage meets `.coverage-thresholds.json` thresholds
383	   - Type checking passes
384	   - Lint passes
385	   - File scope respected
386	3. **REVIEW → COMMIT**: ONLY if adversarial review returns PASS
387	4. **FAIL → retry**: Fix the issue, then re-run the FAILED gate (not skip it)
388	5. **Re-review after fix**: MUST spawn a FRESH reviewer (new instance, no memory)
389	6. **Max retries**: 3 attempts per gate, then ESCALATE to human with full failure history
390	
391	### On FAIL: Mandatory Re-Review Protocol
392	
393	1. Fix the issue identified by the reviewer
394	2. Re-run Phase 2 (VALIDATE) — ALL quality gates, not just the one that failed
395	3. **MANDATORY**: Spawn a NEW adversarial reviewer (fresh instance, no memory of previous review)
396	4. Only COMMIT after the fresh reviewer returns PASS
397	5. Max 3 retry cycles before ESCALATE to human
398	
399	Track each attempt visibly: "Re-review attempt 1/3", "Re-review attempt 2/3", etc.
400	
401	### What the Orchestrator MUST NOT Do
402	
403	- "Coverage is close enough at 92%, proceeding to commit"
404	- "Adversarial review found issues but they're minor, committing anyway"
405	- "Fix applied, skipping re-review since the fix is straightforward"
406	- "5 FAILs encountered, moving to next work unit without resolution"
407	- "Tests pass but coverage command failed — proceeding anyway"
408	
409	### What the Orchestrator MUST Do
410	
411	- "VALIDATION FAIL: coverage at 87%, threshold is 100%. Returning to IMPLEMENT."
412	- "ADVERSARIAL REVIEW FAIL. Spawning fresh reviewer for re-review. Attempt 2 of 3."
413	- "Max retries (3) exceeded for WU-004. Escalating to human with failure history."
414	- "Fix applied. Re-running validation. Re-running adversarial review with FRESH reviewer."
415	
416	---
417	
418	## 5. Parallel Work Unit Execution
419	
420	When multiple work units have no dependencies on each other, execute them in parallel — but with structured convergence points.
421	
422	```text
423	              ┌──── WU-001: IMPLEMENT ────┐
424	              │                            │
425	Fan-out ──────┼──── WU-002: IMPLEMENT ────┼──── Converge for VALIDATE
426	              │                            │
427	              └──── WU-003: IMPLEMENT ────┘
428	                                           │
429	              ┌──── WU-001: REVIEW ────────┤
430	              │                            │
431	Fan-out ──────┼──── WU-002: REVIEW ────────┼──── Sequential COMMIT
432	              │                            │
433	              └──── WU-003: REVIEW ────────┘
434	```
435	
436	**Rules for parallel execution:**
437	
438	1. **Fan-out implementations**: Spawn coding subagents for independent work units simultaneously
439	2. **Converge for validation**: Wait for ALL parallel implementations to complete, then validate each
440	3. **Fan-out reviews**: Spawn review subagents for each work unit simultaneously
441	4. **Sequential commits**: Commit work units one at a time to maintain clean git history
442	5. **If any FAIL**: Only re-run the failed work unit's loop — don't re-run passed units
443	
444	**Stale notification handling:** When parallel subagent results arrive after the orchestrator has moved past their work unit (e.g., at a checkpoint), acknowledge them briefly in one line. Do NOT print a full "still waiting at checkpoint" block for each stale notification — this clutters the conversation and wastes context window.
445	
446	---
447	
448	## 6. Project Context Document
449	
450	The orchestrator MUST maintain a project context document that grows with each work unit. This is passed to every coder subagent to prevent context loss.
451	
452	### Context Document Structure
453	
454	```markdown
455	# Project Context (Maintained by Orchestrator)
456	
457	## Tooling
458	- Package manager: <npm/pnpm/yarn>
459	- Test runner: <vitest/jest> (<config-file>)
460	- Linter: <eslint> (<config-file>)
461	- Build: <vite/webpack/tsc> (<config-file>)
462	
463	## Completed Work Units
464	| WU | Title | Key Files | Services Created |
465	|----|-------|-----------|-----------------|
466	
467	## Established Patterns
468	- <pattern-1>: <description>
469	- <pattern-2>: <description>
470	
471	## Active Services
472	See SERVICE-INVENTORY.md
473	```
474	
475	**Update rules:**
476	- After each Phase 4 (COMMIT), add the completed work unit to the table
477	- After each Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT), update patterns if new ones emerge
478	- Pass this document to every coder subagent alongside the work unit spec
479	
480	### Persisting to Disk (MANDATORY)
481	
482	The Project Context Document MUST be written to `.beads/context/project-context.md` and kept in sync with the in-memory version. This ensures the context survives context compaction and session boundaries.
483	
484	```bash
485	# Create directory if needed
486	mkdir -p .beads/context
487	
488	# Write/update after each Phase 4 (COMMIT) and at orchestration start
489	# The file should always reflect the current state of execution
490	```
491	
492	**When to write:**
493	- At orchestration start (initial creation)
494	- After each Phase 4 (COMMIT) — add the completed work unit
495	- After each Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT) — update patterns if new ones emerge
496	- At human checkpoints — snapshot current state
497	
498	**The file is NOT committed to git** during execution — it's a working document. It gets cleaned up after the PR is created (or left for the next session if interrupted).
499	
500	---
501	
502	## 6.5. Plan Persistence (Context Recovery)
503	
504	Approved plans and execution state are persisted to `.beads/` so agents can recover after context compaction or session interruption.
505	
506	### What Gets Persisted
507	
508	| File | Contents | Written When |
509	|------|----------|-------------|
510	| `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` | The adversarially-reviewed, user-approved implementation plan | After plan review gate PASS + user approval |
511	| `.beads/context/project-context.md` | Project Context Document (tooling, completed WUs, patterns) | After each Phase 4 COMMIT |
512	| `.beads/context/execution-state.md` | Current work unit, phase, retry count | After each phase transition |
513	
514	### Writing the Approved Plan
515	
516	After the Plan Review Gate approves a plan AND the user approves it, persist immediately:
517	
518	```bash
519	mkdir -p .beads/plans
520	
521	# Write the approved plan with metadata header
522	cat > .beads/plans/active-plan.md << 'PLAN_EOF'
523	# Active Plan
524	<!-- approved: <timestamp> -->
525	<!-- gate-iterations: <N> -->
526	<!-- user-approved: true -->
527	<!-- status: in-progress -->
528	
529	<full plan text including work unit decomposition, DoD items, file scopes, dependencies>
530	PLAN_EOF
531	```
532	
533	### Writing Execution State
534	
535	After each phase transition, update the execution state:
536	
537	```bash
538	cat > .beads/context/execution-state.md << 'STATE_EOF'
539	# Execution State
540	<!-- updated: <timestamp> -->
541	
542	## Current Position
543	- Active work unit: <wu-id>
544	- Current phase: <IMPLEMENT|VALIDATE|REVIEW|COMMIT>
545	- Retry count: <0-3>
546	
547	## Work Unit Status
548	| WU | Status | Phase | Retries |
549	|----|--------|-------|---------|
550	| WU-001 | COMPLETE | COMMITTED | 0 |
551	| WU-002 | IN-PROGRESS | VALIDATE | 1 |
552	| WU-003 | PENDING | — | 0 |
553	
554	## Blocked / Escalated
555	<any blocked or escalated work units with context>
556	STATE_EOF
557	```
558	
559	### Context Recovery Protocol
560	
561	When the orchestrator detects it has lost context (after compaction or in a new session), it recovers by reading persisted state:
562	
563	```text
564	1. Check: Does `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` exist with `status: in-progress`?
565	   - YES → Context was lost mid-execution. Recover.
566	   - NO → No active execution. Start fresh.
567	
568	2. Recovery steps:
569	   a. Read `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` — reload the approved plan
570	   b. Read `.beads/context/project-context.md` — reload completed work and patterns
571	   c. Read `.beads/context/execution-state.md` — find where execution stopped
572	   d. Run `bd prime --work-type recovery` — reload relevant knowledge base facts
573	   e. Resume from the current work unit and phase
574	
575	3. Announce recovery to user:
576	   "Recovered execution context from BEADS. Resuming from WU-<id>, Phase <phase>."
577	```
578	
579	**When to trigger recovery:** The orchestrator should check for `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` at the start of any orchestrated execution. If the file exists with `status: in-progress` and the orchestrator has no plan in its current context, it's a recovery scenario.
580	
581	### Cleanup
582	
583	After the PR is created (or the plan is abandoned):
584	
585	```bash
586	# Mark plan as completed (cross-platform sed)
587	if [[ "$OSTYPE" == "darwin"* ]]; then
588	  sed -i '' 's/status: in-progress/status: completed/' .beads/plans/active-plan.md
589	else
590	  sed -i 's/status: in-progress/status: completed/' .beads/plans/active-plan.md
591	fi
592	
593	# Archive execution state (don't delete — useful for post-mortem)
594	mv .beads/context/execution-state.md .beads/context/execution-state-<timestamp>.md
595	
596	# Project context can be kept for reference
597	```
598	
599	---
600	
601	## 7. Human Checkpoints (Proactive)
602	
603	Human checkpoints are **planned pauses**, not reactive escalations. They are defined in the spec before execution begins.
604	
605	### When to Set Checkpoints
606	
607	- After work units that change database schemas
608	- After work units that modify security-sensitive code
609	- After the first work unit in a new architectural pattern
610	- Before any destructive or irreversible operation
611	- At natural boundaries the human specified in the issue
612	- Before work units that depend on external services (APIs, SDKs requiring credentials)
613	  - Present: service name, required env vars, how to obtain credentials
614	  - Ask: "Do you have these configured? [Y/n]"
615	
616	### Checkpoint Report Format
617	
618	When reaching a checkpoint, present this report and **wait for explicit human approval**:
619	
620	```markdown
621	## Checkpoint: <checkpoint-name>
622	
623	### Completed Work Units
624	| WU | Title | Status | Review |
625	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
626	| WU-001 | Schema migration | PASS | Adversarial PASS |
627	| WU-002 | Service layer | PASS | Adversarial PASS |
628	
629	### Key Decisions Made
630	- <decision-1>: <rationale>
631	- <decision-2>: <rationale>
632	
633	Record significant decisions persistently with `bd decision "<decision>: <rationale>"` so they survive compaction and are available across sessions.
634	
635	### What Comes Next
636	- WU-003: <description>
637	- WU-004: <description>
638	
639	### Questions for Human (if any)
640	- <question>
641	
642	---
643	**Action required**: Reply to continue, or provide feedback to adjust course.
644	```
645	
646	**Do NOT continue past a checkpoint without human response.** This is not a notification — it's a gate.
647	
648	---
649	
650	## 8. Final Comprehensive Review
651	
652	After ALL work units are complete and committed, run a final comprehensive review across the entire change set. This catches cross-unit integration issues that per-unit reviews miss.
653	
654	### Final Review Checklist
655	
656	```bash
657	# 1. Combined diff — see the full picture
658	git diff main..HEAD
659	
660	# 2. Full test suite — not just changed files
661	npx vitest run
662	
663	# 3. Type check — catch cross-unit type conflicts
664	npx tsc --noEmit
665	
666	# 4. Lint — catch cross-unit style issues
667	npx eslint .
668	
669	# 5. Coverage — verify overall coverage thresholds
670	if [ -f .coverage-thresholds.json ]; then
671	  CMD=$(node -e "console.log(JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('.coverage-thresholds.json','utf-8')).enforcement.command)")
672	  eval "$CMD"
673	fi
674	
675	# 6. Commit history — verify clean, logical commits
676	git log main..HEAD --oneline
677	```
678	
679	### Cross-Unit Integration Checks
680	
681	- [ ] No duplicate or conflicting imports across work units
682	- [ ] No conflicting type definitions
683	- [ ] No overlapping test fixtures that could cause interference
684	- [ ] API contracts between work units are consistent
685	- [ ] No leftover TODO/FIXME markers from implementation
686	- [ ] File scope boundaries were respected (no unexpected file changes)
687	- [ ] SERVICE-INVENTORY.md is up to date with all created services
688	
689	### Final Report Format
690	
691	```markdown
692	## Final Comprehensive Review
693	
694	### Overall Verdict: PASS / FAIL
695	
696	### Work Units Summary
697	| WU | Title | Impl | Validate | Review | Commit |
698	| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
699	| WU-001 | <title> | Done | Pass | Pass | <sha> |
700	| WU-002 | <title> | Done | Pass | Pass | <sha> |
701	
702	### Quality Gates
703	- [ ] All tests pass
704	- [ ] Type check clean
705	- [ ] Lint clean
706	- [ ] Coverage thresholds met
707	- [ ] No cross-unit integration issues
708	
709	### Remaining Issues
710	<any issues found during final review>
711	
712	### Ready for PR: YES / NO
713	```
714	
715	---
716	
717	## 8.5. Pre-PR Knowledge Capture (MANDATORY)
718	
719	After the final comprehensive review passes but BEFORE creating the PR, run `/self-reflect` to extract learnings into the knowledge base. This captures implementation insights, debugging discoveries, and architectural decisions while context is freshest — not deferred to post-merge when details have faded.
720	
721	```text
722	## Pre-PR Knowledge Capture
723	
724	Final review PASSED. Before creating the PR, extracting learnings...
725	
726	/self-reflect
727	
728	Learnings captured: [N] items added to knowledge base.
729	Committing knowledge base updates...
730	Proceeding to PR creation.
731	```
732	
733	**Why before PR, not after merge?** By the time a PR is merged, the implementing agent's context may be gone (session ended, context compacted). The richest insights — why a certain approach was chosen, what debugging dead-ends were hit, which patterns emerged — exist NOW, immediately after implementation. Capture them now.
734	
735	**Knowledge base changes are part of the PR.** After self-reflect updates the knowledge base files, commit them alongside the implementation. This ensures learnings are reviewed as part of the PR and land atomically with the code that generated them. Do NOT defer knowledge base commits to a separate PR or post-merge step.
736	
737	---
738	
739	## 9. Recovery Protocol
740	
741	When things go wrong during the 4-phase loop, follow this structured recovery.
742	
743	### Step 1: DIAGNOSE
744	
745	Identify what failed and gather evidence:
746	
747	```bash
748	# Capture the failure
749	# - Which phase failed? (IMPLEMENT, VALIDATE, REVIEW)
750	# - What was the error message or FAIL reason?
751	# - Which DoD items are affected?
752	```
753	
754	### Step 2: CLASSIFY
755	
756	Categorize the failure:
757	
758	| Classification | Description | Action |
759	| --- | --- | --- |
760	| **Fixable** | Clear error, known fix | Retry with specific fix instructions |
761	| **Ambiguous** | Unclear root cause | Investigate before retrying |
762	| **External** | Dependency, access, or environment issue | Escalate immediately |
763	
764	### Step 3: RETRY (max 3 attempts)
765	
766	For fixable and ambiguous failures:
767	
768	1. **Attempt 1**: Fix the specific issue, re-run from Phase 1
769	2. **Attempt 2**: If same failure, try alternative approach
770	3. **Attempt 3**: If still failing, gather all evidence for escalation
771	
772	Track retry count:
773	
774	```bash
775	bd label add <task-id> retry:1  # or retry:2, retry:3
776	```
777	
778	### Step 4: ESCALATE
779	
780	After 3 failed attempts, escalate to human with full context:
781	
782	```markdown
783	## Escalation: Work Unit <wu-id> Failed After 3 Attempts
784	
785	### Failure History
786	| Attempt | Phase | Error | Fix Tried |
787	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
788	| 1 | VALIDATE | Tests fail: auth.test.ts:34 | Fixed mock setup |
789	| 2 | REVIEW | DoD #3 not met: missing edge case | Added edge case test |
790	| 3 | VALIDATE | Type error in cross-module import | Restructured imports |
791	
792	### Root Cause Assessment
793	<best understanding of why this keeps failing>
794	
795	### Options
796	1. <option-1>
797	2. <option-2>
798	3. Abandon this work unit and restructure
799	
800	### Recommendation
801	<which option and why>
802	```
803	
804	---
805	
806	## 10. Anti-Patterns
807	
808	These are explicit DON'Ts. Violating any of these undermines the entire orchestration pattern.
809	
810	| # | Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | What to Do Instead |
811	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
812	| 1 | **Self-certifying** — coding subagent says "tests pass" and you believe it | Subagents can hallucinate, skip tests, or misinterpret results | Orchestrator runs validation commands independently |
813	| 2 | **Skipping adversarial review** — "the code looks fine, let's commit" | Visual inspection misses spec violations; confirmation bias | Always run adversarial review against DoD items |
814	| 3 | **Reusing a reviewer** — same subagent re-reviews after FAIL | Anchoring bias: reviewer remembers previous findings and checks for those specifically instead of reviewing fresh | Spawn a new reviewer instance with no prior context |
815	| 4 | **Passing previous findings to new reviewer** — "last reviewer found X, check if fixed" | Creates anchoring bias; new reviewer should find issues independently | Pass only: spec, DoD items, diff. Nothing about previous reviews |
816	| 5 | **Trusting subagent file scope claims** — "I only changed the files in scope" | Subagents may accidentally modify files outside scope | Run `git diff --name-only` and verify each file independently |
817	| 6 | **Combining phases** — "implement and validate in one step" | Removes the independence that makes validation meaningful | Run each phase as a distinct step with its own output |
818	| 7 | **Continuing past a checkpoint without human response** | Defeats the purpose of proactive checkpoints | Wait. If urgent, escalate — don't skip |
819	| 8 | **Skipping final comprehensive review** — "all units passed individually" | Per-unit reviews can't catch cross-unit integration issues | Always run the final review after all units are committed |
820	| 9 | **Skipping coverage enforcement** — "tests pass, coverage doesn't matter" | Coverage thresholds exist for a reason; low coverage means untested paths | Read .coverage-thresholds.json and run the enforcement command. Block on failure. |
821	| 10 | **Building UI components in isolation** — all components tested but never wired into the app | Users can't interact with components that aren't rendered | Plan must include integration WUs that wire components into the app shell |
822	| 11 | **Proceeding without external credentials** — building features that require API keys without verifying the user has them | Features will fail at runtime; user discovers this after 10+ commits | Checkpoint before external-service WUs to verify credentials are configured |
823	| 12 | **Advisory quality gates** — treating FAIL as a suggestion rather than a blocking transition | Undermines the entire trust model; equivalent to skipping the gate | Quality gates are state transitions. FAIL means retry or escalate, never skip. |
824	| 13 | **Using `--no-verify`** — bypassing pre-commit hooks on git commits | Pre-commit hooks catch lint errors, type errors, and formatting issues before they enter history | Never use `--no-verify`. Fix the underlying issue instead. |
825	| 14 | **Skipping design review gate after brainstorming** — going directly from brainstorming to writing-plans | Expensive implementation work begins on unreviewed designs | Always run the 5-agent design review gate between brainstorming and planning |
826	| 15 | **Skipping plan review gate** — presenting a plan to the user without adversarial review | Plans with feasibility gaps, missing requirements, or scope creep reach implementation | Always run the 3-reviewer plan review gate before presenting any plan |
827	
828	---
829	
830	## Quick Reference: Orchestrator Checklist
831	
832	Before execution (Plan Validation):
833	
834	- [ ] Run pre-flight checklist (architecture, dependencies, API contracts, security, UI/UX, external deps)
835	- [ ] Verify all required plan sections are present
836	- [ ] Fix any checklist failures BEFORE submitting to Design Review Gate
837	
838	For each work unit:
839	
840	- [ ] Spawn coding subagent with spec, DoD, file scope, and **Project Context Document**
841	- [ ] Wait for implementation to complete
842	- [ ] **Independently** run: tsc, eslint, vitest, coverage enforcement (do NOT ask subagent)
843	- [ ] Verify file scope with `git diff --name-only`
844	- [ ] Spawn **fresh** adversarial reviewer with spec and DoD
845	- [ ] If PASS: commit with DoD verification in message
846	- [ ] If FAIL: fix → re-validate → spawn **new** reviewer (max 3 retries, then ESCALATE)
847	- [ ] If human checkpoint: present report and wait
848	- [ ] Update BEADS task status
849	- [ ] Update **SERVICE-INVENTORY.md** if services/factories/modules were created
850	- [ ] Update **Project Context Document** with completed work unit
851	
852	Quality Gate Rules:
853	
854	- [ ] VALIDATE → REVIEW: ONLY if ALL checks pass (tests, coverage, types, lint, file scope)
855	- [ ] REVIEW → COMMIT: ONLY if adversarial review returns PASS
856	- [ ] FAIL at any gate: retry (max 3), then ESCALATE — never skip
857	- [ ] Re-review after fix: MUST use FRESH reviewer (new instance, no memory)
858	
859	After all work units:
860	
861	- [ ] Run final comprehensive review (with coverage enforcement)
862	- [ ] Verify SERVICE-INVENTORY.md is complete
863	- [ ] Present final report
864	- [ ] Run `/self-reflect` to capture learnings (BEFORE PR creation)
865	- [ ] Commit knowledge base updates (included in the PR)
866	- [ ] Proceed to PR creation

1	---
2	name: plan-review-gate
3	description: Automatic adversarial review gate that spawns 3 independent reviewers in parallel after any plan is drafted - all must PASS before presenting to user
4	auto_activate: true
5	triggers:
6	  - "plan drafted"
7	  - "implementation plan created"
8	  - after:writing-plans
9	  - after:orchestrated-execution:plan-validation
10	---
11	
12	# Plan Review Gate
13	
14	**Core principle**: No plan reaches the user without surviving independent adversarial scrutiny.
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	This skill automatically activates after any implementation plan is drafted, before presenting it to the user. It spawns three independent adversarial reviewers in parallel — Feasibility, Completeness, and Scope & Alignment — each as a fresh `Task()` instance. ALL three must PASS. On failure, the planner incorporates feedback and resubmits to entirely fresh reviewer instances. The gate iterates until consensus or max iterations.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## Activation Triggers
23	
24	This skill auto-activates when:
25	
26	1. An implementation plan is drafted (by planner, explorer, or orchestrator)
27	2. The `writing-plans` skill completes a plan
28	3. The orchestrator's plan validation phase produces a plan for review
29	4. User explicitly requests: `/plan-review <path-to-plan>`
30	
31	**Do NOT use for**: Trivial changes (single-file bug fixes, copy edits, config tweaks). The plan must have at least 2 work units or touch 3+ files to warrant gate review.
32	
33	---
34	
35	## The 3 Adversarial Reviewers
36	
37	Each reviewer is a fresh `Task()` instance with read-only access to the codebase. Reviewers produce binary PASS/FAIL verdicts backed by cited evidence. No suggestions — only findings.
38	
39	### Reviewer 1: Feasibility
40	
41	Can this plan actually be executed against the real codebase?
42	
43	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
44	| --- | --- | --- |
45	| File paths exist | BLOCKING if fabricated | Every file path referenced in the plan must exist (verify with glob/grep) |
46	| Dependency ordering correct | BLOCKING if circular or forward-ref | Work units depend only on things that exist or are created before them |
47	| Technical approach matches codebase | BLOCKING if incompatible | Proposed patterns, libraries, and conventions match what the codebase actually uses |
48	| No unstated assumptions | BLOCKING if critical | Plan does not silently depend on services, env vars, or infrastructure that doesn't exist |
49	| File scope realistic | WARNING | Each work unit's file scope is achievable without cascading changes |
50	
51	### Reviewer 2: Completeness
52	
53	Does the plan fully address every aspect of the user's request?
54	
55	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
56	| --- | --- | --- |
57	| All requirements mapped | BLOCKING if gap exists | Every user requirement maps to at least one plan item |
58	| Verification steps defined | BLOCKING if missing | Each change has a way to verify it worked (test, manual check, or assertion) |
59	| Edge cases considered | BLOCKING if obvious gaps | Error scenarios, empty states, boundary conditions addressed |
60	| Rollback/backward compatibility | WARNING | Plan considers what happens if changes need to be reverted |
61	| Cross-file integration points | BLOCKING if missing | Files that import/depend on changed files are accounted for |
62	
63	### Reviewer 3: Scope & Alignment
64	
65	Is the plan right-sized for what the user actually asked?
66	
67	| Check | Classification | Criteria |
68	| --- | --- | --- |
69	| Matches user request | BLOCKING if divergent | Plan solves what was asked, not what the planner finds interesting |
70	| No scope creep | BLOCKING if present | No unnecessary features, abstractions, or refactoring beyond the request |
71	| No under-scoping | BLOCKING if present | Obvious implications of the request are not omitted |
72	| Complexity proportional | WARNING | Solution complexity matches problem complexity |
73	| No simpler alternative missed | WARNING | Plan is not over-engineered when a simpler approach would suffice |
74	
75	---
76	
77	## Reviewer Isolation Rules
78	
79	These rules are **mandatory**. Violating any of them invalidates the review.
80	
81	1. **Fresh instances only.** Each reviewer is a new `Task()` instance — never resumed, never given prior context.
82	2. **No cross-reviewer visibility.** No reviewer sees another reviewer's output. Ever.
83	3. **Read-only codebase access.** Reviewers can read files, run grep/glob, but cannot modify anything.
84	4. **No prior review findings on re-review.** When re-reviewing after iteration, completely fresh instances are spawned. The new reviewers have zero knowledge of what previous reviewers found.
85	5. **Input is limited to:** the plan text, the user's original request, and the codebase (via read-only tools).
86	
87	**Why isolation matters:** Reviewers who see prior findings anchor on those findings instead of reviewing independently. Fresh instances prevent this bias and ensure each review cycle is a genuine independent check.
88	
89	---
90	
91	## Workflow
92	
93	```text
94	1. Plan drafted by planner/explorer/orchestrator
95	2. Spawn 3 adversarial reviewers in PARALLEL (fresh Task() instances)
96	3. Collect all 3 verdicts
97	4. IF all PASS --> present plan to user with gate approval summary
98	5. IF any FAIL -->
99	   a. Planner reads ALL feedback from all reviewers
100	   b. Planner incorporates changes OR documents why feedback doesn't apply
101	   c. Spawn 3 NEW reviewer instances (never reuse -- prevents anchoring)
102	   d. Repeat from step 3
103	6. Max 3 iterations --> present plan with remaining issues noted for user decision
104	```
105	
106	### Phase 1: Spawn Reviewers (Parallel)
107	
108	```typescript
109	// Spawn all three reviewers in parallel for efficiency
110	const [feasibilityResult, completenessResult, scopeResult] = await Promise.all([
111	  Task({
112	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
113	    description: "Feasibility review",
114	    prompt: feasibilityReviewPrompt(planText, userRequest),
115	  }),
116	  Task({
117	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
118	    description: "Completeness review",
119	    prompt: completenessReviewPrompt(planText, userRequest),
120	  }),
121	  Task({
122	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
123	    description: "Scope & Alignment review",
124	    prompt: scopeAlignmentReviewPrompt(planText, userRequest),
125	  }),
126	]);
127	```
128	
129	### Phase 2: Evaluate Gate
130	
131	```text
132	IF feasibilityResult.verdict === "PASS"
133	   AND completenessResult.verdict === "PASS"
134	   AND scopeResult.verdict === "PASS":
135	  --> GATE APPROVED. Present plan to user.
136	
137	ELSE:
138	  --> Consolidate all FAIL findings.
139	  --> Pass to planner for revision.
140	  --> Increment iteration counter.
141	  --> IF iterations >= 3: present plan with remaining issues to user.
142	  --> ELSE: spawn fresh reviewers on revised plan.
143	```
144	
145	### Phase 3: Iteration (on FAIL)
146	
147	1. Planner receives consolidated feedback from ALL reviewers (both PASS and FAIL)
148	2. Planner revises the plan, addressing each FAIL finding explicitly
149	3. For each finding, planner either:
150	   - **Fixes it** — modifies the plan to resolve the issue
151	   - **Rebuts it** — documents why the finding is incorrect or not applicable (with evidence)
152	4. Spawn 3 entirely new `Task()` instances for re-review
153	5. New reviewers see ONLY the revised plan and original request — NOT previous findings
154	
155	### Phase 4: Escalation (after 3 iterations)
156	
157	If the gate has not achieved consensus after 3 iterations:
158	
159	```markdown
160	## Plan Review Gate: ESCALATION REQUIRED (3/3 iterations exhausted)
161	
162	### Remaining Blocking Issues
163	
164	#### Feasibility
165	- [issue with evidence]
166	
167	#### Completeness
168	- [issue with evidence]
169	
170	#### Scope & Alignment
171	- [issue with evidence]
172	
173	### Iteration History
174	| Iteration | Feasibility | Completeness | Scope & Alignment |
175	|-----------|-------------|--------------|-------------------|
176	| 1         | FAIL        | PASS         | FAIL              |
177	| 2         | PASS        | PASS         | FAIL              |
178	| 3         | PASS        | PASS         | FAIL              |
179	
180	### Options
181	1. **Override** — Proceed with plan as-is (remaining issues become known risks)
182	2. **Revise** — Continue iterating on the plan manually
183	3. **Simplify** — Reduce scope to eliminate contentious items
184	4. **Cancel** — Abandon this plan and start fresh
185	
186	Please choose an option or provide additional context.
187	```
188	
189	---
190	
191	## Reviewer Prompts
192	
193	### Feasibility Reviewer Prompt
194	
195	````markdown
196	You are the FEASIBILITY REVIEWER for a plan review gate.
197	
198	## Mode
199	Adversarial — your job is to FIND FAILURES in plan feasibility, not to approve.
200	
201	## Rubric
202	Read and follow: ./rubrics/plan-review-rubric-adversarial.md (Feasibility section)
203	
204	## User's Original Request
205	${userRequest}
206	
207	## Plan Under Review
208	${planText}
209	
210	## Your Task
211	Determine whether this plan can actually be executed against the real codebase. Check:
212	
213	1. **File paths exist** — Use glob/grep to verify every file path the plan references actually exists. Fabricated paths = BLOCKING FAIL.
214	2. **Dependency ordering** — Verify work units don't have circular dependencies or reference things created in later steps. Read the codebase to confirm.
215	3. **Technical approach** — Does the plan use patterns, libraries, and conventions that match the actual codebase? Read existing code to verify.
216	4. **Unstated assumptions** — Does the plan silently depend on services, configurations, or infrastructure that doesn't exist?
217	
218	## Rules
219	- Check EACH criterion. Cite file:line or glob results as evidence for PASS, or specific gaps for FAIL.
220	- Any single BLOCKING issue means overall FAIL.
221	- You have NO context from previous reviews. Judge fresh.
222	- Do NOT suggest improvements. Only report PASS or FAIL with evidence.
223	- Do NOT consider other reviewers. You are independent.
224	
225	## Output Format
226	Follow the per-reviewer output format defined in the skill.
227	````
228	
229	### Completeness Reviewer Prompt
230	
231	````markdown
232	You are the COMPLETENESS REVIEWER for a plan review gate.
233	
234	## Mode
235	Adversarial — your job is to FIND GAPS in plan coverage, not to approve.
236	
237	## Rubric
238	Read and follow: ./rubrics/plan-review-rubric-adversarial.md (Completeness section)
239	
240	## User's Original Request
241	${userRequest}
242	
243	## Plan Under Review
244	${planText}
245	
246	## Your Task
247	Determine whether this plan fully addresses every aspect of the user's request. Check:
248	
249	1. **Requirement mapping** — Extract each distinct requirement from the user's request. For each one, find the plan item that addresses it. Missing mapping = BLOCKING FAIL.
250	2. **Verification steps** — Each planned change must have a defined way to verify it works (test, assertion, manual check). Missing verification = BLOCKING FAIL.
251	3. **Edge cases** — Are error scenarios, empty states, and boundary conditions addressed? Obvious gaps = BLOCKING FAIL.
252	4. **Rollback/backward compatibility** — Does the plan consider what happens if changes need to be reverted?
253	5. **Cross-file integration** — Are files that import or depend on changed files accounted for?
254	
255	## Rules
256	- Check EACH criterion. Cite specific user requirements and plan items as evidence.
257	- Any single BLOCKING issue means overall FAIL.
258	- You have NO context from previous reviews. Judge fresh.
259	- Do NOT suggest improvements. Only report PASS or FAIL with evidence.
260	- Do NOT consider other reviewers. You are independent.
261	
262	## Output Format
263	Follow the per-reviewer output format defined in the skill.
264	````
265	
266	### Scope & Alignment Reviewer Prompt
267	
268	````markdown
269	You are the SCOPE & ALIGNMENT REVIEWER for a plan review gate.
270	
271	## Mode
272	Adversarial — your job is to FIND MISALIGNMENT between the plan and user request, not to approve.
273	
274	## Rubric
275	Read and follow: ./rubrics/plan-review-rubric-adversarial.md (Scope & Alignment section)
276	
277	## User's Original Request
278	${userRequest}
279	
280	## Plan Under Review
281	${planText}
282	
283	## Your Task
284	Determine whether this plan is right-sized for what the user actually asked. Check:
285	
286	1. **Matches user request** — Does the plan solve what was asked? Or does it solve something the planner found more interesting? Divergence = BLOCKING FAIL.
287	2. **No scope creep** — Are there features, abstractions, or refactoring beyond what was requested? Unnecessary additions = BLOCKING FAIL.
288	3. **No under-scoping** — Are obvious implications of the request omitted? Missing obvious work = BLOCKING FAIL.
289	4. **Complexity proportional** — Is the solution complexity proportional to the problem complexity?
290	5. **Simpler alternative** — Is there a significantly simpler approach that achieves the same outcome?
291	
292	## Rules
293	- Check EACH criterion. Cite the user's request and plan scope as evidence.
294	- Any single BLOCKING issue means overall FAIL.
295	- You have NO context from previous reviews. Judge fresh.
296	- Do NOT suggest improvements. Only report PASS or FAIL with evidence.
297	- Do NOT consider other reviewers. You are independent.
298	
299	## Output Format
300	Follow the per-reviewer output format defined in the skill.
301	````
302	
303	---
304	
305	## Output Formats
306	
307	### Per-Reviewer Output
308	
309	Each reviewer produces output in this exact format:
310	
311	```markdown
312	## [Reviewer Name] — [PASS/FAIL]
313	
314	### Evidence
315	- [Finding 1]: [file:line reference or specific gap with evidence]
316	- [Finding 2]: [file:line reference or specific gap with evidence]
317	- ...
318	
319	### Verdict
320	[PASS: All criteria met — no blocking issues found]
321	OR
322	[FAIL: {numbered list of blocking issues with evidence}]
323	```
324	
325	### Consolidated Gate Output (All PASS)
326	
327	```markdown
328	## Plan Review Gate — APPROVED (iteration N of 3)
329	
330	| Reviewer | Verdict | Key Finding |
331	|----------|---------|-------------|
332	| Feasibility | PASS | All file paths verified, deps ordered correctly |
333	| Completeness | PASS | All N requirements mapped to plan items |
334	| Scope & Alignment | PASS | Plan matches user request, no scope creep |
335	
336	Plan is ready for user review.
337	```
338	
339	### Consolidated Gate Output (Any FAIL)
340	
341	```markdown
342	## Plan Review Gate — REVISION NEEDED (iteration N of 3)
343	
344	| Reviewer | Verdict | Blocking Issues |
345	|----------|---------|-----------------|
346	| Feasibility | PASS | — |
347	| Completeness | FAIL | 2 blocking issues |
348	| Scope & Alignment | FAIL | 1 blocking issue |
349	
350	### Blocking Issues (MUST ADDRESS)
351	
352	#### Completeness Reviewer
353	1. **Missing requirement mapping**: User asked for X but no plan item addresses it
354	2. **No verification for WU-003**: Plan item has no test or assertion defined
355	
356	#### Scope & Alignment Reviewer
357	1. **Scope creep**: WU-005 adds caching layer not requested by user
358	
359	---
360	
361	**Iteration**: N of 3
362	**Action Required**: Planner must address all blocking issues and resubmit.
363	```
364	
365	---
366	
367	## Anti-Patterns
368	
369	| # | Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | What to Do Instead |
370	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
371	| 1 | **Reusing reviewer instances** — passing findings to next review cycle | Anchoring bias: reviewer checks for previous findings instead of reviewing fresh | Spawn new `Task()` instances with no prior context |
372	| 2 | **Cross-reviewer contamination** — sharing one reviewer's output with another | Destroys independence; reviewers converge instead of checking independently | Each reviewer sees only: plan, user request, codebase |
373	| 3 | **Treating FAIL as advisory** — "reviewer failed but the issues are minor" | Undermines the gate; equivalent to not having a gate | FAIL means revise and re-review. No exceptions. |
374	| 4 | **Skipping the gate for "simple" plans** — "this is straightforward, skip review" | Judgment of simplicity is exactly what the gate validates | If the plan has 2+ work units or touches 3+ files, run the gate |
375	| 5 | **Planner self-reviewing** — same agent that wrote the plan also reviews it | Confirmation bias; planner will approve their own work | Reviewers must be separate `Task()` instances from the planner |
376	| 6 | **Unlimited iterations** — "keep reviewing until it passes" | Diminishing returns; likely a fundamental plan problem after 3 rounds | Max 3 iterations, then escalate to user with full context |
377	| 7 | **Partial re-review** — only re-running the reviewer that failed | Other reviewers might fail on the revised plan | All 3 reviewers re-run on every iteration (fresh instances) |
378	
379	---
380	
381	## Integration Points
382	
383	### Upstream (inputs to this gate)
384	
385	- `writing-plans` skill — produces plans that feed into this gate
386	- `orchestrated-execution` plan validation — pre-flight checklist runs before this gate
387	- Direct plan drafting by planner/explorer agents
388	
389	### Downstream (after gate approval)
390	
391	- Plan presented to user for final approval
392	- **After user approval**: Persist the approved plan to `.beads/plans/active-plan.md` (see Section: Plan Persistence below)
393	- User-approved plan flows to `orchestrated-execution` for the 4-phase execution loop
394	- Work unit decomposition and implementation begin
395	
396	### Plan Persistence
397	
398	After the gate approves AND the user approves the plan, persist it to BEADS so it survives context compaction:
399	
400	```bash
401	mkdir -p .beads/plans
402	
403	# Write with metadata header
404	cat > .beads/plans/active-plan.md << 'PLAN_EOF'
405	# Active Plan
406	<!-- approved: <timestamp> -->
407	<!-- gate-iterations: <N> -->
408	<!-- user-approved: true -->
409	<!-- status: in-progress -->
410	
411	<full approved plan text>
412	PLAN_EOF
413	```
414	
415	This enables context recovery — if the agent loses context mid-execution, it can re-read the approved plan from disk instead of re-running the entire gate. See `orchestrated-execution` Section 6.5 for the full recovery protocol.
416	
417	### Relationship to Design Review Gate
418	
419	The **design review gate** (`design-review-gate`) validates _design documents_ using 5 specialist agents (PM, Architect, Designer, Security, CTO) with APPROVED/NEEDS_REVISION verdicts.
420	
421	The **plan review gate** (this skill) validates _implementation plans_ using 3 adversarial reviewers (Feasibility, Completeness, Scope & Alignment) with binary PASS/FAIL verdicts.
422	
423	These are complementary gates at different stages:
424	1. Design review gate validates _what_ to build
425	2. Plan review gate validates _how_ to build it
426	3. Orchestrated execution validates _that_ it was built correctly
427	
428	---
429	
430	## Success Criteria
431	
432	The plan review gate succeeds when:
433	
434	- [ ] All three reviewers return PASS verdict
435	- [ ] All blocking issues are resolved with evidence
436	- [ ] Plan is presented to user with gate approval summary
437	- [ ] Reviewer isolation was maintained (fresh instances, no cross-contamination)
438	- [ ] Gate completed within 3 iterations
439	
440	---
441	
442	## Related Skills
443	
444	- `design-review-gate` — Validates design documents before planning begins
445	- `orchestrated-execution` — Executes approved plans using the 4-phase loop
446	- `writing-plans` — Produces plans that feed into this gate
447	
448	---
449	
450	## Rubric Reference
451	
452	Reviewers follow: `./rubrics/plan-review-rubric-adversarial.md`
453	
454	This adversarial rubric is distinct from `./rubrics/plan-review-rubric.md` (used by the CTO Agent for collaborative plan review). See the rubric file for detailed scoring criteria and evidence requirements.
455

1	---
2	name: pr-shepherd
3	description: Monitor a PR through to merge — handle CI failures, review comments, and thread resolution automatically until all checks pass
4	---
5	
6	# pr-shepherd
7	
8	Use when a PR has been created and needs to be monitored through to merge - handles CI failures, review comments, and thread resolution automatically until all checks pass and all threads are resolved.
9	
10	**IMPORTANT**: This skill is designed for the **agent working in a worktree**, NOT the orchestrator. The agent handles its own PR monitoring so the orchestrator remains free for other work.
11	
12	## Coordination Mode Note
13	
14	This skill supports both coordination modes:
15	
16	- **Task Mode** (default): Runs as a single long-running `Task()` with `run_in_background: true`. Orchestrator checks via `TaskOutput(block: false)`.
17	- **Team Mode**: Runs as a persistent `shepherd` teammate in the `issue-{number}` team. Sends async status updates via `SendMessage` (CI failure, review comments, all-green, PR merged). Orchestrator can respond with instructions (e.g., "defer that comment, create an issue instead"). Responds to `shutdown_request` for graceful exit.
18	
19	All monitoring, fixing, and review handling logic is identical in both modes. See `./guides/agent-coordination.md` for mode detection.
20	
21	---
22	
23	## When to Activate
24	
25	Activate this skill when ANY of these conditions are true:
26	
27	- Agent just created a PR with `gh pr create`
28	- User asks to "shepherd", "monitor", or "see through" a PR
29	- User invokes `/pr-shepherd <pr-number>`
30	- User asks to "watch this PR" or "handle this PR until it's merged"
31	- Orchestrator spawned you with instructions to shepherd a PR
32	- **Automatic**: `bin/create-pr-with-shepherd.sh` was used (outputs shepherd instructions)
33	
34	### Automatic Activation via Wrapper Script
35	
36	When `bin/create-pr-with-shepherd.sh` creates a PR, it outputs shepherd instructions:
37	
38	```text
39	==========================================
40	  PR Shepherd Active for PR #123
41	==========================================
42	
43	The pr-shepherd skill will:
44	  - Monitor CI/CD status
45	  - Auto-fix lint, type, and test issues
46	  - Handle review comments
47	  - Resolve threads after addressing feedback
48	  - Report when PR is ready to merge
49	
50	To manually invoke shepherd later:
51	  /pr-shepherd 123
52	```
53	
54	When you see this output, **immediately invoke the pr-shepherd skill** with the PR number shown.
55	
56	## Announce at Start
57	
58	"I'm using the pr-shepherd skill to monitor this PR through to merge. I'll watch CI/CD, handle review comments, and fix issues as they arise."
59	
60	## For Orchestrators: Spawning Agents with PR Shepherding
61	
62	When spawning an agent to work in a worktree, include PR shepherding in the task prompt:
63	
64	```text
65	Work in worktree at /path/to/worktree on branch feature/xyz.
66	
67	Task: [describe the implementation task]
68	
69	After creating the PR:
70	1. Use the pr-shepherd skill to monitor it through to merge
71	2. Handle CI failures and review comments autonomously
72	3. Only escalate to orchestrator for complex issues requiring user input
73	4. Report back when PR is ready to merge or if blocked
74	
75	Run in background so I can continue other work.
76	```
77	
78	**Key principle**: The agent owns its PR lifecycle. The orchestrator spawns and forgets, checking back via `AgentOutputTool` when needed.
79	
80	## State Machine
81	
82	The agent operates in one of these states:
83	
84	```text
85	MONITORING → FIXING → MONITORING → WAITING_FOR_USER → FIXING → MONITORING → DONE
86	```
87	
88	| State              | What Happens                                | Exit When                                      |
89	| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
90	| `MONITORING`       | Poll CI and reviews every 60s in background | CI fails, new comments, all done, or need help |
91	| `FIXING`           | Fix issues using TDD, run local validation  | Local validation passes OR need user guidance  |
92	| `HANDLING_REVIEWS` | Invoke `handling-pr-comments` skill         | Comments handled OR need user input            |
93	| `WAITING_FOR_USER` | Present options, wait for user decision     | User responds                                  |
94	| `DONE`             | All CI green + all threads resolved         | Exit successfully                              |
95	
96	## Phase 1: Initialize
97	
98	```bash
99	# Get PR info
100	PR_NUMBER=$(gh pr view --json number -q .number 2>/dev/null)
101	OWNER=$(gh repo view --json owner -q .owner.login)
102	REPO=$(gh repo view --json name -q .name)
103	
104	# If no PR on current branch, check if number was provided
105	if [ -z "$PR_NUMBER" ]; then
106	  echo "No PR found for current branch. Provide PR number."
107	  exit 1
108	fi
109	
110	echo "Shepherding PR #$PR_NUMBER"
111	```
112	
113	## Phase 2: Monitoring Loop (Background)
114	
115	Run GTG every 60 seconds as the **single source of truth** for PR readiness:
116	
117	### Primary Check: GTG (Good-To-Go)
118	
119	GTG consolidates CI status, comment classification, and thread resolution into one call. Use it instead of separate API queries.
120	
121	```bash
122	# Primary readiness check — structured JSON output
123	GTG_RESULT=$(gtg $PR_NUMBER --repo "$OWNER/$REPO" --format json \
124	  --exclude-checks "Merge Ready (gtg)" \
125	  --exclude-checks "CodeRabbit" \
126	  --exclude-checks "Cursor Bugbot" \
127	  --exclude-checks "claude" 2>&1)
128	
129	STATUS=$(echo "$GTG_RESULT" | jq -r '.status')
130	ACTION_ITEMS=$(echo "$GTG_RESULT" | jq -r '.action_items[]?' 2>/dev/null)
131	CI_STATE=$(echo "$GTG_RESULT" | jq -r '.ci_status.state')
132	```
133	
134	**GTG statuses:**
135	
136	| Status               | Meaning                            | Agent Action                              |
137	| -------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
138	| `READY`              | All CI green, all threads resolved | → DONE                                    |
139	| `ACTION_REQUIRED`    | Actionable comments need fixes     | → HANDLING_REVIEWS (use `action_items`)   |
140	| `UNRESOLVED_THREADS` | Review threads still open          | → HANDLING_REVIEWS                        |
141	| `CI_FAILING`         | One or more CI checks failing      | → FIXING                                  |
142	| `ERROR`              | Couldn't fetch PR data             | Retry after 60s, escalate after 3 retries |
143	
144	**GTG reports, agents act**: GTG does not resolve threads or fix code — it only tells you what's blocking. After addressing feedback, you must resolve threads yourself using the GraphQL mutation in `handle-pr-comments.md` (Section 3). GTG will report `READY` on the next poll once threads are resolved.
145	
146	### Evaluate State Transitions
147	
148	```text
149	if STATUS == "READY":
150	  → DONE
151	
152	if STATUS == "CI_FAILING":
153	  → Parse action_items for specific failures
154	  → if is_simple_failure(failure): FIXING
155	  → else: WAITING_FOR_USER
156	
157	if STATUS == "ACTION_REQUIRED" or STATUS == "UNRESOLVED_THREADS":
158	  → HANDLING_REVIEWS (action_items tells you exactly what to fix)
159	
160	if STATUS == "ERROR":
161	  → Retry, then escalate
162	```
163	
164	### Fallback: Manual Checks
165	
166	If GTG is unavailable (e.g., not installed in environment), fall back to manual queries:
167	
168	```bash
169	# CI status
170	FAILED_CHECKS=$(gh pr checks $PR_NUMBER --json name,conclusion --jq '[.[] | select(.conclusion == "FAILURE")] | length')
171	
172	# Unresolved threads
173	UNRESOLVED=$(gh api graphql -f query='
174	  query($owner: String!, $repo: String!, $pr: Int!) {
175	    repository(owner: $owner, name: $repo) {
176	      pullRequest(number: $pr) {
177	        reviewThreads(first: 100) {
178	          nodes { isResolved }
179	        }
180	      }
181	    }
182	  }
183	' -f owner="$OWNER" -f repo="$REPO" -F pr="$PR_NUMBER" \
184	  --jq '[.data.repository.pullRequest.reviewThreads.nodes[] | select(.isResolved == false)] | length')
185	```
186	
187	### Re-triggering GTG CI Check
188	
189	When threads are resolved but the `Merge Ready (gtg)` GitHub Actions check is stale:
190	
191	```bash
192	gh workflow run gtg.yml -f pr_number=$PR_NUMBER
193	```
194	
195	## Phase 3: Fixing Issues
196	
197	### Simple Issues (Auto-fix)
198	
199	These can be fixed without user approval:
200	
201	- Lint failures → run linter
202	- Prettier failures → run formatter
203	- Type errors → fix the types
204	- Test failures in code YOU wrote → fix using TDD
205	
206	### Complex Issues (Need Approval)
207	
208	These require user input BEFORE fixing:
209	
210	- Test failures in code you didn't write
211	- Infrastructure/config failures
212	- Ambiguous errors
213	- Anything you're uncertain about
214	
215	### FIXING State Rules
216	
217	1. **Use TDD** - Invoke `superpowers:test-driven-development` for code changes
218	2. **Kill stale test runners** - Run `pkill -f vitest 2>/dev/null || true` before test runs
219	3. **Stay until green** - Don't leave FIXING until `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run && pnpm test:coverage` all pass
220	4. **Only push when verified** - Never push code that fails local validation or coverage thresholds
221	5. **Return to MONITORING after push** - Let CI run, continue monitoring
222	
223	```bash
224	# Kill stale vitest processes before running tests
225	pkill -f vitest 2>/dev/null || true
226	
227	# After fixing, always validate locally (including coverage)
228	pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run && pnpm test:coverage
229	
230	# Only push if all pass (including coverage thresholds)
231	git add -A && git commit -m "fix: <description>" && git push
232	```
233	
234	## Phase 4: Handling Reviews
235	
236	When new review comments are detected:
237	
238	1. Invoke the `handling-pr-comments` skill
239	2. That skill handles categorization, fixes, responses, and thread resolution
240	3. **CRITICAL: The handling-pr-comments skill includes an iteration loop**
241	4. **ALL threads must be resolved** before returning to MONITORING
242	5. If a thread cannot be resolved (needs clarification from reviewer), query the comment author asking for follow-up
243	6. Return to MONITORING only when:
244	   - All threads are resolved, AND
245	   - Post-push verification confirms NO new comments appeared
246	
247	### Iteration Enforcement
248	
249	**THE #1 FAILURE MODE**: Returning to MONITORING after one pass without checking for new comments.
250	
251	The `handling-pr-comments` skill's Phase 7 (Post-Push Iteration Check) MUST complete successfully before exiting HANDLING_REVIEWS state. The skill will iterate automatically:
252	
253	```text
254	HANDLING_REVIEWS:
255	  → handling-pr-comments skill (Phases 1-7)
256	  → IF Phase 7 finds new comments: skill re-runs Phases 1-7
257	  → IF Phase 7 confirms no new comments: exit to MONITORING
258	```
259	
260	**DO NOT** manually override or skip Phase 7. If you find yourself tempted to skip iteration, you're about to make the #1 mistake.
261	
262	### Out-of-Scope Comments
263	
264	Reviewers may leave comments on code outside the PR diff. The `handling-pr-comments` skill handles these, but key points:
265	
266	- **Treat out-of-scope as IN SCOPE by default** - respect reviewer feedback
267	- Use **ultrathink** to evaluate if fixes are quick (< 30 min, < 3 files)
268	- If simple: fix immediately and note it was outside original scope
269	- If complex: create a GitHub issue and link it in the thread response
270	- **Always respond and resolve** - never leave out-of-scope threads hanging
271	
272	## Phase 5: Waiting for User
273	
274	When user input is needed, ALWAYS:
275	
276	1. **Present the situation clearly**
277	2. **Offer 2-4 options with pros/cons**
278	3. **State your recommendation**
279	4. **Allow user to choose OR provide their own approach**
280	
281	### Template
282	
283	```text
284	[Describe what happened]
285	
286	**Options:**
287	
288	1. **[Option name]** (Recommended)
289	   - [What it involves]
290	   - Pros: [benefits]
291	   - Cons: [drawbacks]
292	
293	2. **[Option name]**
294	   - [What it involves]
295	   - Pros: [benefits]
296	   - Cons: [drawbacks]
297	
298	3. **[Option name]**
299	   - [What it involves]
300	   - Pros: [benefits]
301	   - Cons: [drawbacks]
302	
303	Which approach would you like? (Or describe a different approach)
304	```
305	
306	### After User Responds
307	
308	- If user picks a numbered option → proceed with that approach → FIXING
309	- If user describes alternative → proceed with their approach → FIXING
310	
311	## Phase 6: Soft Timeout (4 Hours)
312	
313	At 4 hours elapsed, pause and checkpoint:
314	
315	```text
316	**PR Shepherd Checkpoint** (4 hours elapsed)
317	
318	Current status:
319	- CI: [status]
320	- Threads: [X] resolved, [Y] unresolved
321	- Commits: [N] fix commits pushed
322	
323	**Options:**
324	
325	1. **Keep monitoring** (Recommended)
326	   - Continue for another 4 hours
327	   - Pros: PR may get reviewed soon
328	   - Cons: Ties up agent resources
329	
330	2. **Exit with handoff**
331	   - Save status report, exit cleanly
332	   - Pros: Frees resources
333	   - Cons: Must manually re-invoke later
334	
335	3. **Set shorter check-in**
336	   - Check back in 1 hour instead of 4
337	   - Pros: More frequent checkpoints
338	   - Cons: More interruptions
339	
340	What would you like to do? (Or describe a different approach)
341	```
342	
343	## Exit Conditions
344	
345	### Success (DONE)
346	
347	Exit successfully when ALL are true:
348	
349	- All CI checks passing
350	- **Every single** code review comment has been addressed (fix or explanation -- NONE ignored)
351	- All review threads resolved (zero unresolved)
352	- No pending questions
353	- PR squash-merged to main (not just "ready to merge" -- actually merged)
354	
355	Report:
356	
357	```text
358	**PR #[number] Ready to Merge**
359	
360	- CI: All checks passing
361	- Reviews: All threads resolved
362	- Commits: [N] total ([M] fix commits)
363	
364	The PR is ready for final approval and merge.
365	```
366	
367	### Post-Completion RAM Cleanup
368	
369	After the PR is merged and knowledge extraction tasks are created, invoke automatic RAM cleanup to free resources:
370	
371	```text
372	/auto-ram-cleanup
373	```
374	
375	**Why**: Development processes (test runners, build watchers, language servers) accumulate during PR work. Cleaning up after merge frees memory for the next task.
376	
377	**What stays running**:
378	
379	- Docker containers (needed for database/services)
380	- Essential IDE processes
381	
382	**What gets cleaned**:
383	
384	- Orphaned test runners (vitest, jest)
385	- Build watchers no longer needed
386	- Duplicate language server instances
387	- Other development tool cruft
388	
389	## Phase 7: Post-Merge Verification & Fallback Knowledge Extraction
390	
391	**Primary path**: Self-reflect should have already run pre-PR (see orchestrated-execution section 8.5), with knowledge base changes committed as part of the PR. This phase verifies that happened and handles the fallback case.
392	
393	**Fallback**: If self-reflect was NOT run pre-PR (e.g., PR was created outside the orchestrated workflow), create a blocking task for knowledge extraction after merge.
394	
395	### When PR is Merged
396	
397	After detecting that the PR has been merged (or after user merges it):
398	
399	```bash
400	# Check if PR was merged
401	MERGED=$(gh pr view $PR_NUMBER --json merged -q .merged)
402	
403	if [ "$MERGED" = "true" ]; then
404	  # Create a task for knowledge curation
405	  # Use your project's task tracking system
406	  echo "Create task: Curate learnings from PR #$PR_NUMBER"
407	
408	  # If there's an associated epic, add this task as a blocker
409	  # (The epic can't close until learnings are extracted)
410	fi
411	```
412	
413	**Note**: Self-reflect (`/self-reflect`) should have already run BEFORE the PR was created (see orchestrated-execution skill, section 8.5). If it was skipped, run it now as a fallback — but the preferred time is pre-PR while implementation context is freshest.
414	
415	### Report to User
416	
417	When creating the curation task:
418	
419	````text
420	**PR #[number] Merged Successfully**
421	
422	Created blocking task: [CURATION_TASK_ID]
423	- Title: "Curate learnings from PR #[number]"
424	- Status: pending
425	- Blocker for: [epic if applicable]
426	
427	To extract learnings, invoke:
428	```
429	/curate-pr-learnings [number]
430	```
431	
432	The command will:
433	1. Fetch PR comments (deterministic script)
434	2. AI analyzes and extracts learnings (your job)
435	3. Store validated learnings (deterministic script)
436	
437	Then close the task.
438	````
439	
440	### Why This Matters
441	
442	1. **Security**: Webhook-triggered code execution is an attack surface. CLI/agent invocation is safer.
443	2. **Blocking Task**: The epic can't close until learnings are extracted, ensuring knowledge capture.
444	3. **Agent Autonomy**: An agent can pick up the curation task and process it.
445	4. **Human Oversight**: Human can also run curation manually via the CLI script.
446	
447	### For Epic Completions: Extract Conversation Learnings
448	
449	When this PR completes an **epic** (closes the last blocking task), you MUST also extract learnings from conversation history. Feature work often contains the richest architectural discussions.
450	
451	**Detect epic completion:**
452	
453	> **Note**: This pattern assumes single-epic workflows. If multiple epics are in-progress,
454	> `.[0]` selects the first one, which may not be the epic related to this PR.
455	> For multi-epic projects, correlate the PR's task to its blocking epic manually.
456	
457	```bash
458	# Check if this PR closes an epic (assumes single in-progress epic)
459	# Use your project's task tracking system to check epic status
460	```
461	
462	**If epic is completing:**
463	
464	1. Create a task for conversation extraction
465	2. Report the task to user:
466	
467	   ```text
468	   **Epic Completion Detected**
469	
470	   This PR completes the epic. Created conversation extraction task.
471	
472	   Before closing the epic, extract learnings from conversations.
473	   ```
474	
475	**Why extract from conversations?**
476	
477	- **Strategic insights**: Architectural decisions, trade-offs discussed
478	- **Debugging discoveries**: Root causes found after hours of investigation
479	- **Non-obvious behaviors**: "It turns out that..." moments
480	- **Integration quirks**: API behaviors that caused issues
481	
482	These learnings are often NOT in code review comments - they're in the back-and-forth conversation.
483	
484	### Timeout with Handoff
485	
486	If user chooses to exit at checkpoint:
487	
488	```text
489	**PR #[number] Shepherd Handoff**
490	
491	Status at exit:
492	- CI: [status]
493	- Threads: [X] resolved, [Y] unresolved
494	- Last activity: [timestamp]
495	
496	To resume: `/pr-shepherd [number]`
497	```
