499	## Skills Invoked
500	
501	| Situation           | Skill                                 |
502	| ------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
503	| New review comments | `handling-pr-comments`                |
504	| Code changes needed | `superpowers:test-driven-development` |
505	| Complex debugging   | `superpowers:systematic-debugging`    |
506	
507	## Mandatory Pre-Completion Check
508	
509	**BLOCKING: You MUST run this script and show its output before declaring ANY PR ready:**
510	
511	```bash
512	bin/pr-comments-check.sh <PR_NUMBER>
513	```
514	
515	This script:
516	
517	- Returns exit code 0 if all comments addressed
518	- Returns exit code 1 if ANY unaddressed comments exist
519	- Shows status for each comment
520	
521	**If the script shows ANY unaddressed comments, you are NOT done.** Address each unaddressed comment:
522	
523	For EACH top-level comment (where `in_reply_to_id` is null) without a reply:
524	
525	1. If actionable → Fix it and reply confirming the fix
526	2. If out-of-scope → Reply explaining deferral (create issue if needed)
527	3. If disagree → Reply with reasoning
528	4. **NEVER ignore silently**
529	
530	A PR is NOT ready until every top-level comment has been addressed with a reply.
531	
532	## Verification Checklist
533	
534	Before exiting DONE state:
535	
536	- [ ] All CI checks are green
537	- [ ] All review threads are resolved
538	- [ ] No pending user questions
539	- [ ] Final status reported to user
540	
541	After PR is merged (Phase 7):
542	
543	- [ ] Created task for knowledge curation
544	- [ ] Added task as blocker to epic (if applicable)
545	- [ ] Reported curation task ID to user
546	- [ ] Verified `/self-reflect` ran pre-PR (if not, run it now as fallback)
547	
548	After all post-merge tasks complete:
549	
550	- [ ] Ran `/auto-ram-cleanup` to free development resources
551	- [ ] Confirmed Docker containers still running (if needed)
552	
553	## Common Mistakes
554	
555	### #1 MISTAKE: Returning to MONITORING without checking for NEW comments
556	
557	- After pushing a fix and responding to threads, you MUST run Phase 7
558	- Automated reviewers (CodeRabbit, Cursor) analyze every commit
559	- NEW comments often appear within 1-2 minutes of your push
560	- If you skip Phase 7, you'll miss the new comments and declare complete prematurely
561	
562	**Pushing without local validation**
563	
564	- NEVER push code that hasn't passed `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test --run && pnpm test:coverage`
565	
566	**Auto-fixing complex issues**
567	
568	- If uncertain, ASK. Always go through WAITING_FOR_USER for complex issues.
569	
570	**Forgetting to invoke handling-pr-comments**
571	
572	- When new comments arrive, delegate to that skill. Don't handle comments inline.
573	
574	**Not presenting options to user**
575	
576	- Always give 2-4 options with pros/cons. Never just ask "what should I do?"
577	
578	**Leaving FIXING state early**
579	
580	- Stay in FIXING until local validation passes. Don't assume a fix worked.
581	
582	**Skipping the handling-pr-comments iteration loop**
583	
584	- The skill has Phases 1-7 with an explicit iteration loop
585	- Phase 7 checks for new comments after your fix push
586	- If Phase 7 finds new comments, the skill loops back to Phase 1
587	- DO NOT exit early - let the skill complete its full iteration

63	## Design Review Gate (NEW)
64	
65	For complex features created via brainstorming, an automatic **Design Review Gate** ensures quality before implementation:
66	
67	```
68	Design Document Created
69	        │
70	        ▼
71	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
72	│           DESIGN REVIEW GATE                     │
73	│                                                  │
74	│  Spawns in PARALLEL:                            │
75	│  • Architect Agent (technical architecture)     │
76	│  • Designer Agent (UX/API design)               │
77	│  • UX Reviewer (user flows, integration WUs)    │
78	│  • CTO Agent (TDD readiness)                    │
79	│                                                  │
80	│  ALL must approve to proceed                    │
81	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
82	        │
83	        ├── Any NEEDS_REVISION? → Iterate on design (max 3x)
84	        │
85	       ALL APPROVED
86	        │
87	        ▼
88	   Create BEADS Epic → Begin Implementation
89	```
90	
91	### Triggering the Design Review Gate
92	
93	The gate is automatically triggered when:
94	
95	- `superpowers:brainstorming` completes and commits a design doc
96	- User runs `/review-design <path-to-design.md>`
97	
98	### Review Criteria by Agent
99	
100	| Agent           | Focus Areas                                                |
101	| --------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
102	| Product Manager | Use case clarity, user benefits, scope, success metrics    |
103	| Architect       | Service architecture, dependencies, patterns, integration  |
104	| Designer        | API design, UX flows, developer experience, consistency    |
105	| Security Design | Threat modeling, auth/authz, data protection, OWASP Top 10 |
106	| UX Reviewer     | User flows, text wireframes, integration WUs, empty/error states |
107	| CTO             | TDD readiness, codebase alignment, completeness, risks     |
108	
109	### Iteration Protocol
110	
111	- **Max 3 iterations** before human escalation
112	- Each iteration: revise design → re-run all reviewers
113	- Escalation options: Override / Defer / Cancel
114	
115	See the `design-review-gate` skill for full details.

119	## Team Mode Coordination
120	
121	When multiple Claude Code sessions are active on the same repository (e.g., parallel worktrees), metaswarm automatically enters **Team Mode**. In Team Mode, agents behave as persistent teammates with context retention across sessions and direct inter-agent messaging for coordination. Mode detection is automatic based on the presence of concurrent sessions.
122	
123	For the full Team Mode protocol — including message routing, context sharing, and conflict resolution — see `./guides/agent-coordination.md`.

127	## Plan Review Gate
128	
129	After the Architect creates an implementation plan and before it reaches the Design Review Gate, the plan passes through the **Plan Review Gate**. Three adversarial reviewers validate the plan independently:
130	
131	| Reviewer | Focus |
132	| --- | --- |
133	| **Feasibility** | Technical viability, dependency risks, resource constraints |
134	| **Completeness** | Missing work units, untested edge cases, gaps in Definition of Done |
135	| **Scope & Alignment** | Plan stays within issue scope, aligns with codebase conventions |
136	
137	All 3 must APPROVE before the plan proceeds. See the `plan-review-gate` skill for the full skill definition.
138	
139	---
140	
141	## Orchestrated Execution
142	
143	After design review approval, implementation follows the **4-phase execution loop** per work unit. This replaces the previous linear "implement then review" flow with rigorous independent validation and adversarial review.
144	
145	### Core Principle
146	
147	**Trust nothing. Verify everything. Review adversarially.**
148	
149	### Plan Validation (Pre-Flight)
150	
151	Before submitting to the Design Review Gate, the orchestrator runs a pre-flight checklist covering architecture, dependency graph, API contracts, security, UI/UX, and external dependencies. This catches structural issues (missing service layer, wrong dependency graph, oversized WUs) before spending agent cycles on review.
152	
153	### The 4-Phase Loop
154	
155	For each work unit (a discrete, spec-driven change with DoD items):
156	
157	1. **IMPLEMENT** — Coding subagent executes against the spec using TDD, with the Project Context Document
158	2. **VALIDATE** — Orchestrator independently runs quality gates (tsc, eslint, vitest, coverage enforcement from `.coverage-thresholds.json`). **Never trust subagent self-reports.** Quality gates are **blocking state transitions**, not advisory.
159	3. **ADVERSARIAL REVIEW** — Fresh review subagent checks against spec contract. Binary PASS/FAIL with file:line evidence. Uses `adversarial-review-rubric.md`. When external tools are configured, cross-model review ensures the writer is always reviewed by a different AI model (see External Tools section below).
160	4. **COMMIT** — Only after adversarial PASS. Updates `SERVICE-INVENTORY.md` and Project Context Document.
161	
162	On FAIL: fix → re-validate → spawn **fresh** reviewer (max 3 retries → escalate to human). There is NO path from FAIL to COMMIT without passing through the retry loop.
163	
164	### When to Use Orchestrated Execution
165	
166	- The task has a **written spec** with enumerable Definition of Done items
167	- The implementation involves **multiple work units** (3+ logical changes)
168	- You need **independent verification** — subagent self-reports aren't sufficient
169	- The changes are **high-stakes** (schema changes, security, new architectural patterns)
170	- You want **proactive human checkpoints** at planned review points
171	
172	### When NOT to Use It
173	
174	- Single-file bug fixes or copy changes
175	- Tasks without a written spec or DoD items
176	- Quick prototyping or exploratory work
177	- Tasks where the overhead of decomposition exceeds the work itself
178	
179	For simple tasks, the standard linear flow (implement → code review → PR) works fine.
180	
181	### Key Concepts
182	
183	**Work Unit Decomposition**: Break the implementation plan into discrete work units, each with:
184	- A spec section and enumerated DoD items
185	- A declared file scope (which files it may modify)
186	- Dependencies on other work units
187	- An optional human checkpoint flag
188	
189	**Independent Validation**: The orchestrator runs `tsc`, `eslint`, and `vitest` directly — it does NOT ask the coding subagent "did the tests pass?" and accept the answer.
190	
191	**Adversarial Review**: Fundamentally different from collaborative code review. The reviewer is an independent auditor checking spec compliance, not a helpful colleague suggesting improvements. Binary PASS/FAIL verdict. Evidence required (file:line references).
192	
193	**Fresh Reviewer Rule**: On re-review after FAIL, a NEW reviewer instance is spawned with no memory of the previous review. This prevents anchoring bias.
194	
195	**Human Checkpoints**: Planned pauses at critical boundaries (schema changes, security code, first use of new patterns). The orchestrator waits for explicit human approval before continuing.
196	
197	**Final Comprehensive Review**: After all work units pass, a cross-unit review catches integration issues that per-unit reviews miss.
198	
199	See `orchestrated-execution` skill for the complete pattern, including work unit structure, parallel execution, recovery protocol, and anti-patterns.
200

202	
203	## External AI Tools (Optional)
204	
205	When external AI CLI tools are configured (`.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml`), the orchestrator can delegate implementation and review tasks to OpenAI Codex CLI and Google Gemini CLI. This enables cost savings through cheaper models and cross-model adversarial review that eliminates single-model blind spots.
206	
207	### How It Integrates
208	
209	External tools slot directly into the existing 4-phase execution loop:
210	
211	- **Phase 1 (IMPLEMENT)**: The orchestrator may delegate to an external tool instead of spawning a Claude subagent. The tool works in an isolated git worktree.
212	- **Phase 2 (VALIDATE)**: Unchanged — the orchestrator independently runs all quality gates regardless of who implemented.
213	- **Phase 3 (ADVERSARIAL REVIEW)**: Cross-model review — the writer is always reviewed by a different model (e.g., Codex writes, Gemini + Claude review).
214	- **Phase 4 (COMMIT)**: Unchanged — merge worktree branch after all phases pass.
215	
216	### Escalation Chain
217	
218	The orchestrator adapts based on tool availability:
219	
220	| Available Tools | Escalation Chain | Max Attempts |
221	|---|---|---|
222	| Both Codex + Gemini | A(2) → B(2) → Claude(1) → user | 5 |
223	| One tool only | Tool(2) → Claude(1) → user | 3 |
224	| No tools | Claude → user (existing behavior) | unchanged |
225	
226	Each escalated model receives the previous model's branch as a reference. See the `external-tools` skill for the full skill definition.
227	
228	### Health Check
229	
230	```bash
231	/external-tools-health
232	```
233	
234	Checks installation, authentication, and reachability of all configured adapters.

238	## Visual Review
239	
240	The `visual-review` skill enables agents to take screenshots of web pages, presentations, and UIs using Playwright for visual inspection. This bridges the gap where agents cannot see rendered output.
241	
242	### Usage
243	
244	The skill is triggered when tasks involve visual output (web UIs, Reveal.js presentations, landing pages, email templates). It captures screenshots at configurable viewport sizes, and agents analyze them for layout, typography, colors, spacing, and content issues.
245	
246	### Prerequisites
247	
248	```bash
249	npx playwright install chromium
250	```
251	
252	For remote/headless environments, the skill serves screenshots via HTTP file server so users can view them in their local browser.
253	
254	See the `visual-review` skill for the complete workflow.

376	### GTG (Good-To-Go) Merge Gate
377	
378	GTG is the final merge gate. It consolidates CI status, comment classification, and thread resolution into a single deterministic check. Agents should use it as the primary readiness signal:
379	
380	```bash
381	# Check if PR is ready to merge
382	gtg <PR_NUMBER> --format json \
383	  --exclude-checks "Merge Ready (gtg)" \
384	  --exclude-checks "CodeRabbit" \
385	  --exclude-checks "Cursor Bugbot" \
386	  --exclude-checks "claude"
387	```
388	
389	**Statuses**: `READY` (merge), `ACTION_REQUIRED` (fix comments), `UNRESOLVED_THREADS` (resolve threads), `CI_FAILING` (fix CI). The `action_items` array tells agents exactly what to fix.
390	
391	**GTG reports, agents act**: GTG does not resolve threads or fix code. After addressing feedback, agents must resolve threads themselves via the GraphQL mutation documented in `handle-pr-comments.md` (Section 3: Resolving Review Threads). GTG will report `READY` on the next check once threads are resolved.
392	
393	If the CI check is stale: `gh workflow run gtg.yml -f pr_number=<PR_NUMBER>`
394	
395	### Automatic PR Review Cycles
396	
397	When a PR is created via `bin/create-pr-with-shepherd.sh`, the script outputs instructions to start monitoring:
398	
399	1. **Creates the PR** with proper title/body
400	2. **Start pr-shepherd** using `/pr-shepherd <pr-number>`
401	3. **PR Shepherd monitors**: CI status, review comments, thread resolution
402	4. **Auto-fixes**: Lint, type errors, test failures in your code
403	5. **Reports when ready**: All CI green, all threads resolved
404	
405	For manually-created PRs, invoke `/pr-shepherd <pr-number>` to start the monitoring cycle.
406

409	## BEADS Commands Reference
410	
411	### Issue Management
412	
413	```bash
414	# Create epic for GitHub Issue
415	bd create "Feature: User Auth" --type epic --issue 123
416	
417	# Create task under epic
418	bd create "Research auth patterns" --type task --parent bd-abc123
419	
420	# Add dependency
421	bd dep add <blocked-task> <blocking-task>
422	
423	# Update status
424	bd update <task-id> --status open|in_progress|blocked|closed
425	
426	# Close with reason
427	bd close <task-id> --reason "Completed successfully"
428	```
429	
430	### Task Discovery
431	
432	```bash
433	# Show ready (unblocked) tasks
434	bd ready --json
435	
436	# List all tasks under epic
437	bd list --parent <epic-id>
438	
439	# Show blocked tasks
440	bd blocked
441	
442	# Show task details

511

513	./agents/issue-orchestrator.md
514	
515	Your task:
516	- Epic ID: <epic-id>
517	- GitHub Issue: #123
518	- Begin the orchestration workflow
519	
520	Follow the workflow phases exactly as specified.`,
521	});
522	```

525	
526	## Human Escalation Protocol
527	
528	### When to Escalate
529	
530	1. **Ambiguous Requirements**: Issue lacks clarity
531	2. **Conflicting Constraints**: Can't satisfy all requirements
532	3. **Risk Decision**: Security or data concerns
533	4. **Blocked > 1 Hour**: External dependency needed
534	5. **3 Failed Iterations**: Agent can't resolve issue
535	
536	### How to Escalate
537	
538	```bash
539	# Mark task as waiting
540	bd update <task-id> --status blocked
541	bd label add <task-id> waiting:human
542	
543	# Post to GitHub Issue
544	gh issue comment <number> --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
545	## 🤖 Agent Request: <type>
546	
547	**Task**: <task-id>
548	**Question**: <clear question>
549	
550	### Options
551	1. **Option A**: <description>
552	2. **Option B**: <description>
553	
554	### Agent Recommendation
555	<recommendation>
556	
557	---
558	Reply: `@beads approve <task-id>` or `@beads respond <task-id> <option>`
559	EOF
560	)"
561	```
562	
563	### Human Response Patterns
564	
565	```bash
566	# Approve a blocked task
567	@beads approve bd-abc123
568	
569	# Respond with choice
570	@beads respond bd-abc123 "Use option A"
571	
572	# Request changes
573	@beads request-changes bd-abc123 "Need more error handling"
574	
575	# Defer to later
576	@beads defer bd-abc123 "Discuss in Monday standup"
577	```
578	
579	---
580	
581	## Agent Spawning Patterns
582	
583	### Sequential Spawning
584	
585	```typescript
586	// Spawn Researcher first
587	const researchResult = await Task({
588	  subagent_type: "general-purpose",
589	  description: "Research for issue #123",
590	  prompt: researcherPrompt,
591	});
592	
593	// Then spawn Architect with research output
594	const planResult = await Task({
595	  subagent_type: "general-purpose",
596	  description: "Planning for issue #123",
597	  prompt: architectPrompt + researchResult,
598	});
599	```
600	
601	### Parallel Spawning
602	
603	```typescript
604	// Spawn Code Review and Security Audit in parallel
605	const [reviewResult, securityResult] = await Promise.all([
606	  Task({
607	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
608	    description: "Code review for #123",
609	    prompt: codeReviewPrompt,
610	  }),
611	  Task({
612	    subagent_type: "general-purpose",
613	    description: "Security audit for #123",
614	    prompt: securityAuditPrompt,
615	  }),
616	]);
617	```
618	
619	---
620	
621	## Knowledge Integration
622	
623	### CRITICAL: Prime Before Starting Work
624	
625	**ALL agents MUST prime their context before starting ANY work.** This prevents bad assumptions and ensures alignment with established patterns.
626	
627	```bash
628	# General prime (loads critical rules + gotchas)
629	bd prime
630	
631	# Prime for specific files you'll modify
632	bd prime --files "src/lib/services/*.ts" "src/api/routes/*.ts"
633	
634	# Prime for specific topic
635	bd prime --keywords "authentication" "jwt"
636	
637	# Prime for work type
638	bd prime --work-type planning     # Before planning
639	bd prime --work-type implementation  # Before coding
640	bd prime --work-type review       # Before reviewing
641	bd prime --work-type research     # Before exploring
642	
643	# Combined (most thorough)
644	bd prime --files "<files>" --keywords "<topic>" --work-type <type>
645	```
646	
647	The prime command outputs relevant facts categorized as:
648	
649	- **MUST FOLLOW**: Critical rules (NEVER/ALWAYS/MUST statements)
650	- **GOTCHAS**: Common pitfalls to avoid
651	- **PATTERNS**: Best practices for this codebase
652	- **DECISIONS**: Architectural choices
653	
654	### After Completing Work
655	
656	Run self-reflection to extract learnings:
657	
658	```bash
659	# Fetch recent PR comments (metaswarm-specific GitHub integration)
660	GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) npx tsx scripts/beads-fetch-pr-comments.ts --days 7
661	
662	# Use self-reflect skill to evaluate and add learnings
663	/self-reflect
664	
665	# Compact closed issues (semantic summarization via beads plugin)
666	bd compact
667	```
668	
669	Or spawn Knowledge Curator agent:
670	
671	```typescript
672	Task({
673	  subagent_type: "general-purpose",
674	  description: "Extract learnings from epic",
675	  prompt: `Review completed epic <epic-id> and extract learnings.
676	
677	FIRST: Run \`bd prime --work-type review\` to load context.
678	
679	Then analyze:
680	- What patterns were used?
681	- What gotchas were discovered?
682	- What should future agents know?
683	
684	Use the knowledge capture service to store learnings.`,
685	});
686	```
687	
688	---
689	
690	## Recursive Orchestration
691	
692	For large epics, orchestrators can spawn sub-orchestrators. The pattern:
693	
694	```text
695	Epic (Issue Orchestrator)
696	├── Sub-Epic A (Sub-Orchestrator)
697	│   ├── Task A1
698	│   └── Task A2
699	├── Sub-Epic B (Sub-Orchestrator)
700	│   ├── Task B1
701	│   └── Task B2
702	└── Integration Task (blocked by A + B)
703	```
704	
705	**When to decompose**: If an epic has more than 5-7 tasks or spans multiple domains (e.g., frontend + backend + schema), split into sub-epics.
706	
707	```bash
708	# Create sub-epics under parent epic
709	bd create "Sub-Epic: API layer" --type epic --parent <parent-epic-id>
710	bd create "Sub-Epic: UI components" --type epic --parent <parent-epic-id>
711	
712	# Each sub-epic gets its own orchestrator
713	# The parent orchestrator coordinates completion
714	```
715	
716	Each sub-epic's orchestrator follows the same workflow (research → plan → review → implement → PR) independently. The parent orchestrator monitors progress and coordinates the final integration.
717

720	## Success Criteria Checklist
721	
722	Before closing an epic, verify ALL:
723	
724	- [ ] Plan validation (pre-flight) passed before design review
725	- [ ] External dependencies identified and user prompted for credentials
726	- [ ] Work units decomposed with DoD items and file scopes
727	- [ ] Each work unit passed the 4-phase execution loop
728	- [ ] All quality gates enforced as blocking state transitions (no advisory skips)
729	- [ ] Coverage thresholds met per `.coverage-thresholds.json`
730	- [ ] All adversarial reviews resulted in PASS (with fresh reviewer on re-review)
731	- [ ] `SERVICE-INVENTORY.md` updated with all new services/factories/modules
732	- [ ] Project Context Document maintained and passed to each coder subagent
733	- [ ] Final comprehensive review completed (cross-unit integration)
734	- [ ] All human checkpoints acknowledged
735	- [ ] All BEADS tasks under epic are closed
736	- [ ] PR is created and linked to GitHub Issue
737	- [ ] All CI checks are passing
738	- [ ] All PR comments are addressed
739	- [ ] All PR threads are resolved
740	- [ ] Human has approved merge
741	- [ ] PR is merged to main
742	- [ ] GitHub Issue is closed
743	- [ ] Learnings extracted to knowledge base

1	---
2	name: setup
3	description: Interactive project setup — detects your project, configures metaswarm, writes project-local files
4	---
5	
6	# Setup
7	
8	Interactive, Claude-guided setup for metaswarm. Detects your stack, asks targeted questions, writes project-local files, and creates command shims. Replaces both `npx metaswarm init` and the old `/metaswarm-setup` command.
9	
10	<CRITICAL-REQUIREMENTS>
11	Setup MUST produce these 3 mandatory outputs. A shell script handles them automatically — you MUST run it.
12	
13	After Phase 2 (user questions), determine the correct coverage command from the detection results, then run this Bash command:
14	
15	```bash
16	bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/lib/setup-mandatory-files.sh" "$(pwd)" <threshold> "<coverage-command>"
17	```
18	
19	Where:
20	- `<threshold>` is the user's chosen percentage (e.g., `100`)
21	- `<coverage-command>` is the enforcement command for their test runner:
22	  - pytest → `"pytest --cov --cov-fail-under=<threshold>"`
23	  - vitest/pnpm → `"pnpm vitest run --coverage"`
24	  - jest/npm → `"npx jest --coverage"`
25	  - go → `"go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./..."`
26	  - cargo → `"cargo tarpaulin --fail-under <threshold>"`
27	
28	The script handles:
29	1. **CLAUDE.md** — appends metaswarm section (or writes new), skips if already present
30	2. **`.coverage-thresholds.json`** — writes at project root with correct thresholds and command
31	3. **6 shims in `.claude/commands/`** — writes `start-task.md`, `prime.md`, `review-design.md`, `self-reflect.md`, `pr-shepherd.md`, `brainstorm.md`
32	
33	The script outputs JSON with what was created/skipped/errored. Check that `"status": "ok"`.
34	
35	**If the script is not available or fails**, fall back to writing these files manually with the Write tool. Do NOT skip them.
36	</CRITICAL-REQUIREMENTS>
37	
38	## Pre-Flight
39	
40	### Existing Profile Check
41	
42	Use Glob to check if `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` exists.
43	
44	- **If it exists**: Read it, present the current configuration summary, and ask the user via AskUserQuestion: "You already have a metaswarm project profile. Re-run setup (overwrites choices) or skip?" Options: "Re-run setup" / "Skip". If the user skips, stop with: "Setup skipped. Existing configuration unchanged."
45	- **If it does not exist**: Continue to Project Detection.
46

48	
49	## Phase 1: Project Detection
50	
51	Scan the project directory silently using Glob and Read. Do NOT ask the user for any of this information. Detect everything, then present results.
52	
53	### 1.1 Language
54	
55	Check for marker files at the project root:
56	
57	| Marker File | Language |
58	|---|---|
59	| `package.json` | Node.js / JavaScript |
60	| `tsconfig.json` | TypeScript (refines Node.js to TypeScript) |
61	| `pyproject.toml` OR `setup.py` OR `requirements.txt` | Python |
62	| `go.mod` | Go |
63	| `Cargo.toml` | Rust |
64	| `pom.xml` OR `build.gradle` OR `build.gradle.kts` | Java |
65	| `Gemfile` | Ruby |
66	| `Makefile` (alone, no other markers) | Unknown (ask user) |
67	
68	If `tsconfig.json` exists alongside `package.json`, the language is "TypeScript".
69	
70	If multiple languages are detected, note all of them but use the primary one (most infrastructure) for command generation.
71	
72	**If no language detected**: Use AskUserQuestion to ask the user what language/stack they are using before proceeding.
73	
74	### 1.2 Framework
75	
76	**Node.js/TypeScript** — Read `package.json` and check `dependencies` + `devDependencies`:
77	
78	| Dependency | Framework |
79	|---|---|
80	| `next` | Next.js |
81	| `nuxt` OR `nuxt3` | Nuxt |
82	| `@angular/core` | Angular |
83	| `svelte` OR `@sveltejs/kit` | SvelteKit |
84	| `react` (without next/nuxt) | React |
85	| `vue` (without nuxt) | Vue |
86	| `express` | Express |
87	| `fastify` | Fastify |
88	| `hono` | Hono |
89	| `@nestjs/core` | NestJS |
90	
91	**Python** — Check `pyproject.toml` or `requirements.txt` for: `fastapi`, `django`, `flask`.
92	
93	**Go** — Read `go.mod` for: `github.com/gin-gonic/gin` (Gin), `github.com/labstack/echo` (Echo), `github.com/gofiber/fiber` (Fiber).
94	
95	If no framework detected, set to `null`.
96	
97	### 1.3 Package Manager (Node.js only)
98	
99	| Lock File | Package Manager |
100	|---|---|
101	| `pnpm-lock.yaml` | pnpm |
102	| `yarn.lock` | yarn |
103	| `bun.lockb` | bun |
104	| `package-lock.json` | npm |
105	
106	Default to `npm` if no lock file found. For non-Node.js, set to `null`.

111	1. Glob for `vitest.config.*` or `vitest` in devDependencies -> `vitest`
112	2. Glob for `jest.config.*` or `jest` in devDependencies -> `jest`
113	3. `mocha` in devDependencies -> `mocha`

115	**Python**: Check for `[tool.pytest]` in `pyproject.toml` or `pytest` in dependencies -> `pytest` (default for Python).
116	
117	**Go**: `go test` (built-in). **Rust**: `cargo test` (built-in). **Java/Maven**: `mvn test`. **Java/Gradle**: `gradle test`.

124	| `biome.json` OR `@biomejs/biome` in devDependencies | biome |

202	4. **CI pipeline** — Ask only if NO CI detected: "Create a GitHub Actions CI pipeline?" Options: "Yes (Recommended)" / "No"

208	## Phase 3: Write Required Files
209	
210	### Step 1: Run the mandatory files script
211	
212	This is the FIRST thing to do after Phase 2. Determine the coverage command, then run:
213	
214	```bash
215	bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/lib/setup-mandatory-files.sh" "$(pwd)" <threshold> "<coverage-command>"
216	```
217	
218	Example for Python with pytest at 100%:
219	```bash
220	bash "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/lib/setup-mandatory-files.sh" "$(pwd)" 100 "pytest --cov --cov-fail-under=100"
221	```
222	
223	Check the JSON output. If `"status": "ok"`, the 3 mandatory files are done. Report to the user what was created.
224	
225	If the script fails or is not found, write the files manually (see CRITICAL-REQUIREMENTS above for what they are).
226	
227	### Step 2: Customize CLAUDE.md TODO sections
228	
229	If CLAUDE.md was newly written (not appended), use Edit to replace the TODO placeholders:
230	- Replace `npm test` / `npm run test:coverage` with the detected test/coverage commands
231	- Replace `TypeScript strict mode` / `ESLint + Prettier` with the detected language tools
232	- Remove the `<!-- TODO: ... -->` comment lines
233	
234	If CLAUDE.md was appended to (existing file), this step is not needed.
235	
236	---
237	
238	### Step 3: Additional files
239	
240	#### Knowledge Base
241	
242	Read each file from `./knowledge/`:
243	- `patterns.jsonl`, `gotchas.jsonl`, `decisions.jsonl`, `api-behaviors.jsonl`, `codebase-facts.jsonl`, `anti-patterns.jsonl`, `facts.jsonl`
244	
245	Write them to `.beads/knowledge/` in the project. Skip any that already exist.
246	
247	#### Shell Utilities
248	
249	Read each file from `./bin/`:
250	- `estimate-cost.sh`, `external-tools-verify.sh`, `pr-comments-check.sh`, `pr-comments-filter.sh`
251	
252	Write them to `bin/` in the project. Make executable with `chmod +x`. Skip any that already exist.
253	
254	#### TypeScript Scripts
255	
256	Read each file from `./scripts/`:
257	- `beads-fetch-pr-comments.ts`, `beads-fetch-conversation-history.ts`
258	
259	Write them to `scripts/` in the project. Skip any that already exist.
260	
261	**Note**: The former `beads-self-reflect.ts` script is no longer bundled — the standalone beads plugin (v0.63.3+) provides `bd compact` for semantic summarization natively.
262	
263	**Node.js dependency warning**: If Node.js was NOT detected as the project language, print:
264	> "Note: scripts/*.ts require Node.js (npx tsx) to run. Some advanced features (PR comment fetching, conversation history) will work once Node.js is available. Core metaswarm functionality does not require Node.js."
265	
266	#### Conditional Files
267	
268	| Condition | Source | Destination |
269	|---|---|---|
270	| User chose YES for CI | `./templates/ci.yml` | `.github/workflows/ci.yml` |
271	| User chose YES for git hooks AND Husky detected or Node.js project | `./templates/pre-push` | `.husky/pre-push` (chmod +x) |
272	| User chose YES for external tools | `./templates/external-tools.yaml` | `.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml` |
273	| Always | `./templates/.env.example` | `.env.example` |
274	| Always | `./templates/SERVICE-INVENTORY.md` | `SERVICE-INVENTORY.md` |
275	| Always | `./templates/gitignore` | Merge into existing `.gitignore` (append missing entries, never duplicate) |
276	
277	For `.gitignore`, read the existing file (if any), then append language-specific entries that are not already present. Always ensure `.env`, `.DS_Store`, and `*.log` are included.
278	
279	---
280	
281	## Phase 4: Profile Creation
282	
283	Write `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` with all detection results and user choices:
284	
285	```json
286	{
287	  "metaswarm_version": "1.0.0",
288	  "distribution": "plugin",
289	  "installed_at": "{current ISO 8601 timestamp}",
290	  "updated_at": "{current ISO 8601 timestamp}",
291	  "detection": {
292	    "language": "{detected language}",
293	    "framework": "{detected framework or null}",
294	    "test_runner": "{detected test runner}",
295	    "linter": "{detected linter or null}",
296	    "formatter": "{detected formatter or null}",
297	    "package_manager": "{detected package manager or null}",
298	    "type_checker": "{detected type checker or null}",
299	    "ci": "{detected CI system or null}",
300	    "git_hooks": "{detected hook system or null}"
301	  },
302	  "choices": {
303	    "coverage_threshold": 100,
304	    "external_tools": false,
305	    "visual_review": false,
306	    "ci_pipeline": false,
307	    "git_hooks": false
308	  },
309	  "commands": {
310	    "test": "{resolved test command}",
311	    "coverage": "{resolved coverage command}",
312	    "lint": "{resolved lint command or null}",
313	    "typecheck": "{resolved typecheck command or null}",
314	    "format_check": "{resolved format check command or null}"
315	  }
316	}
317	```
318	
319	Fill all values from detection and user answers. Use `null` for anything not detected.
320	
321	Command resolution reference:
322	
323	| Test Runner | Pkg Mgr | Test Command | Coverage Command |
324	|---|---|---|---|
325	| vitest | pnpm | `pnpm vitest run` | `pnpm vitest run --coverage` |
326	| vitest | npm | `npx vitest run` | `npx vitest run --coverage` |
327	| vitest | yarn | `yarn vitest run` | `yarn vitest run --coverage` |
328	| jest | pnpm | `pnpm jest` | `pnpm jest --coverage` |
329	| jest | npm | `npx jest` | `npx jest --coverage` |
330	| jest | yarn | `yarn jest` | `yarn jest --coverage` |
331	| mocha | any | `npx mocha` | `npx nyc mocha` |
332	| pytest | -- | `pytest` | `pytest --cov --cov-fail-under={threshold}` |
333	| go test | -- | `go test ./...` | `go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...` |
334	| cargo test | -- | `cargo test` | `cargo tarpaulin --fail-under {threshold}` |
335	| mvn test | -- | `mvn test` | `mvn test jacoco:report` |
336	| gradle test | -- | `gradle test` | `gradle test jacocoTestReport` |
337	
338	---
339	
340	## Phase 5: Post-Setup Actions
341	
342	### 5.1 External Tools (if enabled)
343	
344	1. Check if Codex and Gemini CLIs are installed via Bash (`command -v codex`, `command -v gemini`)
345	2. For tools not installed, tell the user how to install them
346	3. For installed tools, verify with `--version`
347	4. Update `.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml` — set `enabled: true` for installed tools, `enabled: false` for missing ones
348	
349	### 5.2 Visual Review (if enabled)
350	
351	1. Run `npx playwright install chromium` via Bash
352	2. Report success or failure
353	
354	### 5.3 Git Hooks (if enabled)
355	
356	**Node.js/TypeScript**: Install Husky if not present, run `npx husky init`, write pre-push hook.
357	**Python**: Suggest `pip install pre-commit` and offer to create `.pre-commit-config.yaml`.
358	**Other**: Suggest appropriate hook tools for the ecosystem.
359	
360	---
361	
362	## Phase 6: Summary
363	
364	Present a final summary:
365	
366	```
367	Setup complete! Here's what was configured:
368	
369	  Project:         {name}
370	  Language:        {language}
371	  Framework:       {framework or "None"}
372	  Test runner:     {test_runner} -> `{test command}`
373	  Coverage:        {threshold}% -> `{coverage command}`
374	  Linter:          {linter or "None"}
375	  Formatter:       {formatter or "None"}
376	  CI:              {ci or "None"}
377	  Git hooks:       {hooks or "None"}
378	  External tools:  {Enabled/Disabled}
379	  Visual review:   {Enabled/Disabled}
380	
381	Mandatory files:
382	  ✔ CLAUDE.md          — {written new / appended metaswarm section / already had it}
383	  ✔ .coverage-thresholds.json — {threshold}% coverage, enforcement: `{command}`
384	  ✔ .claude/commands/   — 6 shims: start-task, prime, review-design, self-reflect, pr-shepherd, brainstorm
385	
386	Other files written:
387	  {list every other file written or modified with its path}
388	
389	You're all set! Run /start-task to begin working.
390	```
391	
392	**Command naming**: When recommending commands to the user, always use the short shim names (`/start-task`, `/prime`, `/brainstorm`, etc.), NOT the namespaced plugin names (`/metaswarm:start-task`). The shims you just created in `.claude/commands/` make the short names work. The short names are easier to type and remember.
393	
394	Offer 1-2 relevant tips based on configuration:
395	- If external tools enabled: "Use `/external-tools-health` to check tool status."
396	- If no CI set up: "Consider adding CI later -- metaswarm includes a template at `./templates/ci.yml`."
397	- If visual review enabled: "The visual review skill will screenshot your app during development."
398	
399	---
400	
401	## Missing Setup Auto-Detection
402	
403	If `/start-task` is invoked and `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` does not exist, the start skill should auto-route here. This skill will run the full setup flow, then hand back to `/start-task` to continue with the user's original request.
404	
405	---
406	
407	## Error Handling
408	
409	- If any Bash command fails, report the error and offer to skip that step or retry.
410	- If a file cannot be read, note it and continue with other detection.
411	- If AskUserQuestion is dismissed, use defaults: 100% coverage, no external tools, no visual review.
412	- Never leave the project half-configured. The pre-flight check allows re-running setup to completion.
413	- All template paths are hardcoded in this skill. Never construct file paths from user-provided input.
414	
415	---
416	
417	## Final Verification (run this before declaring setup complete)
418	
419	Before saying "setup complete", run this Bash command to verify the 3 mandatory files:
420	
421	```bash
422	echo "CLAUDE.md:"; grep -c "metaswarm" CLAUDE.md 2>/dev/null || echo "MISSING"; echo "coverage:"; ls .coverage-thresholds.json 2>/dev/null || echo "MISSING"; echo "shims:"; ls .claude/commands/start-task.md .claude/commands/prime.md .claude/commands/brainstorm.md 2>/dev/null || echo "MISSING"
423	```
424	
425	If any output says "MISSING", go back and run the setup-mandatory-files.sh script or create the files manually. Do NOT declare success with missing files.
426	
427	When reporting available commands to the user, use the **short names** (`/start-task`, `/prime`, `/brainstorm`, etc.) — NOT the namespaced names (`/metaswarm:start-task`). The command shims make the short names work. Do NOT recommend commands that don't exist (e.g., `/metaswarm:start`, `/metaswarm:status`, `/metaswarm:architect-agent`).
428

1	---
2	name: status
3	description: Diagnostic status report — shows metaswarm installation state, project setup, and potential issues
4	---
5	
6	# Status Skill
7	
8	Generate a diagnostic report of the metaswarm installation, project configuration, and potential issues. Useful for troubleshooting and verifying setup or migration.
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Checks
13	
14	Run each check below and present results in a single formatted report.
15	
16	### 1. Plugin Version
17	
18	- Read `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` from the plugin root -- report the `version` field
19	- Fallback: read `package.json` at plugin root for `version`
20	- If neither found: `Plugin version: UNKNOWN`
21	
22	### 2. Project Setup State
23	
24	- Check if `.metaswarm/project-profile.json` exists in the working directory
25	- If present, report key fields: `distribution`, `metaswarm_version`, `language`, `framework`, `test_runner`
26	- If absent: `Project setup: NOT CONFIGURED -- run /metaswarm:setup`
27	
28	### 3. Command Shims
29	
30	Check these 6 files in `.claude/commands/`:
31	
32	| Shim | Expected |
33	|---|---|
34	| `start-task.md` | Routes to `/metaswarm:start-task` |
35	| `prime.md` | Routes to `/metaswarm:prime` |
36	| `review-design.md` | Routes to `/metaswarm:review-design` |
37	| `self-reflect.md` | Routes to `/metaswarm:self-reflect` |
38	| `pr-shepherd.md` | Routes to `/metaswarm:pr-shepherd` |
39	| `brainstorm.md` | Routes to `/metaswarm:brainstorm` |
40	
41	For each: report Present/Missing. If the file exists but does not contain "metaswarm" routing, flag as `present (non-metaswarm content)`.
42	
43	### 4. Legacy Embedded Plugin
44	
45	- Check for `.claude/plugins/metaswarm/.claude-plugin/plugin.json`
46	- If found: `DETECTED -- run /metaswarm:migrate`
47	- If found alongside the marketplace plugin, flag prominently as a conflict
48	
49	### 5. BEADS Plugin
50	
51	- Scan `~/.claude/plugins/cache/` for a directory containing `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` with `"name": "beads"`
52	- If found: `installed (standalone)` -- metaswarm defers priming to BEADS
53	- If not found: `not separately installed`
54	
55	### 6. `bd` CLI
56	
57	```bash
58	command -v bd && bd --version 2>/dev/null
59	```
60	
61	- If found: report path and version
62	- If not found: `not installed -- knowledge priming and self-reflect require bd. Core orchestration works without it.`
63	
64	### 7. External Tools
65	
66	- Read `.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml` -- if absent: `not configured (optional)`
67	- If present, check each enabled adapter's availability:
68	
69	```bash
70	command -v codex    # Codex CLI
71	command -v gemini   # Gemini CLI
72	```
73	
74	Report per-tool: enabled (yes/no), status (available/not installed).
75	
76	### 8. Coverage Thresholds
77	
78	- Read `.coverage-thresholds.json` -- if absent: `not configured`
79	- If present, report threshold values (lines, branches, functions, statements) and enforcement command
80	
81	### 9. Node.js
82	
83	```bash
84	node --version 2>/dev/null
85	```
86	
87	- If found: report version
88	- If not found: `not installed -- scripts/beads-*.ts require Node.js. Core orchestration works without it.`
89	
90	---
91	
92	## Output Format
93	
94	```markdown
95	## Metaswarm Status Report
96	
97	| Component | Status |
98	|---|---|
99	| Plugin version | 1.0.0 |
100	| Project setup | Configured (distribution: plugin) |
101	| Command shims | 6/6 present |
102	| Legacy embedded plugin | Not detected |
103	| BEADS plugin | Not separately installed |
104	| bd CLI | Available (v0.5.2) |
105	| External tools | Codex: available, Gemini: not installed |
106	| Coverage thresholds | 100% (all categories) |
107	| Node.js | Available (v22.4.0) |
108	
109	### Issues Found
110	- None
111	
112	### Recommendations
113	- None
114	```
115	
116	When issues are found:
117	
118	```markdown
119	### Issues Found
120	1. Legacy embedded plugin detected alongside marketplace plugin -- run `/metaswarm:migrate`
121	2. Command shim `start-task.md` missing -- run `/metaswarm:setup`
122	
123	### Recommendations
124	1. Install `bd` CLI for knowledge priming and self-reflect
125	2. Configure external tools for cross-model review (`.metaswarm/external-tools.yaml`)
126	```
127	
128	---
129	
130	## Error Handling
131	
132	This skill is diagnostic-only and never fails fatally. If any individual check errors, report the failure for that check (e.g., `Plugin version: ERROR -- could not read plugin.json`) and continue with remaining checks.
133

1	---
2	name: visual-review
3	description: Take screenshots of web pages and UI using Playwright for visual review and iteration
4	auto_activate: false
5	triggers:
6	  - "visual review"
7	  - "screenshot"
8	  - "take a screenshot"
9	  - "review visually"
10	  - "check how it looks"
11	  - "review the UI"
12	  - "review the slides"
13	---
14	
15	# Visual Review
16	
17	## Purpose
18	
19	Agents cannot see rendered web pages, presentations, or UI. This skill bridges that gap by using Playwright to capture screenshots of web pages (local files, localhost servers, or deployed URLs) so agents can visually inspect their work, identify layout issues, and iterate.
20	
21	This is especially useful for:
22	- **Reveal.js presentations** — capture each slide individually
23	- **Web UIs** — verify layout, styling, and responsiveness
24	- **Landing pages** — check visual design matches intent
25	- **Email templates** — verify rendering
26	
27	## Prerequisites
28	
29	This skill requires **Playwright** to be available. Before first use, check and install if needed:
30	
31	```bash
32	# Check if Playwright is available
33	npx playwright --version 2>/dev/null
34	
35	# If not available, install it (one-time setup)
36	npx playwright install chromium
37	```
38	
39	**Important:** Only the `chromium` browser is needed. Do not install all browsers — it wastes disk space and time.
40	
41	If `npx playwright` fails entirely, the user may need to install it:
42	```bash
43	npm install -g playwright
44	npx playwright install chromium
45	```
46	
47	## Workflow
48	
49	### Phase 1: Setup
50	
51	1. **Verify Playwright is available** — run `npx playwright --version`
52	2. **If not installed** — run `npx playwright install chromium` and inform the user
53	3. **Create output directory** — `mkdir -p /tmp/visual-review`
54	4. **Determine the target** — local file path, localhost URL, or deployed URL
55	
56	### Phase 2: Capture Screenshots
57	
58	#### For a single page:
59	
60	```bash
61	npx playwright screenshot \
62	  --browser chromium \
63	  --viewport-size "1456,816" \
64	  --wait-for-timeout 3000 \
65	  "<URL_OR_FILE_PATH>" \
66	  /tmp/visual-review/page.png
67	```
68	
69	**Parameters:**
70	- `--viewport-size "1456,816"` — 16:9 aspect ratio, good for presentations
71	- `--wait-for-timeout 3000` — wait 3 seconds for fonts, images, and animations to settle
72	- Use `file:///absolute/path/to/file.html` for local files
73	- Use full URLs for deployed sites
74	
75	#### For Reveal.js presentations (multiple slides):
76	
77	Capture each slide by appending the slide hash to the URL:
78	
79	```bash
80	# Capture all slides (adjust count as needed)
81	for i in $(seq 0 15); do
82	  npx playwright screenshot \
83	    --browser chromium \
84	    --viewport-size "1456,816" \
85	    --wait-for-timeout 2000 \
86	    "<BASE_URL>#/$i" \
87	    "/tmp/visual-review/slide-$i.png"
88	done
89	```
90	
91	**Tip:** To capture slides with all fragments visible, add `?fragments=true` or use JavaScript injection via a Playwright script. The simpler approach is to accept that fragment-heavy slides will appear in their initial (pre-click) state.
92	
93	#### For responsive testing:
94	
95	```bash
96	# Mobile
97	npx playwright screenshot --viewport-size "390,844" "<URL>" /tmp/visual-review/mobile.png
98	
99	# Tablet
100	npx playwright screenshot --viewport-size "768,1024" "<URL>" /tmp/visual-review/tablet.png
101	
102	# Desktop wide
103	npx playwright screenshot --viewport-size "1920,1080" "<URL>" /tmp/visual-review/desktop.png
104	```
105	
106	### Phase 3: Show and Review Screenshots
107	
108	Use the Read tool to analyze each screenshot:
109	
110	```
111	Read /tmp/visual-review/slide-0.png
112	Read /tmp/visual-review/slide-1.png
113	...
114	```
115	
116	**Showing screenshots to the user:** The agent can see screenshots via Read, but the user cannot. When the user wants to see what you see — or when you want to discuss a visual issue together — there are two approaches depending on the environment:
117	
118	**Local machine (has a display):**
119	
120	```bash
121	# Open a specific screenshot
122	open /tmp/visual-review/slide-2.png
123	
124	# Open all screenshots at once
125	open /tmp/visual-review/slide-*.png
126	```
127	
128	**Remote/headless machine (SSH, tmux, etc.):**
129	
130	Serve the screenshots directory over HTTP so the user can view them in their local browser:
131	
132	```bash
133	# Start a simple file server (runs in background)
134	# Use a high port (>10000) to avoid conflicts with dev tools
135	python3 -m http.server 18080 --directory /tmp/visual-review &
136	
137	# User can now browse to:
138	#   http://<hostname>:18080/
139	#   http://<hostname>:18080/slide-2.png
140	#   etc.
141	```
142	
143	Pick any open high-numbered port (18080, 19090, etc.) — low ports like 8080 are often taken by dev tools. This serves the entire `/tmp/visual-review/` directory with an auto-generated file listing. The user can click through all screenshots in their browser. Stop the server when done:
144	
145	```bash
146	# Stop the background server
147	kill %1
148	# Or find and kill by port
149	lsof -ti:18080 | xargs kill
150	```
151	
152	Use whichever approach fits the environment. This is useful when collaborating on visual issues, when the user asks what something looks like, or when you want confirmation on a subjective design choice.
153	
154	For each screenshot, evaluate:
155	- **Layout** — Is content centered/aligned as intended?
156	- **Typography** — Are headings, body text, and code readable?
157	- **Colors** — Do accent colors, backgrounds, and contrasts look right?
158	- **Spacing** — Is there enough whitespace? Any cramped areas?
159	- **Content** — Is all expected content visible? (Note: fragment content may be hidden)
160	- **Responsiveness** — Does it work at the target viewport size?
161	
162	### Phase 4: Document Findings
163	
164	Create a findings summary:
165	
166	```markdown
167	## Visual Review — [Project Name]
168	
169	### Reviewed: [date]
170	
171	| Slide/Page | Status | Notes |
172	|-----------|--------|-------|
173	| slide-0   | Good   | Title centered, accent color correct |
174	| slide-1   | Issue  | Text overflows right edge on mobile |
175	| ...       | ...    | ... |
176	
177	### Issues to Fix
178	1. [Description of issue + which slide/page]
179	2. [...]
180	
181	### Looks Good
182	- [Things that are working well]
183	```
184	
185	### Phase 5: Fix and Re-capture
186	
187	After making fixes:
188	1. Re-capture only the affected slides/pages
189	2. Review the new screenshots
190	3. Repeat until satisfied
191	
192	## Reveal.js-Specific Tips
193	
194	- **Slide numbering**: Reveal.js uses `#/0` for the first slide, `#/1` for the second, etc.
195	- **Vertical slides**: Use `#/horizontal/vertical` format (e.g., `#/2/1`)
196	- **Fragments**: Content with `class="fragment"` is hidden until clicked. Screenshots show the initial state. This is expected behavior, not a bug.
197	- **Speaker notes**: Not visible in screenshots (they appear in a separate window with `S` key)
198	- **Theme loading**: Use `--wait-for-timeout 3000` to ensure CDN-loaded themes and fonts are ready
199	
200	## Common Mistakes
201	
202	| Mistake | Why It's Wrong | Do This Instead |
203	|---------|---------------|-----------------|
204	| Not waiting for page load | Fonts/images may not render | Use `--wait-for-timeout 3000` |
205	| Using `http://` for local files | File URLs need `file:///` protocol | Use `file:///absolute/path` |
206	| Installing all Playwright browsers | Wastes time and disk | Only install `chromium` |
207	| Treating fragment-hidden content as a bug | Fragments are designed to reveal on click | Note it as expected |
208	| Forgetting to create output directory | Screenshots fail silently | Always `mkdir -p /tmp/visual-review` first |
209	| Taking too many screenshots at once | Floods context window | Review in batches of 4-6 |
210	| Not offering to `open` when discussing visuals | User can't see what you're describing | Use `open` when collaborating on visual issues |
211	
212	## Integration Points
213	
214	**Upstream:** Any skill that produces visual output (presentations, web UIs, email templates)
215	
216	**Downstream:** Fixes feed back into the source files, then re-capture to verify
217	
218	## Verification Checklist
219	
220	- [ ] Playwright is installed and working
221	- [ ] Screenshots captured at correct viewport size
222	- [ ] All pages/slides reviewed
223	- [ ] Issues documented with specific descriptions
224	- [ ] Fixes verified with re-captured screenshots
225
